name: ask-strategic-not-interpretive
description: You are critiquing, teaching, workshopping, or revising a story and the questions on the table are about meaning, theme, or symbolism. You risk producing a beautiful interpretation that tells you nothing about how the story works or how to fix a draft.

sources.chapter: We Begin

sections_html.when: You are in a workshop, a reading group, a teaching session, or a revision pass on your own draft. The conversation has drifted to what something means: what a scene symbolizes, what a character represents, what theme the story is exploring. The talk feels deep but is not changing what anyone would do to the page. Reach for this whenever the discussion is interpretive and a writer in the room needs to act on it.
sections_html.move:
1. Notice when a question or comment is interpretive: it asks what something means, what it stands for, what theme it serves.
  2. Re-pose the same observation as a strategic question. Replace "what does X mean" with "what is X doing for the reader" or "why do we need X." Replace "what does X symbolize" with "what would change if X were cut."
  3. Direct the strategic question at a specific element on the page: a scene, a return, a repetition, a digression, a beat. Name the element by location, not by abstraction.
  4. Answer the strategic question in terms of effect on the reader as the story unfolds in time: what the element sets up, pays off, delays, escalates, or earns.
  5. If the strategic question has no good answer, that element is a candidate for cutting or rewriting. Mark it.
sections_html.signal: Each question on the table now points at a specific element of the text and asks what that element is doing for the reader. The discussion produces at least one concrete decision you could apply to a draft (cut this, expand this, move this, replace this), not just a richer interpretation.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, write down the current question being discussed. Ask: "If I had a perfect answer to this question, would I know what to change in the manuscript?"

After applying, ask the same question of your re-posed question. The skill landed if the answer flips from "no, I would just understand the story better" to "yes, I would know whether to keep, cut, or rework a specific passage."
sections_html.failure: Without this move, critique converges on interpretation: the room produces ever more sophisticated readings of what the story means while the writer leaves with nothing to do on Monday. Symbolism talk is especially seductive because it sounds like analysis, but it does not tell you whether the second return to the village is earning its place, whether a digression is paying off, or which sentence is doing the work of the ending. The story gets understood and stays unrevised.
sections_html.example: Saunders contrasts two ways of teaching the same Russian story. The academic version says something like: "Resurrection, in this context, is a metaphor for political revolution, an ongoing concern in the Russian zeitgeist." The strategic version asks: "Why do we even need that second return to the village?" The first answer enriches reading. The second one is the question a writer or reviser can act on, because answering it forces you to articulate what the second return is structurally accomplishing, and whether it is.
sections_html.source: Chapter "We Begin," lines 188-278 (especially 241). Saunders names his approach as "more strategic" than "academic" and gives the contrasting question pair as illustration of what that shift looks like in practice.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends the-basic-drill. Once the basic drill has produced a list of located reactions, re-pose any interpretive question those reactions raise as a strategic one ("what is this element doing for the reader") so the conversation can produce decisions a writer could act on.
  • See also convert-moral-critique-to-technical, which is the same shape applied to moral accusations: convert "this is X-ist" into a technical observation about narrative resources at parallel moments.

name: belief-by-character-attribution
description: You are drafting a story where a sincerely held authorial belief wants in. The belief is real, articulate, and could fill a strong essay. You can feel that delivering it as authorial commentary would read as a lecture, but no other shape is visible. Or revising and a character has just blurted out a position the writer used to hold but no longer does, and the writer is unsure what to do with it.

sources.chapter: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'
sources.note: Saunders names the procedure on Chekhov's handling of Ivan's anti-happiness speech. The speech is 'the stuff of an excellent essay: articulate, earnest, precisely expressed, supported with examples, infused with sincere intent. That's why we believe it and why we're moved by it.' But Chekhov 'makes double use of the speech by attributing it to Ivan. When Ivan, speaking through Chekhov, diverges from Chekhov... Chekhov lets this be (It's not me, it's him) and allows the story to react to that new Ivan.' The result: 'Chekhov has it both ways: he gets the power of his heartfelt opinion (the truth of which we feel), destabilized by its attribution to Ivan (whose flaws we note).' The destabilizing detail is the smelly-pipe reveal at the close (lines 6075-6079): a small concrete late detail (Ivan, who has just preached doing-good, has thoughtlessly let his pipe stink up the room and keep his friend awake) that does not refute the speech but makes it read differently on rereading. Saunders walks the back-scan: 'I find myself scanning back through the story, looking for: Happiness, Things About'; the swimming scene resurfaces as countervailing evidence; 'the two readings coexist, making a truth bigger than either would have alone.' The drafting-side procedure (lines 6337-6353): 'If I'm writing in a character's voice and he or she suddenly blurts something out, is that me? Well, sort of. That blurt came out of me, after all. But is it really me? Do I believe it? Well, who cares? There it is. Is it good? Any power in it? If so, it would be crazy not to use it. That's how characters get made: we export fragments of ourselves, then give those fragments pants and a hairstyle and a hometown and all of that. Having made a character in this way, we can take a step back, look a little askance at him. Any consequences to believing that way? Any suspicious overtones in what he just said? Any collateral damage anticipated? Latent unforeseen consequences? (Any smelly pipes?)' The 'Victory Lap' worked instance: Saunders writes a sixteen-year-old's blurt — 'To do good, you just have to decide to do good. You have to be brave. You have to stand up for what's right.' — in a voice he held at sixteen but no longer holds at fifty-one; the story responds 'Oh, really?' and the plot is forced to challenge her belief. The orientation (lines 6363-6369): 'Any idea we express is just one of many we have within us... Mostly we walk around identifying with one set of opinions and assessing the world from that position. Our inner orchestra has been instructed that certain instruments are to dominate, others to play softly or not at all. Writing, we get a chance to change the mix.'

sections_html.when: You have a belief, a position, an articulate piece of moral or political thinking that wants to be in your story. You can write it well as a paragraph of essay. The story currently does not have a place for that paragraph that does not feel like a lecture pasted in. Or you are revising and a character, mid-scene, has just produced a blurt of conviction the writer used to hold but no longer does, and the impulse is to either rewrite the line into something the writer still endorses or cut it. Reach for this whenever the question "is this me speaking" is interfering with the work, and whenever a story you are writing is at risk of becoming a sermon.
sections_html.move:
1. Render the belief in full conviction. Do not water it down to keep it deniable. Saunders's note on Ivan's speech: "articulate, earnest, precisely expressed, supported with examples, infused with sincere intent." The truth-content of the position is what gives the move its power; if you hedge the speech, the destabilization that follows will have nothing to destabilize.
  2. Attribute the belief to a character. The character is not yourself, even if the belief is. Saunders's frame: "we export fragments of ourselves, then give those fragments pants and a hairstyle and a hometown and all of that." The character can be a former self (Saunders writes a sixteen-year-old in "Victory Lap" voicing what he believed at sixteen and no longer believes at fifty-one), a latent voice (an opinion the writer can imitate but does not endorse), or a fragment of self that competes with other fragments. The constraint is only that the character be rendered as a particular person, not as a mouthpiece.
  3. Refuse ownership. Once the speech is on the page, take a step back. Saunders's framing: "It's not me, it's him." This is not disclaiming responsibility; it is refusing to use the story as the platform for an authorial position. The writer remains responsible for everything in the story, but the story is no longer required to endorse the speech.
  4. Have the story react to the speaker. Ask three diagnostic questions of the character who just delivered the speech. (a) Are there consequences to believing this way that the speaker has not noticed? (b) Are there suspicious overtones in what the character just said — places the conviction got cranky, leapt over a counterargument, addressed an imaginary critic, or revealed personal frustration? (c) Are there latent unforeseen consequences — actions the character will take in the next scene that the conviction makes inevitable but the speaker has not yet seen?
  5. Render one of the diagnostic answers as a small concrete detail in the scene or just after it. Saunders's term: a smelly pipe. The detail does not refute the speech and is not framed as commentary on it. It is an observable fact that, by attribution to the character who delivered the speech, makes the speech read differently on rereading. The pipe stinks; the friend cannot sleep; the man who exhorted "Do not cease to do good" has neglected ordinary courtesy. The story does not say so; the prose lets the reader notice.
  6. Stage the back-scan. After the destabilizing detail, the reader will scan back through the story re-reading earlier elements through the revised question. Saunders walks his own scan in "Gooseberries": "I find myself scanning back through the story, looking for: Happiness, Things About." Make sure earlier elements support the back-scan. The swimming scene must be there for the reader to find when they go looking; the work the writer does in advance is to plant the countervailing evidence, not to engineer the reread.
  7. Let both readings stand. Saunders's criterion: "the two readings coexist, making a truth bigger than either would have alone." Do not write a sentence that resolves the tension. The speech remains true; the speaker remains flawed; the reader holds both.
sections_html.signal: A reader cannot tell whether the writer endorses the speech. The truth-content of the position is felt and remembered; the speaker's flaws are also felt and remembered. The reader's relationship to the speech is "this is true and this is also suspect, and the story does not tell me which to weigh more." You can name, on rereading your draft, both the place where the belief lands with full conviction and the place (often a small concrete detail near the close, often not where you expected it) where the speaker's having delivered it is complicated by an action of theirs the prose merely reports.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, point at the passage where the belief is currently expressed. Ask: "If a reader asked me whether I believe this, what would I say?" If the answer is "yes, this is what I think" or "no, but I want it to be in the story anyway," the belief is currently functioning as authorial position.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes "the character believes it; the story takes the belief seriously; whether I as the writer endorse it is no longer the question the prose is asking." You can also point at the destabilizing detail you wrote in and name what it is doing for the speaker's credibility.
sections_html.failure:
Without this discipline, a story with a sincere belief in it slides toward one of two failures. The lecture failure: the belief is delivered as authorial commentary, by the narrator or by an unflawed mouthpiece character, and the story functions as a vehicle for the position. The reader, sensing the vehicle, either agrees and feels flattered or disagrees and stops reading; in neither case does the story do work that an essay could not have done better. The hedge failure: the writer, sensing the polemic risk, waters the belief down until it has lost its truth-content; the reader feels the hedge and the story no longer has anything to destabilize. Saunders names the third path: deliver the belief in full conviction, attribute it to a character, render a small concrete fact about that character that complicates the conviction without refuting it, and let both stand. The reader exits with the belief's power intact and the speaker's flaws also intact, and the story has done what essays cannot: held two true things in suspension.

A second failure mode: confusing this skill with skaz-narrator. Skaz-narrator is about a narrator-character whose graceless register is itself the meaning; the reader is meant to see past the narrator's voice to the truth the narrator is missing. Belief-by-character-attribution is the opposite shape: the speaker delivers the belief with full sincerity and the writer believes (or once believed) the belief is true; the destabilization comes from the speaker's flaws as a person, not from the speaker's narrative voice. Conflating them produces either a skaz speech that no one is meant to take seriously (which loses the truth-content) or a sincere speech delivered by a "wise" character with no flaws to complicate it (which loses the destabilization).
sections_html.example: Saunders gives a worked instance from his own story "Victory Lap." He set out to write a sweet, nonviolent story like Chekhov's "After the Theatre," in which a teenaged girl waits for her mother to pick her up for dance class and thinks giddily about love. The sweet-monologue version was no good — chatty, anecdotal, static, nothing at stake. But in the inner monologue Saunders had let the girl blurt: "To do good, you just have to decide to do good. You have to be brave. You have to stand up for what's right." Saunders identifies the blurt as a position he held at sixteen and no longer held at fifty-one. The story's response, which Saunders hears as if in dialogue with the character: "Oh, really?" The story is now committed to challenging the girl's facile belief. Saunders does not narrate the challenge as authorial counter-argument; he lets the plot test the belief by what happens next, which (he says without spoiling) is the opposite of the sweet nonviolent story he meant to write. The procedure is the same as Chekhov's: a sincere belief is attributed to a particular character, the character delivers it with conviction, the writer takes a step back, and the story reacts to the character's having said it. The destabilization arrives as event, not as commentary.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'," lines 6073-6107 (the smelly-pipe reveal at the close: Ivan's pipe stinks, his friend Burkin cannot sleep, the moral thinker has acted thoughtlessly; the reader's back-scan and the discovery of the swimming scene as countervailing evidence; "the two readings coexist, making a truth bigger than either would have alone"), 6321-6353 (Ivan's speech as "the stuff of an excellent essay"; "Chekhov makes double use of the speech by attributing it to Ivan"; "Chekhov has it both ways"; the export-fragments-of-self framing; the diagnostic questions about consequences, suspicious overtones, latent unforeseen consequences, smelly pipes; the "Victory Lap" worked instance with the sixteen-year-old's blurt and the story's "Oh, really?" response), 6361-6369 (the inner-orchestra orientation: "Any idea we express is just one of many we have within us"; "Writing, we get a chance to change the mix"; "every person in the world has his or her inner orchestra, and the instruments present in their orchestras are, roughly speaking, the same as the ones in ours").
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also persistent-self-contradiction. The two are complementary and commonly fire together. Belief-by-character-attribution renders one earnest position with full conviction and destabilizes it by a small concrete fact about the speaker; persistent-self-contradiction is the macro-structural method that builds counterweights across the rest of the story so the destabilization fits a thinking machine rather than a single hedge. The first is the move at one position; the second is the architecture that holds many.
  • See also skaz-narrator (kept separate from this skill: skaz renders graceless misfire whose voice the reader sees past, while attribution renders sincere belief whose truth-content the reader is meant to feel; conflating them collapses both moves) and follow-the-voice (the same disposition applied to drafting: write in the voice of a character who is not you, let the prose generate, and trust the story to react).

name: causality-not-sequence
description: You are drafting or revising a sequence in which things happen but the chain reads as episodic. Sentences are individually fine, scenes are individually fine, but the reader cannot say why each event follows the one before. The connector between beats is "and then" rather than "and so."

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Saunders contrasts a 'lesser writer' version of the lost-on-the-road sequence (random crossroads choice, Nikita falls asleep 'just because,' they get lost) with Tolstoy's version (Vasili pokes at Nikita about his wife and the horse purchase; Nikita is irritated; at the crossroads Vasili asks Nikita's wise opinion and ignores it; Nikita, taunted and dismissed, falls asleep as withdrawal; Vasili, unmonitored, gets them lost). The difference is named: 'increased causality.' Saunders identifies causality as one of the two things separating writers who publish from those who don't, gives Forster's queen-and-king formulation, says causality is to the writer what melody is to the songwriter, and offers the painted-kite-and-wind metaphor. The skill is then exercised on the sled-of-drunken-peasants race: the race fires Vasili up; his elation causes him to drive on more boldly without examining the way-marks; that boldness causes them to get lost again. Each beat causes the next, and the next is felt as essential.

sources.external: E. M. Forster, Aspects of the Novel (1927), Chapter V ('The Plot')
sources.note: The canonical home of the queen-and-king formulation Saunders cites in passing. Forster's chapter draws the operative distinction at length: a story is 'a narrative of events arranged in their time-sequence,' a plot is 'a narrative of events, the emphasis falling on causality.' His extended treatment supplies the broader claim Saunders compresses into one paragraph, including the mystery-as-suspended-causality elaboration that helps a reader recognize causal weight in their own drafts.

sections_html.when: You are revising a stretch of narrative and the events feel arbitrary. A character makes a choice for no traceable reason. A scene ends with a development the previous pages have not earned. You can list what happens but cannot say why each thing happens. A reader you trust has reported feeling unmoored, like following a clueless tour guide. Reach for this whenever your sequence reads as "and then, and then" rather than "and so, and so."
sections_html.move:
1. Mark the major beats of the sequence in order. A beat is any unit of action that changes the state of the story: a decision, an action, an encounter, a setback.
  2. For each beat after the first, ask explicitly: "What about the prior beat caused this one?" The answer must be statable as a sentence in the form "Because X happened, Y had to follow." If you cannot complete that sentence, the link is missing.
  3. If a link is missing, do not paper over it with a transition sentence. Either install causation upstream (give an earlier beat a feature that makes the next beat a natural consequence) or remove the unlinked beat. Saunders's failure case: the lesser writer makes Nikita "just" fall asleep; the better writer arranges for Nikita to be taunted and ignored at a crossroads, so falling asleep is felt as withdrawal, which then enables Vasili (unmonitored) to get them lost.
  4. Test each beat against the previous one and the next. The beat earns its place if the previous beat caused it and it causes the next. A beat caused by nothing in particular is filler. A beat that causes nothing in particular is a dead end.
  5. For the load-bearing escalation moments — the beats that change the terms of the story — make the causation visible to the reader. Saunders's example on the sled race: the race fires Vasili up (cause shown via direct text "this encounter cheered and enlivened Vasili"); his elation causes him to drive boldly without examining way-marks (cause shown via the next sentence's behavior); that boldness causes the next essential beat (they get lost again). Three causal links across three sentences; none of them transition-words; all of them earned.
  6. Use the Forster test on adjacent sentences. "The queen died and the king died" is sequence. "The queen died and the king died of grief" is causation. The added phrase is small; the change in meaning is total. Apply this test to every adjacency where you suspect a missing link.
sections_html.signal: For every beat in the sequence after the first, you can finish the sentence "This beat happens because, in the previous beat, ___." A reader cold to the draft can summarize the sequence as a chain of causes ("he poked the servant about a sore subject, the servant withdrew, and with no one to check him he chose the wrong road") rather than a list of events ("a man and a servant left, then they got lost"). The sequence has acquired the appearance of meaning, which is what causation produces.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, name a sequence in your draft and ask: "If I cut any single beat from this sequence, what about the rest of the sequence would no longer make sense?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer changes from "I'm not sure anything would" to specific downstream consequences ("the loss of the road would no longer have a cause, since this is the beat where the servant withdraws and stops correcting his master").
sections_html.failure: Without causation, a sequence of events does not become a story. It remains a list. Saunders's diagnosis: most beginners can make things happen ("a dog barked," "the house exploded," "Darren kicked the tire"), but few can make one thing seem to cause the next, and the gap between those two skills is what separates writers who go on to publish from those who don't. The reader of a non-causal sequence experiences it as a clueless tour guide gesturing at unrelated rooms — the frustrated, unmoored feeling that asks, "What am I supposed to be noting?" Each detail might be vivid in isolation; none of them earn their place by being load-bearing. Saunders's metaphor: a beautifully hand-painted kite lying on the grass is admirable, but only causation is the wind that lifts it. A pleasing prose sequence without causation is a flightless kite.
sections_html.example: Saunders contrasts two versions of the lost-on-the-road sequence. The lesser writer's version: as they leave home Vasili and Nikita pass some things and discuss random topics; at a crossroads Vasili takes the straight road for reasons not provided; Nikita falls asleep just because; somehow they get lost. Each beat occurs but none causes the next. Tolstoy's version: as they leave home Vasili pokes at Nikita about his wife (a sore subject) and asks if Nikita plans to buy a horse (signaling another upcoming cheat); Nikita is irritated. At the crossroads Vasili asks Nikita's opinion (which is wise, given the weather) and ignores it; Nikita, taunted then dismissed, says only "Just as you please" and withdraws. With no one engaged to check his judgment, Nikita falls asleep. Vasili, unmonitored, steers by his own lights and gets them lost. Every beat is caused by the prior; getting lost is felt as the consequence of a chain that started with a poke about a wife. The same plot summary, the same end-state, totally different reading experience — and the difference is "increased causality."
sections_html.source:
Chapter "And Yet They Drove On," lines 4395-4426 (the "lesser writer" contrast, the queen-and-king formulation, the "causality is to the writer what melody is to the songwriter" claim, the painted-kite-and-wind metaphor) and lines 4435-4451 (the sled race exercised through the explicit causal chain: race causes elation causes bold driving causes getting lost).

E. M. Forster, Aspects of the Novel (1927), Chapter V ("The Plot"). The queen-and-king formulation Saunders quotes lives here, embedded in Forster's full distinction between story ("a narrative of events arranged in their time-sequence") and plot ("a narrative of events, the emphasis falling on causality"). Useful when a reader wants the canonical formal treatment of the and-then vs and-so contrast, including Forster's mystery-as-suspended-causality elaboration that further sharpens the test.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also increasing-specification (causation often becomes available only once characters are specified enough that "because of who he is, the next beat had to follow"). Specification supplies the why-this-not-that for each beat.
  • See also mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts (the rotation among public/private/objective makes causal links visible; one character's withdrawal becomes the cause of another's misjudgment) and transformation-by-redirected-energy (the climactic transformation is rendered as the next causal beat after the prior pages, not as a soliloquy).

name: compress-to-force-resolution
description: You are working on a story whose mid-section has produced a real charge in the reader, an unresolved question about how the protagonist will respond to an injustice or revelation. The natural pace of the character's life would dilute that charge across many pages or never produce a clean answer at all. You sense the question is the engine of the remaining story but the story has no engine-room to run it in.

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: Saunders names the move on the roof-fall: 'But then Tolstoy does something smart: he knocks Alyosha off that roof. This has the effect of forcing all of the psychic consequences of the broken engagement to play out within a compressed time frame. If Alyosha is going to act on that disappointed energy, he's only got the remainder of this last page in which to do it.' He surveys the variants the natural pace would have produced (outwardly-same-but-bitter, eventually confronts father, never causes anything and dissipates) and observes 'this seems the variant Alyosha is fated to live.' Then names the function: 'the fall speeds up the time frame in which our question (What is Alyosha going to do about that injustice?) will be answered.' The principle stated: 'Alyosha dies not because he falls off a roof but because Tolstoy, at this late point in his art, knows what it is that we want to know and aims to give it to us as quickly as possible.' The Akakii Akakievich / 'The Overcoat' parallel sharpens the contrast: Akakii's death is a natural continuation of a lifetime of petty abuse; Alyosha's fall is 'not caused by anything, really' and is instrumental, the writer's tool to foreshorten the answer.

sections_html.when: You are revising or drafting and have arrived at the stretch after the central event. The story has produced a real coil, an unresolved question the reader is now waiting for the protagonist to answer. The protagonist's life, at its natural pace, would not answer the question quickly. The honest rendering of the next months or years of that life would be either a long slow dilution of the charge or, more likely, no resolution at all. You sense the story is over once the question is answered, but the story has no machinery to force the answer in the time it has left. Reach for this whenever you can name the charged question the story has raised and cannot see how the protagonist's life, left to itself, would ever answer it.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the charged question in one present-tense sentence. Saunders's worked example: "What is Alyosha going to do about that injustice?" Or, equivalently: "How is the injustice Alyosha has experienced going to come home to roost?" The sentence should be a question the prior pages have made the reader carry forward, not a thematic abstraction.
  2. List, in one or two phrases per item, the variants the natural pace of the protagonist's life would produce. Saunders's worked variants for Alyosha: outwardly-same-but-inwardly-bitter, eventually-confronts-father, bitterness-dissipates-and-never-causes-anything. Each variant is a faithful rendering of how this specific character would actually live out the coiled energy.
  3. Read the variants and ask: which one does the character's nature most plausibly point to? Saunders's diagnosis of Alyosha is the third variant: the bitterness never causes anything, and "this seems the variant Alyosha is fated to live." The natural variant is often the least dramatic.
  4. Decide whether the natural variant resolves the charged question on the page. If it does, the story does not need the move; render the natural variant. If it does not (because the dilution is the answer, or because the answer arrives only outside the story's frame), the story needs an event that compresses the time available for the question to discharge.
  5. Introduce a compressing event. The event must be of a kind that forces the discharge into the remaining text. Saunders's instance is a fatal accident; the same logic applies to any event that limits the protagonist's available time, attention, or location: an arrest, a deadline, a final visit, a forced departure. The event should not be the answer to the question; it should be the foreshortening that makes the answer come into the remaining pages.
  6. Allow the event to be uncaused or only loosely caused. Saunders observes that Alyosha's fall is "not caused by anything, really. 'His foot slipped and he fell with his shovel.' He just dies by chance, in a manner equivalent to, say, being hit by a falling tree." A heavily motivated compressing event will read as the story's answer rather than as the device that compels the answer. The reader needs to feel the event's instrumentality without seeing the writer's hand on it.
  7. Write the discharge into the remaining real estate. The remaining pages are now structurally obligated to render the answer to the charged question, in whatever form the protagonist's nature produces. The compression is what makes that answer felt rather than dispersed.
sections_html.signal: You can name the charged question in one present-tense sentence at the boundary between the central event and the compressing event. You can name the compressing event and the remaining-page count in which the discharge must occur. The compressing event is uncaused or only loosely caused at the level of plot, but is structurally necessary at the level of the reader's experience: the reader feels the question pressing against the remaining pages and the answer arriving under that pressure. A reader cannot point to the compressing event as "what the story is about"; they can point to the discharge that follows it as the answer the story raised.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of the post-event stretch in your draft: "What is the question the reader is carrying past this point, and how many pages does the natural unfolding of the character's life need to answer it?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the second answer names a compressing event that has reduced the page-count to a feasible remainder, and the discharge fits inside that remainder without rushing or summarizing.
sections_html.failure: Without this procedure, a writer who has produced a real charge in the mid-story is faced with two bad options. The first is to render the natural unfolding faithfully, which produces a long slow dilution of the charge across pages the reader experiences as inert; the question the prior pages raised becomes the question the prior pages used to raise, and the reader registers the loss as the story going slack. The second is to drop the question and let the protagonist's life go on without it, which betrays the prior pages by treating the charge they produced as not real. Both failures share a root: the writer is honoring the world's continuity at the cost of the story's structure. Saunders's diagnosis: a story knows what the reader wants to know and aims to give it to them as quickly as possible; the late-art writer's tool for that aim is to foreshorten the time available for the answer, by introducing an event that compresses the remaining stretch. The compression is not a cheat; it is the structural decision that lets the prior pages' charge resolve.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks the closing structural decision in "Alyosha the Pot." The story has produced a real charge: Alyosha has burst into tears on being told to forget the marriage, the reader knows the cheerful obedience has cost him something this time, the question pressing against the remaining text is "What is Alyosha going to do about that injustice?" Saunders surveys the variants Alyosha's actual nature would produce, names the third one as the most likely (the bitterness dissipates without ever causing anything), and observes that this is the variant Alyosha is fated to live. Then he names what Tolstoy does: knock Alyosha off the roof. The fall is barely caused at the plot level — a foot slips, a shovel falls — and Saunders parallels it explicitly with Akakii Akakievich's death in "The Overcoat," where Akakii's collapse is the natural continuation of a lifetime of abuse and Alyosha's is not. The function of the fall is structural: it foreshortens the time available for the discharge, and the remaining page is structurally obligated to render whatever answer Alyosha's nature can produce within it. The answer that arrives — the small protest of the burst-into-tears, the un-resolved final amazement, the still-cheerful obedience held even on the deathbed — is the discharge the compression made possible.
sections_html.source: Chapter "The Wisdom of Omission," lines 6781-6799 (the roof-fall as compression device; the variant-list of natural-pace unfoldings; the "fall speeds up the time frame in which our question will be answered" naming; the late-art principle "knows what we want to know and aims to give it to us as quickly as possible"; the Akakii Akakievich / "The Overcoat" parallel that distinguishes the naturally-caused death from the instrumentally-caused fall).
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also withhold-the-resolving-interior and transformation-by-redirected-energy (the two climactic-resolution skills the compression operates with: the foreshortener creates the time pressure under which either the omitted interior or the redirected action discharges).
  • See also skip-the-meaningless-interval (the related pacing move: that skill leaps over time the story has no work to do in, while this skill introduces an event that limits the time available for the discharge of a charged question).

name: confess-the-oddness
description: You are working on the ending of a story whose surface has accumulated unaddressed implausibilities, narrator-quirks, or structural irregularities the reader has been silently tracking. Any explanation you might write would dissipate the strangeness that gave the story its truth. You sense the ending must address the cart of suspicions but cannot resolve them without ruining the effect.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders names the move and walks Gogol's closing: at the end of 'The Nose' the narrator confesses, 'Only now, on second thoughts, can we see that there is much that is improbable in it.' Saunders frames the choice: 'we feel that all of this needs to be explained, somehow, and our friend the lesser writer might be tempted to do just that ('Actually, it turns out, what had really happened was...'), but if the story's crazy logic gets explained away, then so does that revelatory feeling it has produced in us, the sense that the story's logic is actually the logic of the universe.' The procedure: 'He confesses... He addresses exactly none of the concerns he's raised... but the flailing gestures he's making in that direction have the effect of emptying out my TICHN cart.' He gives three analogies for the move's everyday equivalent: the dinner-party 'this has been such a stilted conversation' admission, the bus driver who turns around and says 'Jeez, that's one ominous clunk, folks, isn't it?', and the car dealer who 'in mid-transaction, wonders aloud how he can stand here telling me all of these lies, and I respond to his honesty with a renewed burst of trust and end up buying the car.' Saunders summarizes the dialog with the narrator: 'I make a protest about the story's failure to cohere logically. I know, right? the narrator says. It's a train wreck, isn't it? Somehow that's enough.'

sections_html.when: You are at the end of a draft whose middle has accumulated things the reader has been politely ignoring: implausibilities, unexplained gaps, a narrator who keeps misplacing emphasis, structural choices that do not quite cohere. The reader has been tracking these on a private cart (the TICHN cart, in the existing skill's vocabulary). You sense the ending needs to engage the cart, but you also know that explaining away the irregularities would dissolve the spell that made them work. The lesser-writer move is to write a paragraph that resolves the loose ends. Reach for this whenever the temptation is to explain and you can feel the explanation would ruin the story.
sections_html.move:
1. Inventory the cart. List, briefly, the implausibilities, narrator-quirks, and structural irregularities the reader has been silently tracking. Be specific: which gaps, which improper emphases, which unexplained transitions. The list is for you, not for the prose.
  2. Refuse the lesser-writer move. Do not write a paragraph that retroactively explains how the impossible thing happened, what the narrator really meant, or how the irregularities resolve into a coherent system. Saunders's note: "if the story's crazy logic gets explained away, then so does that revelatory feeling it has produced in us, the sense that the story's logic is actually the logic of the universe."
  3. Have the prose acknowledge the oddness directly. The narrator (or the prose itself) names the irregularities as irregularities. Saunders's Gogol-line: "Only now, on second thoughts, can we see that there is much that is improbable in it." The acknowledgment can be performed through the narrator's voice (consistent with the voice you have been using) or through a closing meta-comment, but it must be an admission, not a solution.
  4. Address none of the concerns. The crucial constraint: the confession must not actually answer any of the questions it raises. Saunders: "He addresses exactly none of the concerns he's raised... but the flailing gestures he's making in that direction have the effect of emptying out my TICHN cart." The reader's residual suspicion drops away because the irregularities have been seen, not because they have been explained.
  5. Stay in voice. If your story has a skaz narrator, the confession arrives in skaz. If your story has a transparent narrator, the confession is a sudden honest moment from that narrator. The voice consistency is part of why the move works.
  6. Let the irregularities remain. The story does not become coherent. The reader does not, on rereading, find that the gaps now close. The shards-not-vase arrangement stays as it was. What changes is the reader's relationship to the gaps: they have been named and let stand.
sections_html.signal: A reader who would have, before the ending, complained that the story does not add up, instead reports that the ending is satisfying. The complaint is not that you have explained the irregularities; the satisfaction comes from the prose's having seen them. The reader's residual suspicion is gone but the gaps remain. On a second reading, the reader notices that the confession resolves nothing and is not bothered by this.
sections_html.probe:
Before. Read your current ending and ask: am I explaining the loose ends? If yes, you are performing the lesser-writer move. Write down what you would lose by deleting the explanation. (Usually: the spell.)

After. Same prompt. The ending acknowledges the loose ends without resolving them. The prose admits the story does not add up. The reader, instead of being confused or annoyed, feels seen. The irregularities are now load-bearing rather than evidence of failure.
sections_html.failure: The endings that destroy their own stories by explaining them. The writer, anxious about coherence, writes a final paragraph that retroactively resolves the impossibilities into a dream, a hoax, a misunderstanding, a metaphor. The strangeness that gave the story its truth-claim collapses into a tidy fable. The opposite failure is also prevented: endings that simply trail off, leaving the reader irritated by the cart of unresolved suspicions. The confession is the third path: the prose names what it has done, and the act of naming it does the work that an explanation could not.
sections_html.example: A story has accumulated by its closing pages: a nose found in bread, a couple's irrational reaction, an odd plan to ditch the nose, a world full of policemen, a Nose-as-man with a face that may or may not exist, a series of offices that respond to the impossibility with strangely mild civility, and a narrator who has been digressing, misplacing emphasis, and failing to register the strangeness. The lesser-writer move is to write a final paragraph explaining the dream / spell / hoax / chemical hallucination. Gogol instead has the narrator turn directly to the reader: "Only now, on second thoughts, can we see that there is much that is improbable in it." The narrator goes on to flounder: he criticizes the wrong things (Kovalyov used "an improper method to advertise for one's nose"), digresses into "it's improper, embarrassing, not nice!", asks "how did the nose come to be in a newly baked loaf?", and admits "I simply don't know what to make of it." None of the actual questions are answered. But the cart empties. The reader's residual suspicion drops away. The irregularities remain, and they now feel like the right shape.
sections_html.source: George Saunders, A Swim in a Pond in the Rain, "The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'," lines 5648-5678.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts track-non-normative-excesses. Use confess-the-oddness at the ending of a draft whose middle has accumulated unredeemable excesses, when the question is "how do I close without explaining them away?"; the procedure is to acknowledge them without resolving them. Use track-non-normative-excesses while drafting or critiquing, when the question is "is this excess being redeemed?"; the procedure is to log items and watch the rest of the work pay them off. The cart that the first skill empties is built by the second.
  • See also skaz-narrator (the confession is often delivered in skaz, in voice with the narrator who has been misplacing emphasis throughout) and psychological-physics-of-strangeness (the strange-premise stories that most need this ending move are the ones whose physics this skill governs).

name: convert-moral-critique-to-technical
description: A story you are reading, revising, or teaching has been flagged for a moral failing (it seems sexist, racist, classist, homophobic, transphobic, pedantic, appropriative, derivative). The accusation is sticky and general. As stated, you can neither accept it as a final verdict nor dismiss it, and either way you cannot act on it. You suspect there is a fixable inequity in the prose itself but you have not located it.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #4
sources.note: The Gogol 'Nevsky Prospect' classroom episode. A student calls the story sexist. Saunders asks 'Where?'. The student locates two passages: when a man is insulted, Gogol enters the character's head and we hear his response; when a woman is insulted, the third-person narrator steps in and makes a joke at her expense. The class then imagines the corrected story with internal monologue equally distributed and a 'collective sigh/smile' as they see 'the better story it could have been: just as dark and strange, but funnier and more honest.' Saunders generalizes: 'any story that suffers from what seems like a moral failing... will be seen, with sufficient analytical snooping, to be suffering from a technical failing, and if that failing is addressed, it will (always) become a better story.'

sources.chapter: Afterthought #4
sources.note: The worked instance on 'Master and Man': the floating accusation 'Tolstoy seems to be exhibiting class bias' is converted to 'In (at least) two places, when Nikita comes home from the hospital and in their respective death scenes, Nikita is denied the interiority that Tolstoy gave Vasili in similar moments.' Saunders walks the diagnosis: Section VII gives Nikita a 'less interesting, less detailed' interior pass than Section VI gives Vasili; Section X gives Nikita's twenty later years and his death without the long inner monologue Tolstoy granted Vasili at the parallel moment of his death in Section IX. The downstream consequence is that Nikita never thinks of Vasili, never reflects on the sacrifice, walks off unchanged: 'If a person gives his life to save another person, and the saved person never thinks about it, doesn't appear grateful, seems unchanged by it, it makes us wonder about the value of the sacrifice. It also makes us wonder about the person saved. It also makes us wonder about the writer.' The corrective exercise: imagine the version of Section X in which Nikita is granted narrative resources equal to Vasili's at parallel moments. 'Rewrite Section X. Write it like Tolstoy. Use, you know, a lot of facts.'

sections_html.when: A story has been called sexist, racist, classist, homophobic, transphobic, pedantic, appropriative, or derivative. Maybe a student said it, or a workshop, or a reviewer, or your own quiet reading. The accusation is general. As long as it stays general, two bad things happen: defenders dismiss it, accusers escalate, and nobody fixes anything. Reach for this whenever a moral concern about a story is real enough to take seriously and abstract enough that you cannot point to a paragraph and say what to change.
sections_html.move:
1. Refuse both the verdict and the dismissal. The accusation is not the answer, but it is data. Treat it as a noticed effect on a reader whose source in the prose has not yet been located.
  2. Ask "Where, exactly?" Get specific citations. If the accuser cannot supply them, ask them to re-read with the question in hand. Two or three concrete passages are enough.
  3. For each citation, name the technical maneuver that produced the moral effect. The most common shape is inequitable narration: one character was granted a narrative resource that another, at a parallel moment, was denied. Resources to check: interior monologue, omniscient access to thought, time on the page, narrator's investment in the character's inner life, freedom from authorial joke at their expense, full rendering of pivotal scenes (homecoming, death, the moment of decision).
  4. Audit parallel moments. Find the structural twins in the story (both die, both come home, both face the test, both react to the same event) and lay them next to each other. The technical inequity is now nameable as a sentence of the form: "At moment X, character Y is denied [narrative resource Z] that character W received at parallel moment X'."
  5. Follow the inequity downstream. If a saved or transformed character is followed past the pivotal event, check whether the prose grants them the interior life to register the event in subsequent action. A saved person who never thinks of the saver, never reflects on the sacrifice, walks off unchanged is the strongest tell that the inequity is structural, not local.
  6. Write the corrected version. Imagine and draft the rebalanced passage. Grant the denied character the interior monologue at the parallel moment. Render their death with the same omniscient lens. Skip the narrator's joke at their expense. Hold the corrected version next to the original and confirm the change is, in fact, an improvement (two-version-comparison handles this verification step).
  7. State the fix as a craft instruction, not a moral grade. The accusation "the story is X-ist" becomes "at moments A, B, C, character Y needs the same narrative resources character W got at parallel moments." The first is unactionable; the second is a revision plan.
sections_html.signal: You can produce a sentence of the form "In (at least) N places, character Y is denied [resource Z] that character W received at parallel moment(s)." You can name the parallel moments. You can describe what the corrected version would look like. The moral charge has not gone away, but it has moved into a form the writer can act on, the critic can verify, and the next reader can check.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, write the moral concern as a single sentence ("the story is X-ist toward Y characters"). Try to derive a revision instruction from it. Notice that you cannot, or that the instruction you derive ("be less X-ist") cannot be applied to a specific paragraph.

After applying, write the converted observation. Try to derive a revision instruction from that. The skill landed if the new instruction names a specific moment, a specific resource, and a specific imbalance the writer could address in one sitting.
sections_html.failure: Without this conversion, two failures replace each other indefinitely. The writer or defender treats the moral accusation as something to argue against on its own terms ("I didn't mean it that way," "you're reading too much in"), and the prose stays exactly as it was, because no specific change has been proposed. The accuser, frustrated, escalates the moral framing, which is now even less actionable. Saunders's diagnosis is direct: the moral concern is real, and it is also incomplete; the prose is doing something specific to produce it, and that something is fixable. The conversion does not soften the critique; it sharpens it into a form the writer cannot wave away and the critic does not have to keep restating in louder versions of the same sentence. A reader's "this is sexist" and a writer's "I didn't mean to be" are both true and both useless. "Here, here, and here, the woman gets a narrator joke where the man gets a thought" is the version both can use.
sections_html.example:
Saunders is teaching Gogol's "Nevsky Prospect" and a student says she does not like it because it is sexist. Saunders asks where. The student locates two passages: one where a man is insulted, where Gogol drops into the character's head and we hear his interior response; one where a woman is insulted, where the third-person narrator steps in and makes a joke at her expense. Saunders then asks the class to imagine the story if Gogol had distributed interior monologue equally. There is a pause and what Saunders calls a collective sigh/smile as the class sees, at once, the version that would have been "just as dark and strange, but funnier and more honest." The accusation has been converted: from "the story is sexist" to "the story has inequitable narration at the moments of insult." The first is a verdict; the second is a revision instruction Gogol could have followed.

The same procedure applied to "Master and Man": the floating accusation "Tolstoy is exhibiting class bias toward Nikita" gets located. Section VI gives Vasili an extended pass through his coping methods (cigarettes, disparagement, bragging, money, victorious thinking) failing in the freezing storm. Section VII gives Nikita the parallel pass: less interesting, less detailed, his fears soothed by the mere thought of God. Section IX closes Vasili's death with a long interior monologue. Section X gives Nikita's twenty later years and his death in bare facts ("He died at home as he had wished... under the icons with a lighted taper in his hands"), with the explicit deferral "we'll have to wait for our own deaths to see whether Nikita was disappointed or found there what he expected." The downstream consequence: Nikita, twenty years later, never reflects on the sacrifice, never asks why his master did that, walks off as if the sled night never happened. The conversion: "in at least two places — Nikita's homecoming and the two death scenes — Nikita is denied the interiority Tolstoy gave Vasili at parallel moments." The corrective exercise Saunders sets is: rewrite Section X with Nikita granted narrative resources equal to Vasili's. Write it like Tolstoy. Use, you know, a lot of facts.
sections_html.source: Chapter "Afterthought #4," lines 4796-4805 (the "Nevsky Prospect" classroom episode and the general thesis: any story whose moral failing is real will, on inspection, be a technical failing, and if the technical failing is addressed the story will be a better story); lines 4754-4784, 4794, 4804, 4810-4820 (the worked diagnosis on "Master and Man," the parallel-moments audit, the downstream-unchanged-savee as evidence of denied interiority, and the corrective rewrite-Section-X exercise).
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends ask-strategic-not-interpretive. The strategic re-posing converts interpretive questions into questions a writer can act on; this skill is the same shape applied to moral accusations, with the specific procedure being a parallel-moments audit that surfaces inequitable narration.
  • See also mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts (the most common technical inequity surfaces here: one character was granted a vantage another, at a parallel moment, was denied) and two-version-comparison (the corrected version is composed and held against the original to verify that the rebalanced narration is in fact an improvement).

name: cornfeld-principle
description: You are revising or critiquing a draft and you suspect a particular scene, chapter, or section is doing less than it should be doing. The scene reads fine in isolation, even pleasantly, but you cannot say what it earns its place by accomplishing.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders attributes the principle to producer Stuart Cornfeld, names it explicitly, and tests Chekhov's teahouse scene against both criteria.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Sharpens the second criterion (advance the story in a non-trivial way) by giving it a procedural shape. A structural unit that advances the story tends to do so partly by enacting its own miniature arc — its own exposition, rising action, and climactic moment. Saunders frames this as Freytag's-triangle-per-unit: 'a structural unit, like a story, wants to look something like Freytag's triangle... if a structural unit in a story we're writing isn't shaped like that, we might wonder if it wants to be; if it is shaped like that, we might want to make that shape sharper.' Worked on the second Grishkino stop, where the 'young people getting out of hand' conversation is identified as the unit's climactic moment; the cozy-warmth, Nikita-not-drinking, and Paulson-quoting-grandson material is identified as exposition that needs the conversation to convert it into rising action. The fold sharpens the test: when the second Cornfeld criterion stalls on a unit, ask whether the unit has its own miniature arc, and if not, ask whether it should.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Names a recognized exception to the two criteria: a passage that produces strong reader-side joy or delight and was clearly written from the writer's pleasure rather than from plot necessity is allowed to stay even when it fails both Cornfeld tests. Saunders gives the worked instance: in the newspaper-office scene of 'The Nose,' a group of people waiting to place ads 'get transformed into that for which they are advertising, and suddenly that little office is full of a coachman of sober conduct... a nineteen-year-old serf girl... a sound droshky with one spring missing... a young and fiery dappled-gray horse... a summer residence with all the appurtenances.' Saunders names this 'what makes Gogol great, that he somehow felt inclined to do that, and then did it, with such strange, happy confidence. The inexplicable uptick in fondness for the world I feel, moving through that section, which is not essential to the action of the story but seems to have been done just for the fun of it, is, for me, what Gogol is all about.' The fold adds the exception condition to the audit: before cutting a passage that fails both criteria, run the joy-burst test. Does the passage produce a marked uptick of fondness, delight, or affection in the reader, written from the writer's pleasure rather than from plot necessity? If yes, the joy is itself non-trivial; the passage stays. The diagnosis question: would cutting this prune the writer's best instinct? The contrast condition is narrow — the test is uptick-of-fondness, not 'I like this scene' — but when it fires, it overrides the two main criteria.

sections_html.when: You are revising a draft, evaluating a workshop story, or trying to decide whether a scene should stay. The scene is enjoyable in its own right. You can describe what happens. You cannot describe what changes because of it. Reach for this when "but it's a good scene" is the only argument keeping a scene in.
sections_html.move:
1. Locate the structural unit you are testing. A scene, a chapter, a section, an interlude, a flashback, a digression.
  2. Apply the first criterion. Is the unit entertaining in its own right? Vivid, lively, funny, gripping, unsettling, beautiful — something that holds the reader on the page through it.
  3. Apply the second criterion. Does the unit advance the story in a non-trivial way? Name what is different on the other side: a character is in a new state, the story's question has been refined, a possibility has been opened or closed, a stake has been raised.
  4. Both must be true. If only the first is true, the unit is a vignette in a story that does not need a vignette there. If only the second is true, the unit is functional but boring and the reader is leaking attention. If both are true, keep it.
  5. If only the first is true, your choices are: cut the unit, replace it with one that also does (2), or rewrite it to do (2) while preserving the entertaining quality you already have.
sections_html.signal: For every structural unit in the draft, you can finish two sentences. "This unit is entertaining because ___." "This unit advances the story by ___." Any unit where the second sentence stalls is marked for cutting or rewriting. The marks point at concrete revision actions, not at vague reservations.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, name a scene you suspect is weak and ask: "Why am I unsure about this scene?" Note your answer. It will likely be vague.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes specific: "It is entertaining (this and that work), but on the other side of it the story is in the same state it was on the entry side, so it advances nothing." That answer tells you what to do.
sections_html.failure: Without this test, you keep scenes for the wrong reason. The scene is well-written, you remember writing it, your critique partner laughed at the joke in it, so it stays. Meanwhile the reader passes through it without anything in their understanding of the story changing, which is the exact failure Saunders calls out: the dog runs in and out, the ray of sunlight passes through the window, plates break, all in the manner of a real teahouse, none of it doing the work of a story. The draft accumulates such scenes and grows long without growing more story.
sections_html.example: Saunders tests Chekhov's teahouse scene against both criteria. Entertaining? Yes — there is a drunken peasant, a confused apology, the door's pulley banging. Advance the story? Yes, in a way that is easy to miss until you ask. Marya has, throughout the story so far, presented herself in interior monologue as a woman fallen among the rabble, and the reader has half-believed her. Inside the teahouse, an insulting peasant and a jeering reaction make her precarious social position visible from outside her head. The reader leaves the scene seeing her differently, and Saunders names the change: "Now we know" how far she has fallen. The scene satisfies both criteria.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 964-1008. Saunders names the principle, attributes it to producer Stuart Cornfeld, and walks the teahouse scene through both tests.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends heart-of-the-story. The two-criterion test for any structural unit (entertaining + advances the story) presupposes a destination for "advances the story." The heart of the story names that destination; the Cornfeld principle is the unit-by-unit test against it.
  • See also truncation-test (a specific diagnostic procedure for surfacing what an ending or internal unit supplies that the surrounding prose does not), take-responsibility-for-every-element (the element-by-element audit at finer scope), and two-version-comparison (the comparator method for naming what the unit's contribution actually is).

name: energy-not-aspiration
description: You are a writer producing technically careful work in the mode of a writer you admire and the work is coming back described as "interesting" or "well-crafted" rather than alive. You suspect your chosen voice is the wrong voice for you but cannot bring yourself to abandon it because it is the voice you wanted to have.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Saunders names the criterion as energy output, gives the wrist-mounted-meter / 1000-units thought experiment, and tells the autobiographical story of abandoning a Hemingwayesque mode for a 'Saunders Mountain' voice that was a 'shit-hill' but actually generated energy.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders sharpens the post-abandonment phase. Once the aspirational mode has been let go, the work is to refine the natural mode within its own grain rather than to re-stretch toward the original aspiration. He frames the choice as two models: 'One model of writing is that we strive upward to express ourselves precisely, at the highest levels of language (think Henry James). Another is that we surrender to our natural mode of expression, flawed though it may be, and, by way of concentrated work within that mode, raise it up, so to speak, creating a poetic rarefication of that (inefficient) form of expression.' The Gogol case is the worked instance: 'a provincial from Ukraine, a bit of a mama's boy who moved to St. Petersburg and found himself outclassed by the more aristocratic writers he met there.' Pevear: 'Russian prose had attained perfect ease and clarity in the works of Pushkin and Mikhail Lermontov. Gogol admired them greatly and did not try to match them. He set about creating another sort of medium, not imitative of the natural speech of educated men, not graced with the prose virtues of concision and accuracy, but apparently quite the opposite.' Sinyavsky: Gogol resorted 'not to the speech in which we talk, but rather, to the inability to speak in an ordinary way.' The 'fence of language' framing names the procedure: 'every soul is vast and wants to express itself fully. If it's denied an adequate instrument (and we're all denied that, at birth, some more than others), out comes... poetry, i.e., truth forced out through a restricted opening... The poet proves that language is inadequate by throwing herself at the fence of language and being bound by it. Poetry is the resultant bulging of the fence.' The corporate-poem worked example (5394) demonstrates the same principle in the wild: a 'not right' speech ('The stress being felt by some is, in terms of how we might view it is, we did not meet or exceed our goals...') becomes 'a poem, because it is not right. There's a true statement inside it (We failed and are fucked), but there's also something true about its not-rightness, the flavor of which tells us things about the speaker and his culture that aren't conveyed by We failed and are fucked.' The fold adds the second-half procedure: after the energy probe identifies the off-license mode that actually generates energy, the work is concentrated refinement within that mode (raise the inefficient form into a 'poetic rarefication of itself'), not a return to the aspirational mode through a back door.

sections_html.when: You have been writing for a while in a mode you chose because of writers you wanted to resemble: a painstaking realist, a Hemingwayesque minimalist, a Nabokovian stylist, a deeply spiritual Robinson-style writer, whichever lineage you admired. The work is coming back to you, from friends, editors, your own honest re-reads, with reactions like "interesting," "a lot going on," "you really worked hard on this." You are not getting reactions like laughter, tears, hunger for more. Reach for this whenever the gap between the writer you intended to be and the responses your prose actually produces has become impossible to ignore.
sections_html.move:
1. Run the energy probe. Set aside the question of whether your prose is good in the abstract. Ask instead: when someone reads it, do they react with energy (laughter, alarm, eagerness for more, an immediate physical response) or with respect (patience, "interesting," "you worked hard")? Saunders's image: the wrist-mounted meter measures energy output during dancing; the goal is 1,000 units. Aspiring to dance the way you wanted is irrelevant if the meter shows 50.
  2. Look for evidence of a different mode that does generate energy. Saunders's autobiographical case: he was writing minimal Hemingwayesque oilfield realism, eliciting "interesting" reactions, until on a boring conference call he scribbled some Seussian poems with cartoons and dropped them on the kitchen table; his wife laughed out loud reading them. That laugh was the meter going past 1,000 for the first time in years. The non-real-writing thing was the real-writing thing.
  3. Catalog the off-license modes. The voices you have been keeping out of your real work because they are not what a serious writer of your aspired lineage sounds like. Humor at awkward moments. A working-class register. An overdriven corporate voice. A raunchy, attention-seeking impulse. A kind of structure your tradition does not approve of.
  4. Try one. Write a story (not a fragment) entirely in one of the off-license modes. Pay attention to two specific signals: do you have stronger opinions while drafting (knowing immediately when a line is wrong, in the form of an impulse rather than a deliberation), and does the imaginary reader feel hungrier as the story proceeds rather than more patient.
  5. Read the result. Saunders's diagnostic: there will be some "essential me-ness" in it that no one else could have written. The story will likely also be embarrassing, exposing taste you would rather not have admitted to (working-class, raunchy, attention-seeking, sentimental, whatever). The two go together.
  6. Make the trade. Saunders's framing: you climbed Hemingway Mountain and could only ever be an acolyte at the top. You stumble back down and find a smaller hill labeled "Saunders Mountain" (your own name, your shit-hill). The hill is little and disappointing. It is also energetic. Decide to stand on it.
  7. Commit. Saunders is direct about what makes the small hill grow: not waiting for it to become impressive before committing, but committing to it as the working ground. The hill grows because you stay on it; if you keep climbing back to Hemingway Mountain whenever your shit-hill embarrasses you, the hill never grows.
  8. Continue applying the energy probe. Some of what you produce on the new hill will still be inert. The criterion is not "stay loyal to Saunders Mountain" but "stay loyal to whatever produces the energy." If a different mode shows up later that produces more energy still, follow it.
sections_html.signal: For your current writing project you can finish two sentences. "When I write in mode X, the meter reads ___." "When I write in mode Y, the meter reads ___." You can name which mode you are writing in, and you have stopped writing in modes whose meter reads low for the sake of being the writer you originally wanted to be. At least one piece of the new work has produced an energetic response (laughter, tears, eagerness, alarm) that your old mode was not producing.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "Am I writing in this voice because it generates energy, or because it is the voice I wanted to have?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes a specific energy reading on a specific mode, and you can name a piece of recent work whose energy output told you which mode to keep.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, you stay loyal to the writer you imagined being and produce inert work indefinitely. The work is technically defensible, often "well-crafted," and dead on the page. Editors and friends, sensing this, give the polite reactions writers dread, "interesting," "ambitious," "clearly a lot of work in there," and you take these as evidence of difficulty rather than evidence of low energy. Saunders is blunt about why this is fatal: the writer can choose what to write about, but cannot choose what they are able to make live. A writer who refuses the voice that lives, in favor of the voice they admire, is choosing inanimacy. The shit-hill problem is real (the voice that works will likely be smaller, less prestigious, embarrassing) but the alternative, climbing Hemingway Mountain to be an acolyte, is a much harder fate to recover from because you can spend decades there before you notice the meter never moved.
sections_html.example: In his early thirties, Saunders sees himself as a Hemingwayesque realist working out of his time in the Asian oil fields. The stories he writes are minimal, strict, efficient, lifeless, humor-free. The reactions he gets are the polite ones: "interesting," "a lot going on." On a boring conference call at his environmental engineering job, he scribbles dark Seussian poems with cartoons. He almost throws them out as not-real-writing. He brings them home, drops them on the table, walks off; from the kitchen he hears his wife laughing genuinely as she reads them, the first time in years anyone has reacted to his writing with pleasure. The next day he writes a futuristic theme-park story in the same overdriven, slightly raunchy, working-class corporate voice. He finds that in this mode he has stronger, immediate opinions about what is wrong, in the form of impulses rather than deliberations. The story becomes "The Wavemaker Falters," the first piece of CivilWarLand in Bad Decline. The voice is embarrassing to him; the work it produces is the best he has ever done. He names the trade explicitly: he had climbed Hemingway Mountain and could only be an acolyte at the top. Coming back down he finds a smaller hill marked "Saunders Mountain," disappointing, "kind of a shit-hill," but with his own name on it. He decides to stand on it and commit, and the hill grows because he stayed.
sections_html.source: Chapter "The Heart of the Story," lines 2064-2130. Saunders sets up the energy criterion via the wrist-meter / 1000-units image, generalizes it via the Olympic-sprinter-becomes-shotputter analogy, and demonstrates it on his own career, naming the moment of acceptance ("standing on the shit-hill") as the precondition for the hill growing into actual ground.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also follow-the-voice (the voice the energy probe identifies is the seed the writer then follows; energy-not-aspiration tells you which voice to follow, follow-the-voice tells you how to write from it).
  • See also escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint and forced-cut-pace-discovery (constraint exercises that bypass the writer-self-image and surface the off-license modes the energy probe is meant to identify).

name: escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint
description: You are a writer whose drafts catalogue and explain but never escalate, who can name the deficit ("my stories don't boil") but cannot produce a draft that does because the writer-self-image you channel when you sit down is the same one that produced the inert drafts. Category-by-category audits of an existing slack draft return "fine" because you have no felt knowledge of what generating-with-escalation feels like in the first place. You need a constraint exercise that produces a draft with rising action in a single session, bypassing the writer-self-image rather than arguing with it.

sources.chapter: Appendix B: An Escalation Exercise
sources.note: Saunders supplies the practice protocol: 45-min timer, write a 200-word story using only 50 unique words, track via a running list (plurals count as the same word), final length must be exactly 200 — not 199, not 201. He frames escalation as 'meaningful action' via the pot-of-water-on-the-stove (exposition) vs boiling-water (rising action) image, names the empirical observation that the exercise almost always produces rising action and sometimes funnier, more dramatic, more entertaining results than the writer's 'real' work, and gives the why: the conscious mind that would usually be thinking about themes, style, politics, or goals is kept busy counting words, which 'allows another part of her mind to step forward, a less conscious, more playful part.' He installs the read-aloud-afterward intensifier ('when I assign this exercise in class, I announce beforehand that everyone will be reading their story aloud afterward. This intensifies things.') with the Johnson hanging-in-a-fortnight quote, and triangulates the same effect with the Doug Unger on-the-spot oral-storytelling anecdote ('compared to the stories we'd been submitting, the stories we told that night were, without exception, livelier and more dramatic and more infused with who we really were, richer with our real charms, the way we actually were witty in the world'). He gives the after-care variants: chain multiple 50-word passes with constant characters for a longer story; or use one pass as a starter piece with rising action and relax the constraints to rewrite with unrestricted vocabulary. He names the deeper diagnosis the exercise interrupts: 'we usually walk around with a certain idea of the writer we are in our head. When we sit down to write, that writer is the one we start channeling. In that instant, our brain function changes. We're less open to what the story wants to do, to what that language generator inside of us wants to do. We're working within the narrow range of how we think we should write. This exercise shuts down that way of thinking by keeping it busy with the practicalities of the exercise, which leaves the rest of the mind asking, Well, what else have we got? That is: What other writers might be in here?' He closes with the drunk-dancing-and-filming-it analogy: 'in playback, we might catch a glimpse of something we don't normally attempt, but that we like. And if we like it, we might want to do it on purpose, later.'

sections_html.when: You are a writer whose drafts read as long stretches of exposition, characterization, and atmosphere where the tension never rises. You have read enough advice on rising action to name the deficit (the pot is on the stove and never boils, the beats catalogue rather than escalate, the prose elaborates a state instead of moving past it) but you cannot produce a draft that escalates. The reason is not analytic. When you sit down at the page, you channel the writer you imagine yourself to be, and that writer's habitual mode is the slack one. Audit-shaped revision skills cannot help here because there is nothing yet to audit; you need a draft that escalates so you have a felt sense of what generating-with-escalation feels like. Reach for this when you need to bypass the writer-self-image rather than reason with it, and when you need that bypass to take less than an hour.
sections_html.move:
1. Set a timer for forty-five minutes. The timer is not a goal; it is a fence around the session that prevents you from drifting back into your habitual mode by widening the time available.
  2. Set the constraints. The piece will be exactly 200 words long, not 199 and not 201. You may use only 50 unique words across the whole piece. Plurals of a word count as the same word.
  3. Set up a running list at the bottom of the page. As you write, append each new word to the list with its number. When you hit 50, the list is closed; from that point on you must reuse only words on the list. The list-keeping is not bookkeeping; it is the conscious mind's busywork, the displacement that lets the playful mind run the prose.
  4. Pre-install the read-aloud constraint if you can. Tell yourself, or tell someone in the room, that you will read the piece aloud at the end of the timer. Saunders names the effect directly: announcing the read-aloud "intensifies things" by drawing on the natural performative streak almost every writer has. If you are alone, tell a friend you'll text the piece in 45 minutes. The performance deadline is the second pressure that shuts down the writer-self-image.
  5. Write. Pay no attention to whether what you are writing is the kind of story you usually write. The constraint will pull you toward escalation because exposition is expensive: cataloguing a state requires lots of nouns and verbs not yet on your list, while rising action lets you reuse the words you've already paid for. The mechanism is not "try to escalate"; the mechanism is "the constraint makes elaboration uneconomic and rising action cheap."
