  6. When the timer ends, stop, even mid-sentence. Land at exactly 200 words. The hard stop is part of the design. You are not allowed to edit your way to a satisfying length.
  7. Read the piece aloud to whatever audience you committed to. Notice your own voice. Notice what is funny, what is dramatic, what has rising action. Saunders's empirical claim: in a class of twenty, almost every one of these pieces will have rising action, and some will be funnier and more dramatically shaped than the writer's "real" work.
  8. Write down two answers in plain prose. What was different about this draft compared to my usual drafts? What does the writer who produced this draft do that the writer I usually channel does not do? You are looking for the glimpse Saunders names with the drunk-dancing-and-filming-it analogy: in playback you may catch yourself doing something you do not normally attempt but that you like. Naming what it was is the precondition for replicating it on purpose later.
  9. Decide what to do with the result. Saunders gives two after-care variants: chain multiple 50-word passes (each pass uses a different 50-word set but keeps the same characters) into a longer story, or treat the piece as a starter with rising action already in place and rewrite with the constraints relaxed (use as many "new" words as you want). The starter use is the most useful for a writer with an inert draft already on the desk: the constrained piece supplies the rising-action shape, the relaxed rewrite fills it back in with the writer's full vocabulary. The constraint did the structural work; the relaxed rewrite supplies the breath.
sections_html.signal: You have a 200-word piece written under the constraints. Read aloud, it has rising action where your slack drafts had none. You can name, in two sentences, what the constrained-mode writer did differently from the writer you usually channel. If you ran the after-care, you have either a longer chained piece with constant characters across multiple constraint passes, or a relaxed rewrite that retains the rising-action shape the constrained piece supplied. You have at least one move identified that you intend to attempt on purpose in subsequent unconstrained work.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "What does generating-with-escalation feel like for me?" Note your answer. It will likely be theoretical (a definition of escalation, a quote from a craft book) rather than experiential.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes a felt setting you can recognize on the page when you produce it, named with reference to the constrained piece you just wrote, and if you can finish the sentence "I will attempt move X on purpose in my next draft" with a specific X.
sections_html.failure: Without a constraint exercise that bypasses the writer-self-image, the writer who knows their drafts do not escalate revises only by audit: each pass diagnoses the slack, names the missing escalation, prescribes a fix, and produces another inert draft because the writer who sits down to write the next draft is the same writer who wrote the slack one. The audit-shaped skills (refuse-to-repeat-beats, parameter-table-for-recurrence, causality-not-sequence, ritual-banality-avoidance) all assume the writer can produce escalation when prompted; they help shape it once it is on the page but cannot generate it from a writer whose habitual mode is exposition. Saunders names the deeper trap directly: the writer-self-image is what we channel when we sit down, and channeling that self-image changes our brain function so we are less open to what the story wants to do. The exercise's value is not the 200-word piece itself; the value is the proof that the writer can produce escalation under the right conditions, the catalogue of what those conditions did to the prose, and the named moves that can be carried back into unconstrained work. A writer who does not run a constraint exercise of this kind has only the audit route to escalation, used by the same writer-self-image that produced the deficit.
sections_html.example: Saunders does not produce a finished 200-word piece in the chapter. He gives the protocol and the example sentence "A cow stood in the field" with its running list (a, cow, stood, in, the, field — six words used, forty-four remaining). The chapter's worked instance is at the level of the pattern itself: in his classroom the exercise "almost always" produces rising action, and "for certain students" the results are funnier and more dramatically shaped than the writer's "real" work. He gives a parallel anecdote: as a student at Syracuse, Doug Unger fed up with the cleverness of the workshop's submitted stories announced after a break that each student would tell a story aloud on the spot during the second half. The stories that came out of that nerve-filled break were "without exception, livelier and more dramatic and more infused with who we really were, richer with our real charms, the way we actually were witty in the world." The Unger anecdote is the same mechanism without the word constraint: a forced-performance condition collapses the writer-self-image and surfaces a register the writer's submitted work was not reaching. The escalation exercise is the textual form of the same effect.
sections_html.source: Chapter "Appendix B: An Escalation Exercise," lines 7575-7649. Saunders gives the practice protocol (45-min timer, exactly 200 words, only 50 unique, running list, plurals as same word), the empirical observation that the exercise almost always produces rising action, the why (constraint occupies the conscious mind so a more playful mind takes over), the read-aloud-afterward intensifier with the Johnson hanging quote, the Doug Unger on-the-spot anecdote as parallel evidence, the after-care variants (chain passes with constant characters, or use as starter and relax constraints), the writer-self-image diagnosis ("the writer we are in our head" is what we channel, and the exercise shuts that down by keeping it busy), and the closing drunk-dancing-and-filming-it analogy for what to do with the result (catch a glimpse of something you don't normally attempt; do it on purpose later).
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends energy-not-aspiration. The energy probe identifies that the writer-self-image is producing inert work; this exercise is the procedural form that bypasses that self-image in a single 45-minute session and surfaces the off-license writer who can produce escalation. The constraint shuts down the conscious mode that energy-not-aspiration diagnoses as the source of inanimacy.
  • Contrasts forced-cut-pace-discovery. Both are constraint exercises that bypass the writer-self-image, but they apply pressure in opposite directions. Use escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint when the writer cannot generate rising action; the constraint is generative (50 unique words). Use forced-cut-pace-discovery when slack prose is already on the page and needs the editing basis surfaced; the constraint is subtractive (cut twenty words at a time). The first generates; the second cuts.
  • See also refuse-to-repeat-beats and parameter-table-for-recurrence (audit-shaped escalation skills that assume the writer can produce rising action when prompted; this exercise produces the felt knowledge those skills then shape).

name: follow-the-voice
description: You are starting a story (or stuck mid-draft) and the question on the table is what the story should be "about" — its theme, message, outcome, or topic. You sense that committing to a destination before writing will produce a lecture or one-sided performance, and you have no method for generating a draft that is not goal-directed.

sources.chapter: Appendix C: A Translation Exercise
sources.note: Saunders adds a fourth seed-source to the three named in Afterthought #5 (real person, exaggerated self-tendency, fragment of language from elsewhere): a single sentence the writer has perfected by radical preference alone, with no service to any pre-existing host story. The procedural shape is the same as the existing skill's voice-fragment seed — the seed implies a world, the writer follows the implications, the path narrows by itself — but the seed is a single artifact rather than a continuous voice register. Saunders's framing names the move directly: 'forget that the sentence is part of a Babel story and make it as good as you can. That new sentence, sitting there by itself, instantaneously starts dictating a whole story for itself, i.e., a story in which it would make sense.' The mechanism: 'our style (which we find by radical preference, in pursuit of fun or cool or delight) results in a sentence, and that sentence has the DNA of a story in it.' The worked instance is his own arrived-at sentence about three white wicker chairs that look as if contemplating escape; he reads what world it implies and observes the world-narrowing constraint: 'if a person comes into the frame and sits down in one of those chairs, he has entered a world fraught with desire for escape... he won't be — he can no longer be — just anyone. He's a man who walked into a story that has the idea of escape hovering around it, and he's not entirely free.' This is the same world-narrowing the existing skill names at line 5712 ('the path the story was on had narrowed') as a byproduct of voice-sustaining; here the narrowing happens at the unit of a single sentence rather than across a sustained voice. The fold installs the perfected-sentence-from-radical-preference as a fourth seed-source, with the explicit detachment gesture ('forget the host story') as the procedural addition. The detachment gesture is the new element: where the three voice-fragment sources begin from material the writer has not yet committed to a story, the perfected-sentence source begins from material that may have been intended for a story and then liberated from it. The post-detachment procedure is identical to the existing skill — read the implied world, follow it, let escalation arrive.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #5
sources.note: Saunders names the method 'following the voice' and walks the worked autobiographical case for 'Jon.' A line from a student paper ('Upon perusing this story, I felt myself at a distinct tilt') becomes the seed; he riffs on the voice and within a page has 'Privacy Tarps' and 'Gender Areas' and a confined-teenagers facility he had not set out to write. He frames the principle: 'I had not thought to myself, I'd like to do a little something on the topic of how a group of confined teens deal with their powerful dawning sexuality. I was just riffing on that sentence in the student paper, trying to sound like that sentence.' The world emerges from the voice; 'the path the story was on had narrowed' as a byproduct of voice-sustaining. The named inspiration sources are three: a real person, a tendency in the writer that gets exaggerated (Morse in 'The Falls' as ratcheted-up Saunders monkey-mind), or a fragment of language from elsewhere (the student-paper sentence). The escalation engine is named at lines 5740-5742: 'Having grasped the approximate rules of the voice, the reader will get restless if subjected to a series of sentences that (merely) abide by those rules. So I have to keep finding new ways to make the person sound like himself. The best way to do this is to keep putting new events in front of him, events that are escalatory (new to him), so that he has to find new registers in his voice with which to respond. (If a character, talking along in a certain voice, has never seen a horse before, and I show him one, his voice has to expand, to accommodate the horse.)' The chicken-and-egg of voice-leading-writer / writer-leading-voice is the procedure's reflexive shape. The orientation that licenses it is named at lines 5772-5778: 'voice is not just an embellishment; it's an essential part of the truth' — the honey/milk LI 34321 sentence carries what bare paraphrase ('I really enjoyed that and I think I'm in love') cannot reach; 'love has fallen on a particular, goofy person. Which is what love always falls on'; 'that morning has to fall on a certain mind for it to feel like any kind of real morning.' The generalization at 5754 names 'follow the voice' as one of many methods, the broader category being attention focused on something the writer has strong opinions about (voice, recurring imagery, words on the page, sound, structural minutiae). Saunders frames the failure mode the method prevents at 5754: 'knowing-in-advance... has a tendency to deaden a work and turn it into a lecture or a one-sided performance and drive the reader away.'

sections_html.when:
You are sitting down to start a story and find yourself trying to choose a topic, theme, or message. Or you are mid-draft and have a clear conception of what the story is "about," and the resulting prose is reading like a position paper or a one-sided performance. Or you have read enough writing advice to know that "no worthy problem is solved in the plane of its original conception" and want a method, not a slogan, for proceeding without a destination. Reach for this whenever the next move would otherwise be to decide what the story should mean before letting any prose generate.

The orientation that licenses this skill: voice is not decoration on top of a propositional content that could in principle be paraphrased. It is the part of the prose that carries the felt truth the propositional content does not reach. "I really enjoyed that and I think I'm in love" is recoverable as a sentence; what Jon actually feels in Saunders's honey/milk LI 34321 passage is not. Following the voice is following the truth.

The skill is one specific instance of a broader move. The general shape is: identify the thing about prose you have strong opinions about — voice, recurring imagery, the look of the words on the page, sound, the minutiae of structure — and proceed by attending to that thing, so that you do not know too well in advance what you are doing. Voice is the instance Saunders walks in detail; the steps below are voice-shaped, but a writer whose strong-opinion thing is sound or structure can adapt them.
sections_html.move:
1. Find a seed. Saunders names four sources. (a) A real person whose voice you can hear, sometimes a person you only know by their writing (a student's paper, a colleague's email, a bus driver's announcement). (b) A tendency in yourself, exaggerated past where you would normally let it go (a worry-prone monkey mind ratcheted up; an inarticulateness dial turned past your ordinary self-correction). (c) A fragment of language from elsewhere whose flavor you cannot stop noticing (a sentence, a clause, a misuse). The student-paper line about a reader feeling at a distinct tilt is Saunders's worked instance of the third. (d) A single sentence the writer has perfected by radical preference alone, with no service to any pre-existing host story (Saunders's Appendix C addition; see step 1a). For the first three, the seed is a voice — a continuous register the writer will sustain. For the fourth, the seed is a single artifact whose virtues come from the writer's own taste rather than from imitation; the artifact's implications, not a register's continuation, supply what comes next.

1a. Detachment gesture (perfected-sentence variant). If the sentence you are starting from was originally written for a host story (your own draft, or a translation exercise where the source is some other writer's story), explicitly detach it. Saunders frames the move as a deliberate act: forget the host story, and perfect the sentence by your own preference until it lands as good as you can make it. Then read it as if you had just encountered it. The world it implies — the character it would suit, the mood it presupposes, the constraint it places on what can come next — is what you follow from here.

  1. Commit to imitating it. Try to sound like that sentence. Write a paragraph or a page in the voice. The goal is not to satirize, document, or analyze; the goal is to sustain the voice. Saunders's "Jon" example: he was "just riffing on that sentence in the student paper, trying to sound 'like' that sentence."
  2. Keep almost no other goal in mind. Saunders is direct: "I'm giving almost no thought to anything but sustaining the voice — not thinking of the story's themes or what needs to happen next or any of that." If a thought about "what should happen" surfaces, note it and return to the voice.
  3. Let the world emerge. The voice will force vocabulary, idiom, register, and reference. Those force a setting: who would talk this way, where, about what. Saunders's "Jon" page produced "Privacy Tarps" and "Gender Areas" by lexical pressure alone; a roomful of confined teenagers and a corporate masturbation-encouragement program then arrived because the vocabulary had nowhere else to go. The path narrows by itself.
  4. Apply the escalation engine. As the voice settles into rules the reader can sense, the reader will get restless with sentences that merely abide by those rules. To keep the voice generative, keep putting new events in front of the character — events new to that character — so the voice has to find new registers in response. Saunders's image: "If a character, talking along in a certain voice, has never seen a horse before, and I show him one, his voice has to expand, to accommodate the horse." The horse can be anything the character has not yet had to speak about: a piece of news, an arrival, a small trauma, a confession, a kiss.
  5. Let the voice and the writer co-lead. Saunders's framing is reflexive: the voice leads the writer (forcing world, vocabulary, plot), and the writer leads the voice (turning dials, pushing the inarticulateness or the cynicism or the corporate-poetry up a notch when the voice plateaus). Neither is alone in charge. If you find yourself only following the voice with no input, the work flattens; if you find yourself only steering the voice with no listening, the work freezes.
  6. Beat back conceptions of "outcome" or "message" as they arrive. The story will start trying to be about something. Note this; it is fine. Saunders's "Jon" came to be "about" corporate capitalism, materialism, language, love, marriage, loyalty — none of which he intended at the start. The aboutness is what arrives, not what you aimed at. When you catch yourself trying to engineer the message, return to the voice.
  7. Read what you have. The arc that has appeared is the arc; the world is the world; the meaning is whatever the voice forced into being. Revise from there using iterative-preference-revision.
sections_html.signal: The draft contains a setting, a cast, and a situation that you can describe but did not plan. You can name the seed (the student paper, the exaggerated tendency, the real person) and trace specific elements of the world back to vocabulary or register pressure from that seed. You can describe at least one moment when a new event ("the horse") forced the voice to find a register it had not used before, and you can hear in the prose that the voice did expand. The story has acquired what feels like a topic, but you can confirm — by checking your notes or by asking yourself "did I sit down planning to write about this" — that you did not start with it.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "What is this story going to be about?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer changes from a stated topic ("about loneliness," "about corporate language") to a description of a voice and an account of what that voice has so far forced into being. The topic, if there is one, is the byproduct, not the input.
sections_html.failure:
Without this method, drafting begins from conception: a topic, a theme, a message, an outcome the writer has decided the story will deliver. The writer then tries to engineer prose that gets the reader to that destination. Saunders's diagnosis: this produces a lecture or a one-sided performance and drives the reader away. The reader senses that the writer arrived knowing what to make them feel and has been steering accordingly; the prose registers as advocacy rather than as truth. The writer also freezes, because the gap between current sentence and intended destination is always large enough to demand strategy, and the strategizing is what the writer is doing instead of writing. Saunders's archery image names the corrective: stop aiming at the target, attend to the feeling of the arrow leaving the bow, and let the arrow find its own target.

A second failure mode: writing in voice but treating voice as decoration. The writer composes a paraphrasable proposition ("I really enjoyed that and I think I'm in love") and then dresses it up. The dressing-up reads as ornament; the truth-content of the prose is recoverable as the bare statement. The reader feels nothing the proposition did not already convey. The corrective is the orientation in "When to reach for this": voice carries what propositional content cannot reach, so the voice is not added on top of the truth, the voice is part of the truth.
sections_html.example: Saunders is grading student papers on Kafka's Metamorphosis and reads the line "Upon perusing this story, I felt myself at a distinct tilt." His first reaction is "Pretty bad. But also: kind of great." He starts trying to imitate the voice, looking for a corollary inside himself, and within a few pages has produced a sentence that begins "Back in the time of which I am speaking, due to our Coordinators had mandated us, we had all seen that educational video..." A roomful of horny teenagers is suddenly there, in a facility where horniness has been judged so problematic that the management has produced a corporate masturbation-encouragement video called "It's Yours to Do With What You Like!" The vocabulary keeps forcing the world. A few lines later "Privacy Tarps" and "Gender Areas" appear because the syntax wants them. Once Privacy Tarps and Gender Areas exist, two of the teenagers (Ruthie and Josh) leave their respective Gender Areas to have sex on the very next page; the path the story is on has narrowed. Saunders had at no point sat down thinking "I'd like to do a little something on the topic of how a group of confined teens deal with their powerful dawning sexuality." He had been riffing on a sentence. Later he turns up a dial labeled "Level of Inarticulateness" and tells himself "do part surfer, part corporate wonk." Sentences arrive whose syntax is defective but whose meaning lands oddly efficiently ("Then came the final straw that broke the back of me saying no to my gonads"). The horse, for Jon, is whatever new event arrives next: a chip in the neck loaded with TV commercials indexed by Location Indicators; a girl named Carolyn; a melting kiss; eventually an exit from the facility. Each forces the voice to expand. The story comes to be "about" corporate capitalism, materialism, the deformation of language by commerce, love, marriage, loyalty. None of that was intended at the start. The aboutness was what arrived.
sections_html.source:
Chapter "Afterthought #5," lines 5700-5712 (the student-paper anecdote and the world emerging from the voice), 5722-5732 (the black-box framing of story as state-change device, the archery release metaphor, the imitating-uncles improv as the underlying disposition), 5736-5750 (the named method "following the voice"; the three inspiration sources — real person, exaggerated self-tendency, fragment of language from elsewhere; the no-other-goal stance during drafting; the escalation engine of new events forcing new registers; the "Jon" Inarticulateness dial and "part surfer, part corporate wonk"), 5748-5752 (the chicken-and-egg of voice-leading-writer and writer-leading-voice; the beating-back of conceptions of "outcome" or "message"; "Jon" coming to be "about" things "but none of that was intended at the beginning"), 5754 (the generalization: "follow the voice" is one of many methods, others being recurring imagery, the look of words on the page, sound, structural minutiae), 5762-5780 (the orientation that voice is not embellishment but an essential part of the truth — the honey/milk LI 34321 passage and the morning-walking-out passage), 5790-5806 (the closing return to the black box: the carnival-barker stance, the roller-coaster designer who attends to particular curves and drops without caring about the exact flavor of the reaction).

Chapter "Appendix C: A Translation Exercise," lines 7706-7714 (the fourth seed-source: a single sentence the writer has perfected by radical preference alone, with no service to a host story; the explicit detachment gesture that licenses the procedure; Saunders's named principle that style produces a sentence and the sentence carries the DNA of a story; the worked instance of the contemplating-escape sentence and the world-narrowing constraint it places on what character can come into the frame; the same path-narrowing-by-itself mechanism the existing skill names at line 5712, here triggered at the unit of a single sentence rather than across a sustained voice).
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also iterative-preference-revision. The two are sequential: follow-the-voice generates the draft from a seed; iterative-preference-revision then revises that draft per-decision by P/N reaction. Both refuse the engineer-with-destination stance and rely on the same disposition (acting on the felt impulse, not on theory).
  • See also energy-not-aspiration (the energy criterion that tells you which voice to follow when more than one is available) and belief-by-character-attribution (the move applied to a sincere belief: render it in voice, attribute it to a character, let the story react).

name: forced-cut-pace-discovery
description: You suspect your prose is loose, you cannot articulate your editing sensibility or your natural pace, and you cannot tell what to cut because nothing in particular feels wrong. You need a constraint that forces the question rather than another category-by-category audit.

sources.chapter: Appendix A: A Cutting Exercise
sources.note: Saunders supplies the practice protocol: take a 600-word text, set a five-minute timer, cut twenty words, ask three reflection questions (what did I cut, why, is the piece better or worse), repeat round after round until the piece is half its length. He frames the exercise as discovery of voice through pace, gives the Ideal Body Weight mirror analogy as a model for how successive strictness reveals the right setting, names the threshold at which the cutter loses purchase (the moment you need to know where the piece is going to judge what is wheat and what is chaff) and observes that almost everything cut up to that threshold was cut for other reasons readers should examine in themselves. He names the second mechanism the exercise teaches: cuts open room for the rhythm of a partial sentence to suggest its completion, so that words come 'based not entirely on sense but also on sound,' and the post-cut state lets the writer hear what to put in. The exercise's stated transfer is to one's own work (page 4 of a story currently in progress); students report 'muscle memory' carrying over.

sources.chapter: Appendix C: A Translation Exercise
sources.note: Saunders supplies a sister exercise that operates on the same target — surfacing the writer's editing basis and innate sense of voice — through a different operation: rank-among-finished-alternatives instead of cut-to-quota. The protocol: present multiple published translations of one source sentence (Saunders supplies five English versions of one Babel sentence from 'In the Basement'), rank them best-to-worst without being given a basis, then ask 'on what basis did I just now make that ranking?' The success criterion is named explicitly: 'if you could do the ranking, this proves that you had a preference. Consider that this preference might have (must have) something to do with your innate sense of voice, i.e., that which you are going to be relying on in every sentence of your career.' The two-part exercise structure mirrors Appendix A: a forced operation on someone else's prose (rank the five), then transfer to one's own work (write your own 'translation' of the stipulated content — chairs, light, foliage — and afterward introspect with three named questions: 'what was I honoring? on what was I relying? how did I decide?'). Saunders walks his own iterative micro-revisions on the same sentence as the worked instance: a passive-voice avoidance becomes 'White wicker chairs gleamed in the green,' which he reacts against ('what green are you talking about?'), revises to 'White wicker chairs, tucked into a foliage-thick promenade, gleamed,' reacts again ('hmm, not quite'), and so on. The fold installs the rank-among-versions exercise as a second mode of the existing skill alongside cut-to-quota: same purpose (surface editing basis and voice through forced operation on others' prose), same two-part shape (practice on someone else's, transfer to your own), same terminal introspection (named questions that surface preferences as data), different operation (rank vs. cut). The two modes are complementary — the cut surfaces offenses the writer cuts for, the rank surfaces preferences in arrangement among artifacts where nothing is removable — and Saunders's framing 'a good voice-finding exercise for any aspiring writer' uses the same voice-discovery vocabulary as Appendix A's 'voice is made in revision through cutting.' Saunders's named principle from the Appendix C section after the exercise — 'our style (which we find by radical preference, in pursuit of fun or cool or delight) results in a sentence' — restates the same intensity-not-flavor stance the existing skill installs as its closing failure mode.

sections_html.when:
You are revising prose that reads acceptably but slack. You cannot point at sentences that are wrong, only at a piece that feels long. The category audits (does this earn its place, would the reader supply this, does each element have a function) all return "fine" because you have not internalized the pace those audits should be cutting toward. You also notice that you cannot describe what offends you in prose or what delights you when you protect a phrase. Reach for this when the question you need answered is not "what should I cut here" but "what is my editing sensibility, and what is my natural pace."

The skill has two modes that fire on the same target — surface the writer's editing basis and innate sense of voice — through different operations on someone else's prose. The cut-to-quota mode (the main procedure below) operates by forced compression and surfaces what offends the writer. The rank-among-versions mode (Saunders's Appendix C translation exercise, summarized at the end of the procedure) operates by forced choice among multiple finished alternatives of the same content and surfaces what the writer prefers in arrangement, register, sound, and image-order when nothing is removable. Run the cut mode when the prose you are studying is one piece you can compress; run the rank mode when you have multiple existing translations or versions of the same source content. The two are complementary and frequently fire on adjacent sessions in a writer's voice-finding practice.
sections_html.move:
1. Pick a passage you did not write that you can mark up freely. Saunders supplies one in the appendix; any roughly 600-word passage will do as long as it is not your own. Cutting someone else's prose is easier than cutting your own; the exercise's transfer step depends on starting where the resistance is lower.
  2. Set a timer for five minutes. In that window, cut exactly twenty words. The quota is the point. You are not cutting because something feels wrong; you are cutting because the constraint says so, and you are watching where your hand goes under the constraint.
  3. After each round of twenty, answer three questions in writing. What did I cut. Why did I cut it. Is the piece better or worse than before this round. The second answer is your editing sensibility surfacing: name the offense each cut removed (a stated reaction, a default detail, a clause that restated the obvious, a flourish that drew attention to itself, a word repeated from earlier in the paragraph). The third answer is your taste calibrating: notice the rounds where the piece improves and the round where it begins to lose something.
  4. Repeat. Saunders takes the exercise from 600 words to 300 — half — round by round. Do the same. The point is not to land at a particular length but to traverse the range of strictness, noticing what becomes available to cut in each round and at what point the cutting starts costing the piece.
  5. Notice the threshold. Somewhere in the descent you will hit a round where you cannot tell what to cut next, because the answer depends on where the piece is going. Saunders names this directly: at that point the question of which detail is wheat and which is chaff requires knowing what later pages will need. Mark the round where you hit that threshold and read back: nearly everything you cut up to that point you cut for reasons you can name without knowing the rest of the piece. Those reasons are your editing basis surfacing.
  6. Tally the categories. Write down the kinds of things you cut (reaction-naming, default detail, restated context, throat-clearing, sentence-tail filler, repeated word, flourish, etc.) and the kinds of things you protected against cutting (a particular image, a rhythm, a turn that surprised you). Saunders frames the question explicitly: what, in prose, offends you, and what delights you and inspires you to protect it. The two lists are your editing basis named.
  7. Listen for sound, not just sense, in the late rounds. Cuts often leave a partial sentence whose tightened state suggests how it wants to finish. Read the tightened version aloud. Some words come to mind that complete the sentence on rhythm rather than on logic, and those are usually better than what you started with. Saunders demonstrates with a sentence cut down to its first three words and then completed by what the rhythm of those three words wanted next; the completion introduces a fact the original draft did not have. This is the second product of the exercise: cuts make room not only for less, but for new material that arrives because the surviving rhythm asks for it.
  8. Now run the same procedure on your own work. Pick a page from a story you currently have in progress (Saunders suggests page 4). Set the timer. Cut at the same quota. Saunders notes the difficulty differential — easier on someone else, harder on yourself — and the practical case for the exercise: students report that the muscle memory built on the practice piece carries over.

Rank-among-versions mode (Appendix C translation exercise)

When you have multiple existing translations or versions of the same source content (as is the case with any well-translated foreign-language sentence), run this sister exercise instead of, or in addition to, the cut-to-quota procedure.

a. Assemble three to five existing versions of the same content. Saunders supplies five English translations of one sentence from Babel's "In the Basement" — a description of white wicker chairs in a foliated walk. The versions vary on word choice (foliage vs. verdure vs. leaves), syntax (front-loaded vs. back-loaded), register, image-order (chairs first or paths first), and sound.

b. Rank the versions best-to-worst. Do not look up any criterion. The whole point is that no basis is supplied; you supply it, by way of your taste.

c. After the ranking, write down the answer to one question: "On what basis did I just now make that ranking?" Saunders frames the introspection: since no basis was supplied, the basis you used reveals an innate preference — a preference you will be relying on in every sentence of your career.

d. Transfer to your own work. Saunders supplies the second half: write your own version of the stipulated content (in his case, the elements are chairs, light, paths, foliage). Afterward ask three more named questions: "What was I honoring? On what was I relying? How did I decide?" The questions externalize the same per-decision introspection that the cut mode produces from cut categories. Saunders's worked example shows him iterating in real time, each draft followed by a one-line reaction that surfaces what the previous version was missing.

e. Tag the categories the same way the cut mode does: write down what you preferred in arrangement (image-order, sentence shape, sound) and what you preferred in word choice (register, sensory specificity). The two lists are your editing basis surfacing through choice rather than through cut.
sections_html.signal:
You can produce three artifacts after a session: a marked-up passage cut from its original length to roughly half; a written list of what you cut, with each cut tagged by category in your own words; and a written list of two or three things you protected, with reasons. You can name where the threshold landed for that piece — the round at which you stopped being able to cut without knowing the destination. Run on your own page, the cuts come faster than they did before the exercise, and the resulting page reads at a pace closer to the one you found acceptable on the practice text.

For the rank-among-versions mode, the artifacts are: a written best-to-worst ranking of three to five versions; a written answer to "on what basis did I rank"; a version of your own composed from the stipulated elements; and a written answer to "what was I honoring, on what was I relying, how did I decide." The basis you wrote down should be specific enough that, given a sixth version, you could predict where it would fall in the ranking before reading it carefully.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "What is my editing sensibility?" and "What is my natural pace?" Note your answers. They will likely be vague or empty.

After applying, ask the same questions. The skill landed if the first answer becomes a list of nameable offenses you cut for and protections you held against cutting, and the second answer is a felt setting you can recognize on a page rather than a number you can state.
sections_html.failure: Without a forced-quota exercise, the writer revises only by diagnosis: each cut requires a reason articulated in advance, so cuts are slow and the writer never reaches the strictness setting that would reveal what slack the prose was carrying. Pace stays at first-draft length because the writer has no felt knowledge of what tighter would feel like. Voice stays generic because, as Saunders frames it, voice is made in revision through cutting, not in the first draft where most of us "sing for too long, in ways that actually sound pretty similar to all of those other writers singing." The two routes to a distinctive voice are putting it in or discovering it by erasure, and a writer who has never erased to a quota has only the first route, used without knowledge of how strict the result needs to be. The exercise produces the felt knowledge that makes both routes work.
sections_html.example: Saunders supplies a 600-word DMV passage about a man named Bill, full of explanatory asides, hedged phrasings, and stretches of paranoid interior monologue. The reader is to cut twenty words at a time, asking after each round what was cut, why, and whether the piece improved. He then demonstrates the rhythm-of-completion mechanism on a separate sentence. A loose draft sentence about a college student in a class taught by Professor Vader is cut to a much tighter form; the cut version is described as smarter and more considerate, the writer having sifted through the meandering original and brought forward what he found most vital. He demonstrates the same mechanism on a third sentence: "Sam was this big sort of dumb guy who I knew" cut to "Sam, big and dumb…" and then completed by what the partial sentence's rhythm asked for next. The completion arrives as "could, nevertheless, run." Now Sam is a runner, which the original draft did not yet know, and the new fact entered through the cut. The exercise's second product is named: cutting opens room for material the writer would not have written from a slack draft.
sections_html.source:
Chapter "Appendix A: A Cutting Exercise," lines 7472-7574. Saunders gives the practice protocol (timer, twenty-word quota, three reflection questions, iterate to half), the Ideal Prose Pace framing via the Ideal Body Weight mirror analogy, the threshold observation (the round at which you need to know the piece's destination to keep cutting), the rhythm-of-completion mechanism (the post-cut state lets the writer hear, on sound as much as sense, how to finish a partial sentence), and the transfer step (apply the same procedure to page 4 of a story you have in progress).

Chapter "Appendix C: A Translation Exercise," lines 7664-7714. Saunders supplies the rank-among-versions sister exercise: five English translations of one Babel sentence, the named instruction to rank them best-to-worst, the introspection question that surfaces the basis ("on what basis did I rank"), and the second-half transfer in which the reader composes their own version of the stipulated content (chairs, light, paths, foliage) and afterward answers "what was I honoring, on what was I relying, how did I decide." The two-mode framing — same target, same two-part shape, same terminal introspection, different operation — is the fold's organizing structure.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint. Both are constraint exercises that bypass the writer-self-image, but they apply pressure in opposite directions and address different deficits. Use forced-cut-pace-discovery when the writer has slack prose already on the page and needs to surface what their editing basis cuts for and what their natural pace is; the constraint is subtractive (cut twenty words at a time). Use escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint when the writer cannot generate rising action in the first place; the constraint is generative (200 words, only 50 unique). The first surfaces what to remove; the second surfaces what to put on the page in the first place.
  • See also respect-the-reader-cut (the audit-shaped revision skill the cut mode of this exercise calibrates) and iterative-preference-revision (the per-decision reaction loop that this exercise builds the editing basis for).

name: heart-of-the-story
description: You are evaluating, revising, or critiquing a story and you sense it has bloat, sections that are pleasant but not earning their place, but you cannot say what they are failing against. You have no reference point that lets you ask of any given part whether it belongs.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Saunders names the question 'What is the heart of you, dear story?' and proposes the candy-factory and Club-Story-bouncer images as the procedure for auditing every part against that heart. He demonstrates the procedure on Turgenev by tabulating the eleven pre-contest pages and asking what work each does for the contest.

sources.chapter: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'
sources.note: Saunders runs the heart-finding question on Chekhov's 'Gooseberries' and discovers a recursive structure. The heart of the story is Ivan's anecdote about his brother (the answer to the Seussian question 'Why are you bothering telling me this?'). Within that anecdote, Saunders identifies a further heart: Ivan's impassioned speech about happiness. He names the recursion: 'At the heart of that anecdote (i.e., the heart of the heart of the story) is Ivan's impassioned speech about the nature of happiness.' The retroactive effect: 'because that speech (the heart of the heart of the story) is about the question of whether our urge to be happy is to be indulged or resisted, we feel the story to be a sort of meditation on that question. Ivan's conclusion that there is no happiness and there should be none hangs, retroactively, over the whole story.' The fold adds the recursion to the audit: when the heart is itself a multi-element scene (a speech delivered inside a tale told inside a frame; a contest with a climactic note; a ceremony with a vow inside it), the audit recurses — identify the heart-of-the-heart and run the same procedure for the inner layer. The Seussian formulation 'Why are you bothering telling me this?' is a fresh restatement of the heart-finding question, useful when the candy-factory or Club-Story-bouncer images are not firing.

sections_html.when: You are looking at a draft, your own or someone else's, that contains stretches you cannot defend or attack precisely. The piece moves but feels long, or moves but feels off-center. You can describe what is happening in each section but cannot say which section is the one the others should be serving. Reach for this whenever "I don't know what this scene is for" comes up and you suspect the problem is not the scene but the lack of a center to test the scene against.
sections_html.move:
1. Identify the heart. Ask the story: "What is the heart of you, dear story? Why are you bothering telling me this?" The heart is the central event, scene, performance, or arrival the story exists to deliver. In a contest story, it is the contest. In a wedding ceremony, the exchanging of vows. In a joke, the punchline. Name it as a specific moment in the manuscript, not as a theme.
  2. State the heart in one sentence and locate it in the manuscript by page or line range. The rest of the audit depends on the heart being a fixed reference point.
  3. If the heart is itself a multi-element scene (a tale told inside a frame, a performance with a climactic note, a ceremony with a vow inside it), recurse. Ask the same heart-finding question of the inner scene. Saunders names the recursion: the heart of "Gooseberries" is Ivan's anecdote about his brother; the heart of that anecdote (the heart of the heart of the story) is Ivan's speech about happiness. The recursion stops when the inner question lands on a single moment that cannot be subdivided further. Run the audit at both layers.
  4. Walk through the rest of the story unit by unit (scene, paragraph, descriptive passage, sub-scene). Saunders frames the procedure two ways. The candy factory: every department in the place must be related to making the candy; an office labeled "Center for the Planning of Steve's Wedding" is a sign of inefficiency. Club Story: roam the club as a bouncer, asking each part, "Excuse me, but why do you need to be in here?"
  5. For each part, finish the sentence: "This part is here because it ___ the heart." Acceptable verbs: prepares, sets up the criteria for judging, deepens the stakes of, primes a contrast that the heart will resolve, supplies a panel that the heart needs, foreshadows. Unacceptable verbs: "feels nice next to," "happens to be true," "got into the draft and would not come out."
  6. If a part stalls on this test, do not yet cut it. Mark it as on-trial and ask one more question: is there a non-obvious way it serves the heart? In Saunders's reading, the eleven static pages of pub description in "The Singers" pass on the second look because they build the panel of judges whose reactions later make the unsinging-able contest readable.
  7. Apply the test generously, not mathematically. Saunders is explicit that scrutiny administered too cleanly will produce a story that is too neat. Atmosphere, mood, and texture are valid functions if you can name them as decisions, not defaults.
  8. Parts that fail the second look, fix or cut. The fix is to give them a function pointed at the heart. The cut is the same as cutting them.
sections_html.signal: You can name the heart of the story in one sentence pointing at a specific moment on a specific page. Every other part of the story is annotated with what it does for that heart, in concrete terms (preparing this contrast, supplying this judge, raising this stake). At least one part is marked for cutting or rewriting because, on second look, it cannot be tied to the heart.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I cut any random scene from this story, would I know whether the cut hurt the story?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer flips from "I'd be guessing" to "yes — the question is whether the cut weakens what the heart needs, and I can name what it would weaken."
sections_html.failure: Without a named heart, every part of the story is on equal footing and nothing can be tested against anything. Edits become tinkering. You move sentences, sharpen lines, polish description, but you cannot tell whether a whole section is doing structural work or merely existing pleasantly. The draft accumulates parts that pass the local test ("this is a good scene") and fail the global test (no part of it routes energy to the central event). Saunders names the underlying craft fact: a story has a heart in the same way a Mass has communion or a wedding has the vows, and the rest of the ceremony is felt as beautiful and necessary to the extent that it serves that heart. A story that does not know its heart cannot ask its parts to serve.
sections_html.example: Saunders identifies the heart of "The Singers" as the singing contest, five pages out of nineteen, beginning on what he marks as the contest page. He then walks through the eleven pre-contest pages part by part and tabulates them: a description of the ravine, of the innkeeper, of the innkeeper's wife, of some birds, of the town, of Booby and Blinker, of the pub, of Yashka, of the Wild Gentleman, of the contractor, of a peasant, plus a few brief active sequences. The tabulation makes the imbalance impossible to overlook. He then runs the bouncer-at-Club-Story test on each item: what is it doing for the contest? The character descriptions earn their keep by establishing a graded panel of judges whose reactions during the contest will tell the reader who is winning. The ravine description earns its keep by cueing the binary structure of the story (a town cleft in two, two singers, Yashka vs. the contractor, technique vs. emotion). The bird descriptions earn their keep by setting up the doleful-vs.-spunky contrast that will read forward onto the contestants. Each pre-contest part, once tested against the contest, turns out to be on-purpose. The procedure is the audit, and the audit is what converts the apparent bloat into apparent virtuosity.
sections_html.source:
Chapter "The Heart of the Story," lines 1676-1694 (the question "What is the heart of you, dear story?" and the Mass/coronation/wedding analogy), 1782-1798 (the candy-factory and Club-Story-bouncer images for the audit), 1800-1854 (the worked tabulation of the eleven pre-contest pages).

Chapter "A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'," lines 6047-6055 (the heart-of-the-heart recursion: Ivan's anecdote is the heart of the story; Ivan's speech about happiness is the heart of that anecdote; the Seussian formulation "Why are you bothering telling me this?" as a fresh restatement of the heart-finding question; the retroactive effect: "Ivan's conclusion that there is no happiness and there should be none hangs, retroactively, over the whole story").
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts hollywood-version. Use the heart of the story when auditing parts against a center, when the question is "what is this scene for"; the output is a specific moment on a specific page that the rest must serve. Use the Hollywood version up front, when the working description of "what story this wants to be" needs to be agreed before changes are proposed; the output is a one-sentence shape used as the standard for suggestions. The heart gives "advances the story" a destination; the Hollywood version gives "this is the story of ___" a shape.
  • See also cornfeld-principle (the unit-level test runs against the heart as its destination), holding-up-against-the-heart (the imagery-level audit uses the heart as the comparator), truncation-test (cut the ending to surface what it supplies; cut an internal unit to surface what it does for the heart), and panel-of-judges (when the heart is an unwitnessable event, the panel is the device that adjudicates it for the reader).

name: holding-up-against-the-heart
description: You have noticed an apparently static element in a story (a description of a setting, a small image, a piece of background, a digression) and you suspect it is doing real work, but you cannot say what work, and "good detail" is not an answer you can act on or learn from.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Saunders names the move 'holding up against the heart,' demonstrates it on the ravine and on the rooks/crows/sparrows, and uses the two-trees painting analogy to explain why juxtaposition produces meaning by inference rather than by assertion.

sections_html.when: You are reading or revising and a non-event element in the story keeps drawing your attention. A setting detail, a description of a place, a flash of birds, a piece of weather, a minor digression, an image. It feels like it matters. The story is not telling you why. Reach for this whenever you sense an apparently decorative element is structurally loaded but you cannot articulate the load.
sections_html.move:
1. Identify the heart of the story (the central event the rest serves). If you have not done this, do it first.
  2. Pick the suspect element. The ravine, the birds, the description of the town in its former glory, the small image at the close.
  3. Hold the two side by side in mind. Not literally on the page, in mind: the suspect element on one side, the heart of the story on the other. Saunders calls this "holding up against."
  4. Watch for the immediate, pre-articulate response. Either a feeling arises ("there is a relation here, and it is not random") or a non-feeling arises ("Meaningful Relation Not Found"). Saunders's emphasis: this initial response is the data, prior to articulation. Do not skip it.
  5. If the response is "not random," now articulate. Try to name what relation links the two. Often it will be a binary, a structural pattern, a contrast, or a foreshadowing. The ravine cleaves the town in two; the contest pits two singers against each other; the story is full of binaries.
  6. Once you have one relation, sweep for others. The cleft town, the two singers, the doleful birds vs. the spunky birds, the technical singer vs. the emotional singer, the sparrows that fly up vs. the rooks that stay grounded, the noble narrator vs. the lowly rustics. The story turns out to be organized by the pattern.
  7. If the response is "Meaningful Relation Not Found," the element is a candidate for cutting in your own draft, or evidence of looseness in someone else's, depending on what you are doing.
  8. Trust the immediate response over your articulation. Saunders is explicit that the mind is at its most intelligent in the instant before it explains, and that the articulation is always partial. The instant of recognition is what art is for; the articulation is the receipt.
sections_html.signal: For each suspect element you held up, you can produce a sentence of the form: "Held against the heart, this element produces the feeling that ___, and once articulated, the relation is ___." The relation points at a structural pattern (a binary, a recurrence, a contrast) that the rest of the story turns out to also be organized by, and you can list at least one other element in the story that participates in the same pattern.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, point at the suspect element and ask: "Why is this here?" Note your answer. It will likely be "atmosphere" or "good detail."

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes a specific structural relation (a binary, a foreshadowed contrast, a pattern the heart resolves), and you can name one other place in the story where the same pattern appears.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, you discount apparently static elements as "atmosphere" and miss the level at which a great story does its real organizational work. The story feels mysterious in its rightness. You can feel that the ravine matters but you cannot say how, and so you cannot reverse-engineer the move for your own writing. In your own draft, the inverse failure: you preserve elements because they "felt right" without checking whether they hold up against the heart, and the draft accumulates motifs that point in inconsistent directions. Saunders names the underlying craft fact: a story's controlling sensibility lives in the meaningful relations between its non-event elements and its heart, and these relations are felt before they are explained. A reader who never holds them up never sees them. A writer who never holds them up never makes them.
sections_html.example: Saunders takes the ravine, an apparently static piece of geography described early in "The Singers," and holds it up against the singing contest. The first response is a feeling: there is a relation, and it is not random. He stays in that feeling for a moment, then articulates: a ravine is a thing that splits something into two; a contest is a thing that stages two against each other. The story, once that pattern is noted, reveals itself to be saturated with binaries: town's former glory vs. its present ruin, doleful rooks vs. spunky sparrows, technique vs. emotion, Yashka vs. the contractor, the comfortable narrator vs. the lowly rustics, even the closing call between the two boys across the valley. The ravine, held against the heart, unlocks the story's organization. He runs the same exercise on the burst of birds early in the story, holding them up against the two singers, and finds the contractor in the chirping sparrows and Yashka in the more doleful rooks, with the further complication that the sparrows (like Yashka) are also the ones capable of brief ascension. He notes that the tracking is imperfect ("a wild animal that refuses to get into the box we've made") and treats the imperfection as a feature, not a bug, of the move.
sections_html.source: Chapter "The Heart of the Story," lines 2002-2038. Saunders names the move, gives the two-trees painting analogy for why juxtaposition produces meaning prior to articulation, and demonstrates the procedure on the ravine and the birds.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends heart-of-the-story. The juxtaposition move presupposes the heart already named: the suspect element is held against it, and the felt relation (or its absence) is read out. Once the heart is identified, this is the audit move for static or apparently decorative elements that the unit-level Cornfeld test would miss.
  • See also two-version-comparison (the comparator-method shape; here the comparator is the heart rather than a counterfactual) and track-non-normative-excesses (the TICHN cart often supplies the suspect elements held up against the heart).

name: hollywood-version
description: You are starting a workshop critique, an editorial pass, or a self-revision and the room is about to start suggesting changes before anyone has agreed on what the story is actually trying to be. Suggestions made before that agreement will pull in incompatible directions.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders names this 'the Hollywood version' and demonstrates the artillery-fire sequence of refinements on the in-progress reading of 'In the Cart.'

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: A second worked instance of the move: the Hollywood version of 'The Singers' is 'Two men have a singing contest in a Russian pub. One wins, one loses.' Saunders uses it here to set up a related but distinct question — the heart of the story — clarifying that the Hollywood version is the one-sentence shape, not the central event the rest of the story serves.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Saunders extends the move from the writer's planning to the reader's experience: the Hollywood version of 'Master and Man' is 'We're driving out to buy that land,' and Tolstoy announces this organizing principle inside the story so digressions are read against an established through-line. Saunders gives the mansion-tour analogy — a tour announced as 'we're going to start in the attic and work our way down' lets the visitor enjoy a side detour into a secret room without losing orientation. The skill operationalizes for the writer's planning what this passage shows operating on the reader: a one-sentence shape stated up front buys digression-tolerance later.

sections_html.when: You are about to discuss a draft in a workshop, give an editor's notes, or revise your own piece. The conversation is heading toward suggestions: cut this, expand this, change that. You can feel that the people in the room hold different ideas of what story this is. Reach for this before any change is proposed.
sections_html.move:
1. Demand a one-sentence summary. Ask, "What story does this story appear to want to be?" Get someone to answer in one or two sentences. Saunders calls this the Hollywood version.
  2. Treat the first answer as a ranging shot, not a verdict. It is the opening artillery round, deliberately broad.
  3. Iterate. Add one piece of specificity per pass. Each pass narrows by ruling something out. "An unhappy woman is going somewhere in a cart" becomes "A schoolteacher, unhappy because she's been teaching too long, is on her way home." Each addition is a fact the next pass cannot contradict.
  4. Stop iterating when an additional specification no longer changes what the story would do next. At that point you have the Hollywood version.
  5. Use the result as the standard against which suggestions are measured. A proposed change either serves the story this story appears to want to be, or it proposes a different story (which is fine, but must be named as such).
sections_html.signal: The room (or you, alone) can finish the sentence "This is the story of ___" with a single specific clause that everyone present accepts as the working description. Subsequent suggestions can be evaluated by asking whether they serve that description or replace it.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "If I made the changes I am about to propose, would I know whether the story is still the same story?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer flips from "I'd be tinkering" to "I'd be either keeping it the same story (and these changes serve that) or proposing it become a different story (and these changes substitute one for another) — and I can tell which."
sections_html.failure: Without this move, critique converges on a Frankenstein draft. Each reader, holding a different unspoken Hollywood version in their head, suggests changes that would serve their version. The writer, trying to honor everyone, ends up with a draft that is consistent with no story at all. Saunders frames the rule plainly: you would not start altering a complicated machine in your yard before you had some idea of its intended function. The same applies to a draft.
sections_html.example: In the chapter, Saunders walks through the Hollywood version of "In the Cart" as it would emerge in his own classroom, mid-read at the appearance of Hanov. The first ranging shot is "An unhappy woman is going somewhere in a cart." Successive passes add "schoolteacher," "unhappy because she's been teaching too long," "alone in the world, teaching only out of necessity," and finally the meet-cute formulation "Lonely woman encounters possible lover." Each pass rules something out: she is not unhappy because of love, not unhappy because of grief, and so on. By the time the sentence is "Lonely woman encounters possible lover," everyone in the room can predict what kinds of next moves would be on-story (some development between Marya and Hanov) and which would be off-story (a spaceship abducts Semyon).
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 569-595. Saunders names this exercise as how he opens workshop critique at Syracuse, gives the artillery-fire metaphor (an initial shot, then adjustments for precision), and shows the iterations applied live to "In the Cart" up through the meet-cute version.
sections_html.related_skills: • Contrasts heart-of-the-story. Use the Hollywood version up front, when the working description of "what story this wants to be" needs to be agreed before changes are proposed; the output is a one-sentence shape used as the standard for suggestions. Use the heart of the story when auditing parts against a center, when the question is "what is this scene for"; the output is a specific moment on a specific page that the rest must serve. Both name the story's center but at different stages and for different decisions.

name: improper-narrative-emphasis
description: You are drafting a scene with an impossible or unrenderable element where direct description would force a choice between two incompatible specifications, or would break the spell by making the impossibility too literal. The literal mechanics of the strange thing, if rendered, would expose the strange thing as nonsense. Direct description is the wrong tool.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders names the technique 'improper narrative emphasis' (citing Vinokur) and demonstrates it on the moment Kovalyov first sees his runaway nose. The translations Saunders gives both deflect the literal question: 'Imagine the horror and at the same time the amazement of Kovalyov when he recognized that it was his own nose!' Saunders walks through the question the prose sidesteps: 'What's the it? Who's the him? What, exactly, does Kovalyov see? Does the Nose have... a nose? Does the Nose have a face?' He names the mechanic: 'the confident statement that he recognized that it was his own nose causes our mind to insert, here, a Nose, a Platonic Nose, one that we couldn't, if pressed, draw.' On the second sighting, the deflection is to costume and rank: 'we learn only that he is wearing a gold-embroidered uniform with a big stand-up collar and doeskin breeches and that from his plumed hat one could infer that he held the rank of a state councillor. The focus is on the nose's change in status, not its new form or size.' Saunders frames the principle: 'improper narrative emphasis being used to sidestep a question that, had these events really happened, and had we been there, we would have been able to answer.' He generalizes to language itself: 'language can appear to say more than it has a right to say; we can form it into sentences that are not in relationship with what actually is or even what could be.' The desk-with-fate-muffins example demonstrates the mechanic on a sentence with no truth-claim — the words still produce something 'sort of' in the reader's mind.

sections_html.when: You are drafting a scene that contains something that cannot be coherently described. A nose that is also a man in a uniform. An object with a face whose face is unspecifiable. A creature whose anatomy would, if pinned down, contradict itself. A magical event whose mechanics would, if rendered, expose the spell. You can feel that any direct description forces a choice between two specifications that cannot both be true, or would render the impossibility too literally and lose the spell. Reach for this whenever the question "what does this thing actually look like" would, if answered, ruin the scene.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the question you are going to sidestep. Be explicit: "Does the Nose have a face?" "Does the talking glove have a mouth?" "How does the disembodied voice produce sound?" Write the question down so you know what you are deflecting.
  2. Pick a different attribute to emphasize. Saunders's Gogol-procedure: instead of describing the Nose's form, describe its rank, costume, station, mood, or social bearing. The replacement attribute should be one that the impossibility could plausibly possess, and that carries weight in the world of the story (in Gogol's case, social rank is the load-bearing currency of the world, so the Nose's uniform tells the reader everything the world cares about).
  3. Compose the sentence with confident syntax. The prose must not flag its own deflection. Saunders's note: "the confident statement that 'he recognized that it was his own nose' causes our mind to insert, here, a Platonic Nose, one that we couldn't, if pressed, draw." Confidence in the syntax is what licenses the reader's mind to fill in the unrenderable.
  4. Use the pronoun the deflection requires. "It" or "him" rather than a full referent. When Kovalyov sees his nose, the prose says "he recognized that it was his own nose" — the "it" both is and is not a person. The grammatical hedge is the deflection.
  5. Trust the reader to fill in a Platonic version. The reader will supply a mental image at the level of confidence the syntax establishes. They will not draw it; they will accept it. Saunders's principle: "Yet there it is, rushing into a house."
  6. Test by asking the deflected question after writing the scene. If a careful reader could answer the literal question ("the Nose has a face that looks like X") from your prose, the deflection is incomplete. The prose should leave the literal question structurally unanswerable.
  7. Hold the deflection across the scene. If you deflect once and then later, in the same scene, render the literal mechanics, you have broken the spell. The deflection must be sustained until the scene closes.
sections_html.signal: A reader cannot, on rereading, answer the literal mechanical question about the strange thing. They have a sense of the thing — they would say "yes, I know what it looked like" — but cannot draw it. The scene reads as confident and clear at the surface, while the impossible element remains structurally undescribable. You can name, in revision, the specific question you sidestepped, and you can locate the sentence that performed the deflection.
sections_html.probe:
Before. For the impossible element in your scene, write down the literal question: "Does X have a face? How does Y make sound? What is the geometry of Z?" Try to answer the question from your current prose. If the prose answers it, you are still rendering the literal mechanics.

After. Same prompt. The prose does not answer the question. The prose has emphasized something else (rank, mood, costume, social station, action) that carries the scene's weight. The reader will accept the impossibility because the syntax around it is confident, but the prose does not commit to a specification that could be falsified.
sections_html.failure: Two opposite failures. (1) Over-specification: the writer renders the impossible thing literally, the reader notices the mechanics cannot work, and the spell breaks. The Nose has a face; the face has eyes; whose eyes? whose voice? — the questions stack and the reader becomes a forensic reader. (2) Under-specification: the writer hedges with "somehow" and "in some way" and the prose loses confidence, signaling its own embarrassment. Improper narrative emphasis is the third path: confident syntax around a deliberate deflection. The literal question is never raised because the emphasis is elsewhere; the impossibility is accepted because the prose has not asked the reader to inspect it.
sections_html.example: Kovalyov sees his nose for the first time as a separate being. The prose: "Imagine the horror and at the same time the amazement of Kovalyov when he recognized that it was his own nose!" The reader's mind supplies a Nose without being told what the Nose looks like. The pronoun "it" both is and is not a person. The next sighting: the Nose comes out of a building "wearing a gold-embroidered uniform with a big stand-up collar and doeskin breeches" with a "plumed hat" indicating "the rank of a state councillor." The emphasis is on costume and rank — both load-bearing in this world — and the literal questions (face? size? proportions?) are never asked. The Nose elsewhere is described as having "slightly knitted brows," indicating a face, but the face is not specified. The reader holds, simultaneously, "a nose with a face" and "a nose without a face," and accepts both, because the prose has never paused on the question.
sections_html.source: George Saunders, A Swim in a Pond in the Rain, "The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'," lines 5346-5367.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends skaz-narrator. The deflection-via-replacement-attribute is one of the skaz narrator's signature techniques, but it is documented separately because it operates as a sentence-level mechanic and can fire even when the broader narrator-character is not skaz. The skaz voice supplies the social-rank vocabulary the deflection latches onto; the deflection is the specific composition technique at the moment the impossible thing must be on the page.
  • See also psychological-physics-of-strangeness (the deflection works because the replacement attribute — rank, costume, social bearing — is load-bearing in the world's psychological physics; meaning lands in the reactions, not in the impossible event's literal form) and multiple-superimposed-weirdness (the narrator-tier is where this technique is most useful, since deflection from a transparent narrator reads as evasion).

name: increasing-specification
description: You are drafting a story and a character feels generic, or the plot won't move because the character is too vaguely drawn for any particular thing to need to happen to them. The character is "an unhappy woman" or "a lonely teacher" without yet being a particular unhappy woman or a particular lonely teacher.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders names this 'increased specification,' performs it on Marya in the opening pages, demonstrates the cross-painting variant on Hanov, and tracks Marya's continued specification at the teahouse.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Sharpens the trigger for contest-shaped stories: A and B must each stand for something distinct so the outcome can carry meaning. Saunders gives 'specificity makes character' as the principle and shows the contractor becoming a particular person ('a shredder, in guitar terms') only once the singing is rendered specifically.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #2
sources.note: Sharpens the trigger to the sentence level: a sentence that states a verdict on a character ('Bob was an asshole') instead of rendering the moment that produced the verdict. Saunders walks through successive rounds of asking 'why' until 'Bob was an asshole' becomes 'Bob snapped impatiently at the young barista, who reminded him of his dead wife, Marie, who he missed so much, especially now, at Christmas, which had always been her favorite time of year,' and notes the side effect: the revised writer becomes 'more alert to Bob,' a moral-ethical deepening produced as a byproduct of asking the procedural question 'which particular person, doing which particular thing, why?' The trigger generalizes the skill from character introduction to any sentence that names a verdict instead of rendering the situation that earns it.

sources.chapter: A Pattern Story: Thoughts on 'The Darling'
sources.note: Names the principle the skill operationalizes: 'in specificity lies nascent plot.' Saunders walks a third worked sentence-level instance, this time on a situation rather than a verdict: 'Some guy is sitting in a random room thinking nothing at all and this other guy walks in' becomes 'An angry racist is sitting in a room, thinking of how unfairly he's been treated all his life, and a person of another race walks in' becomes 'An angry white racist named Mel, who has cancer, is sitting in the examination room, thinking of how unfairly he's been treated all his life, when his doctor, a slightly egotistical Pakistani American, Dr. Bukhari, walks in, bearing bad news for Mel but glowing, in spite of that, with happiness because he's just won a major award.' Saunders's added framing: specificity does not just decorate, it produces the conditions under which meaningful action has to occur. 'I don't know what's going to happen next in that last, more specific, room but I'm pretty sure something will.' The chapter then immediately demonstrates the principle at work in 'The Darling': Olenka's specific trait (when she loves someone, she becomes that person) is what gives the story its plot — once the trait is specified, kill off her first love and present her with a second, and meaningful action follows. The trigger generalizes from character/sentence to whole-story: when your story is not moving, the missing thing is often not plot but specification.

sections_html.when: You are drafting and your character feels like a type rather than a person. Or you have a draft where the protagonist exists but the plot keeps stalling, with no particular event seeming necessary. Reach for this whenever you sense that a character has not yet specified far enough for meaningful action to be available to them.
sections_html.move:
1. Ask the question: "Which particular person is this, anyway?"
  2. Answer with a fact. The fact should rule something out. "She is unhappy" does not rule out enough; "she is unhappy because of the monotony of her life, not because of grief or scorned love" rules out two clichés and points the path narrower.
  3. Repeat. Add another fact, then another, each one ruling out more possibilities. How long has she been in this state? Why? What does she do when she encounters relief? What can she imagine for her future? Each fact closes off a generic alternative.
  4. After two or three such facts, notice the pull. A specified character generates pressure for particular events. A boy afraid of water makes a body of water feel imminent. A woman who can imagine no other future makes a disturbance to her future feel due.
  5. For characters of a recognizable type, cross-paint: set up the type, then add a contradictory trait that makes the person three-dimensional. The kindly examiner who is also a clueless drunk; the warmhearted gentleman who is also a bumbler. The contradiction prevents the reader from filing the character away as "the rich landowner."
  6. Continue specifying as the story progresses. A character is not finished when introduced. New events are opportunities to specify further, even at the cost of complicating the reader's view (the noble teacher who is also complicit in her own diminishment).
sections_html.signal: You can write the character's defining facts as a short list, and reading the list, you can predict the kinds of events the story is now ripe for. The list also rules out the obvious clichéd version of this character. The plot, which had been stuck, suggests its own next move.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, write your character in one sentence and ask: "What needs to happen to this person?" Note your answer. If it is "I don't know" or a generic event, the character is underspecified.
