After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes a specific event or class of event that the character's facts make pressure for, and you can name which facts produced that pressure.
sections_html.failure: Without this work, your character remains a placeholder. You write "an unhappy woman" and try to put her into a plot, but no particular plot fits, because nothing about her selects for one. You may then try to fix this by adding more dramatic events, but events without specification feel arbitrary; the character is not the kind of person these things would happen to. Or, equally common: you produce a flat type the reader files away on first encounter and stops attending to. The story has nothing out of which to make its meaning, in Saunders's phrase, because before the character was specified, there was nothing in the story that wanted to happen.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks through Chekhov's specification of Marya in the opening pages of "In the Cart." She is unhappy. (Type: an unhappy person.) She is unhappy not because of grief or scorned love but because of the monotony of her life. (Cliché ruled out: tragic backstory.) She has been teaching for thirteen years and has done this trip countless times. (Cliché ruled out: she is new to her plight.) She can imagine no other future. (The trap is internal as well as external.) Saunders names the effect: as a particular person gets made, the potential for meaningful action increases. He then demonstrates the cross-painting variant on Hanov: the warm gentleman is set up, then complicated by alcoholism, by bumbling, by the absurdity of giving globes to a school in the middle of nowhere; the result is a person we wonder about rather than have neatly in our pocket.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 415-434 (the question "which particular person is this," and the introduction of "meaningful action" as the better word for plot), 627-633 (cross-painting Hanov), and 914-924 (Marya's specification continuing at the teahouse, into complicity with her own situation).
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also matrix-of-facts (both produce belief through factual specificity, but at different scopes: increasing-specification specifies the character or situation until plot becomes available, while matrix-of-facts specifies the prose register that any individual sentence sits in). When a draft has under-specified characters, both skills usually fire together.
  • See also causality-not-sequence (specification supplies the why-this-not-that for each beat; once a character is specified, causality between beats becomes available).

name: is-it-helping
description: You have encountered a craft model, writing rule, theory, or workshop slogan and are about to argue about whether it is true, defend it, or accept it on authority. You are at risk of becoming a connoisseur of theory instead of a writer.

sources.chapter: We Begin
We End
sources.note: Saunders bookends the trial-balloon disposition with two extensions. (1) The mentor-period framing (7307-7319): 'the mentor strongly expresses his view as if it is the only and entirely correct view. The student pretends to accept that position: takes the teacher on faith (tries on his aesthetic principles for size, surrenders to his approach), to see if there might be something in it. At the end of the mentoring period (now), the student snaps out of it, disavows the teacher's view, which is starting to feel like a set of bad-fitting clothes anyway, and goes back to her own way of thinking. But maybe along the way she's picked up a few things. These are things she likely knew all along, of which the teacher simply reminded her.' The fold installs the bounded-period stance as the trial-balloon procedure's natural temporal shape — the student commits to the teacher's view for the duration of the teaching, then steps out and audits what stayed useful. The success criterion sharpens: the model that stays is one that names something the writer already had energy in, and the student's resistance to a model is the student's artistic will asserting itself ('that feeling of disagreeing with me was your artistic will asserting itself... That resistance is something to note and be glad of and honor'). (2) The teacher-side complement, the 'according to George' parenthesis (7449-7455): 'At the beginning of the first class of the semester I always ask my students to imagine themselves putting a parenthesis in front of everything I'm about to say (i.e., over the course of the semester) and preceding that parenthesis with the words 'According to George.' At the end of the last class, I ask them to close the parenthesis and add the phrase 'Well, anyway, that was all according to George.'' This is the install-in-advance form of the same disposition: the teacher pre-installs the trial-balloon stance in the student by announcing explicitly that the teaching is one perspective and by ritually closing the bracket at the end. The fold adds the teacher-side ritual as a paired procedure: when delivering craft teaching (workshop, mentorship, book), open the parenthesis at the start, close it at the end, and the student arrives at the keep-what-helps disposition by the bracket itself rather than having to independently discover it.

sections_html.when: Someone has just offered you a craft model: a rule about openings, a theory of plot, a structural template, an aphorism about voice, a workshop slogan. You feel pulled to argue for or against it, or to adopt it because the source is trusted. Reach for this before you commit to or reject the model on grounds of correctness.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the model in one sentence so its claim is on the table.
  2. Name the state of mind you are actually trying to reach: the mode in which you could write the kind of story you want to write. This is "the moon."
  3. Ask the criterion question: "Is this model helping me get there?" Not: "Is it correct?" Not: "Is it widely accepted?" Not: "Did a great writer say it?"
  4. Try the model on a real piece of work, your own or someone else's, for a bounded period. Watch what happens to the work and to the state of mind you bring to the page.
  5. Decide: keep it (it is helping), discard it (it is not), or hold it loosely (sometimes helps, sometimes does not, and you can name the conditions of each).
  6. If you keep it, say what it is helping you do. If you discard it, do so without arguing it is wrong, only that it is not helping you here.
sections_html.signal: For every craft model you currently hold, you can finish the sentence "This is helping me ___" with a specific effect on a specific kind of writing problem. Models that fail this test are gone, downgraded, or scoped to the situation in which they actually work. You no longer hold any model purely because it is correct or prestigious.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, list the craft rules and theories you currently hold and feel attached to. For each, ask: "Why am I holding this?" Note any that you cannot connect to a concrete effect on your writing.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if any model has moved from the "true and prestigious" column to the "actively helping me with X" column or has been let go.
sections_html.failure: Without this test, craft theory accumulates into a museum. You collect models because they are taught, because authoritative writers endorse them, or because they sound right, and you defend them as correct. Two hazards follow. First, you spend energy on theological arguments about which model is true rather than on the work the model was supposed to serve. Second, you mistake the finger for the moon: you confuse fluency with the model for the state of mind that produced the stories you actually love. The signal you are in this failure mode is that your craft talk gets richer while your drafts do not.
sections_html.example: Saunders frames every model he will offer in the rest of the book as a "rhetorical trial balloon" and invokes the Buddhist image of "a finger pointing at the moon": the finger is not the moon, and a teaching is to be judged not by whether it is right but by whether it directs you toward enlightenment, here defined as the state of mind from which you could write a story like the ones you love. He explicitly tells the reader: if a model appeals to you, use it; if not, discard it. The criterion is "Is it helping?"
sections_html.source: Chapter "We Begin," lines 188-278 (especially 271). Saunders sets up the disposition with which to receive every craft model in the book that follows, including the ones he himself proposes, by naming the goal as a state of mind and the test as utility, not correctness.
sections_html.related_skills: (none) — this skill names the disposition with which to receive every other craft model in the library, including the rest of Saunders. It does not extend, refine, or contrast with any of them; it sits above them as the test each is held to.

name: iterative-preference-revision
description: You are revising a draft and keep trying to make the big decisions top-down (what is this story about, what is its structure, what does it mean) or feel paralyzed because the first draft is not good enough to start work on. Your revision sessions feel like decisions, not reactions.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #2
sources.note: Saunders names the procedure (P/N meter on the forehead, instant fix at each needle-drop, hundreds of passes), gives the apartment-redecoration analogy for why iteration produces self-revealing work, and frames the disposition with the Stan-and-model-railroad opening (art begins in 'the instant of intuitive preference') and the apartment-and-seed-crystal middle. He summarizes it as 'intuition plus iteration.'

sources.chapter: Afterthought #3
sources.note: Saunders deepens the disposition that makes the iterative method work and sharpens the framing of what the method is for. Three additions worth folding in. (1) The index-cards-on-a-date image (lines 3199-3209): a writer who arrives with a fixed plan and merely executes it is the conversational equivalent of a date consulting index cards, leaving the reader out of the process; the reaction the prose deserves is moment-to-moment responsiveness, not execution. He triangulates the same point with Donald Barthelme ('The writer is one who, embarking upon a task, does not know what to do'), Gerald Stern (if you set out to write a poem about two dogs and produce a poem about two dogs, that's all you produced), and his paraphrase of Einstein ('No worthy problem is ever solved in the plane of its original conception'). The skill's anti-top-down stance is the same; the metaphor is sharper. (2) The first-time-reader self-impersonation framing (lines 3233-3235): the goal is 'not so much trying to perfectly imagine another person reading my story, but to imitate myself reading it, if I were reading it for the first time.' The reader-side effect is named: 'this manifests to the reader as evidence of care. (We might say that a first-time reader is able to intuit the many less-cared-for versions of a sentence behind the one the writer let stand.)' This is the procedure the existing skill operationalizes, with a clearer specification of who the imagined reader is (the writer themselves, freshly) and what the reader feels (intuited care via implied alternatives). (3) The N-drop diagnosis (line 3239): when the needle drops, this 'tells us that, in the instant of the writing, we weren't there.' Revision is the chance to be there now. The locality framing follows: 'I find I can't complete that sentence, not in any general way. And I don't need to. To be a writer, I only need to read a specific sentence of mine, in its particular context, on a given day, pencil in hand, changing the sentence as it occurs to me to do so.' Then do it again.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #5
sources.note: Saunders gives three sharper metaphors for the same anti-top-down, attend-to-the-local stance the skill operationalizes. (1) The black box (5722): 'We might imagine a story as a room-sized black box. The writer's goal is to have the reader go into that box in one state of mind and come out in another. What happens in there has to be thrilling and non-trivial.' The flavor of the thrill is what the writer is writing to find out, not what they decided in advance. (2) The archery release (5730): 'one way to produce the thrill is to stop aiming at the target and concentrate on the feeling of the arrow leaving the bow. In this alternate version of archery, the arrow then sails off in a certain direction and keeps adjusting course, and wherever it lands…that's the target.' This names the orientation under which the locality of decision is licensed: the writer cannot aim at a destination because the destination has not yet been chosen by the prose itself. (3) The carnival-barker / engineer contrast and the roller-coaster designer (5790-5806): 'If I think of a story as something that has to convey a certain message, as a train that has to pull into a certain station at a certain time, and myself as the stressed-out engineer trying to make that happen — it's too much. I freeze up and no fun is had. But if I imagine myself as a sort of genial carnival barker, trying to usher you into my magical black box, the workings of which even I don't fully understand — that, I can do.' The roller-coaster designer's procedure ('focuses her attention on particular curves and drops. She knows, technically, how to maximize the intensity at those places. She doesn't know or care much about the exact flavor of your reaction. What she really wants is for you to get your ass kicked at those places, then step out of that little car at the end so dazed and changed and happy that, for a few seconds, you've got nothing much to say') is the existing skill's procedure restated as apparatus design: attend to the local intensity-maximizing decision, do not try to specify the reader's reaction in advance. The fold adds these metaphors as additional motivation for the anti-top-down stance and clarifies the criterion for a successful pass: in every decision, proceed by 'Does this delight me?' and trust that since you delighted, there will be some delight for the reader too. The freeze-up failure mode is named in its train-engineer form for the failure-mode section.

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: Saunders extends the locality-of-taste stance to whole-story scale. Tolstoy's stated agenda for the story (praise cheerful obedience, render Alyosha as a Christian holy fool) is the top-down theory; the prose's reaction (the swerve out of Alyosha's head at the climactic moment, the omission that produces the Rubin's-vase ambiguity) is the per-decision locality. Saunders names the macro-version explicitly via Tolstoy's own essay on Chekhov's 'The Darling': 'in writing that story, Chekhov started out to mock a certain sort of woman... Like Balaam in the Book of Numbers, Tolstoy suggests, Chekhov went up the mountain to curse Olenka, but when he began to speak, the poet blessed what he had come to curse.' Saunders then names the inverse on Alyosha: 'Tolstoy did the inverse: he unintentionally cursed what he set out to bless. That is, though he admires Alyosha in theory, and wrote the story to praise his acquiescence and cheerful obedience, the story itself, touched by Tolstoy's honest artistry, can't quite bring itself to deliver that message unambiguously.' The mechanism is the same as the existing skill's per-decision locality, scaled up: the writer's stated agenda is set aside not by an explicit rejection but by the felt impulse at each decision point that contradicts the agenda. Saunders's account of the swerve (lines 7149-7167): 'the (mere) scribbling man, guided by that suprapersonal wisdom (that is, by a lifetime of artistic practice), didn't decide not to go into Alyosha's head; he just didn't feel like it.' The fold adds the cursed-what-he-set-out-to-bless framing as a worked instance at whole-story scale: trust the per-decision impulse even when (especially when) it contradicts the stated agenda, because the cumulative effect of the contradicting impulses is what produces the artifact richer than the agenda would have permitted. The stated-agenda failure mode joins the existing failure-mode section: a writer who overrides per-decision impulses to enforce a top-down agenda produces an artifact whose richness has been suppressed by their own intention.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #6
sources.note: Saunders supplies the most compressed account in the book of what the iterative method is for, what its terminal product looks like, what its single criterion is, what its stopping rule is, and how to visualize it. Five elements fold in. (1) The terminal product (6433-6443): a 'highly organized system' is one in which 'the causation is more pronounced and intentional. The elements seem to have been more precisely selected. Things escalate decisively; everything is to purpose.' These four axes — pronounced causation, precise selection, decisive escalation, everything to purpose — are what the cumulative iterative method produces; the writer does not aim at them, they emerge. (2) The central claim (6453-6471): the prime virtues of fiction (be specific, use details, escalate, show don't tell) are agreed on by every writer who can name them, but 'the difference between a great writer and a good one (or a good one and a bad one) is in the quality of the instantaneous decisions she makes as she works. A line pops into her head. She deletes a phrase. She cuts this section. She inverts the order of two words that have been sitting there in her text for months.' The reduction Saunders offers as a description of all writing: 'we read a line, have a reaction to it, trust (accept) that reaction, and do something in response, instantaneously, by intuition. That's it. Over and over.' The whole game is named twice: '(1) becoming convinced that there is a voice inside you that really, really knows what it likes, and (2) getting better at hearing that voice and acting on its behalf.' (3) The stopping rule (6479-6481): 'we do this repetitively until a decision has been reached, and the way we know a decision has been reached is that that place stops changing.' The criterion is not 'this is good' but 'the impulse to change has ceased across passes.' (4) The editing-basis framing (6493-6497): 'Let's fancily call your system of preferences your editing basis. Well, get to it. Apply your editing basis. You'll find out everything there is to know about yourself as a writer by what you feel like doing to that swath of prose.' The Ann/Marie example shows three different writers' editing-basis revisions of the same opening passage, each producing a recognizably different highly organized system. The line that consolidates the procedure: 'it's not the flavor of your taste that matters; it's the intensity with which you apply your taste that will cause the resulting work of art to feel highly organized.' The corollary stance: 'no defense or rationalization needed.' (5) The mixing-board metaphor (6507-6519): a story is a mixing board with thousands of fader switches; the writer has to do two things, 'create the fader switch in the first place (stumble on the moment when Mike thinks of appealing to his father and leave that moment in) and then set that switch.' The metaphor is then adjusted: the board is not for music but to 'flood a room with beautiful, high-intensity light. Every adjustment of any one of those thousands of fader switches will subtly change the quality and intensity of light in that room. In a perfect story, every switch is set just right, and the light in there couldn't get any brighter or more beautiful.' Revision is 'microtuning the adjustment of the existing fader switches and introducing new ones as needed.' The closing motif (6517-6519): 'How emphatically can you like what you like? How long are you willing to work on something, to ensure that every bit of it gets infused with some trace of your radical preference? The choosing, the choosing, that's all we've got.' The fold installs the four-axis terminal product as the success criterion at the skill's tail, the editing-basis no-defense-or-rationalization stance as the licensing disposition, the stopping rule as the practical exit condition for any one place, and the mixing-board image as the visualization that makes the per-decision view legible across the life of a draft.

sources.chapter: Appendix C: A Translation Exercise
sources.note: Saunders walks his own iterative micro-revisions on a single sentence as the worked instance of the exercise's second half (write your own version of stipulated content). The procedure is identical to the existing skill at sentence scope: he reacts to each draft with a one-line response and adjusts. A passive-voice avoidance becomes 'were gleaming' becomes 'gleamed.' 'White wicker chairs gleamed' becomes 'White wicker chairs gleamed in the green'; he reacts ('uh, what green are you talking about?') and revises to 'White wicker chairs, tucked into a foliage-thick promenade, gleamed'; he reacts again ('hmm, not quite'); and the path eventually arrives at 'Three white wicker chairs faced vaguely but not exactly off in the same direction, looking distractedly off into the jungle that surrounded them, as if considering escape.' Each step is the same P/N drop and per-decision adjustment the existing skill names, performed live. The fold installs Saunders's three named introspection questions — 'what was I honoring? on what was I relying? how did I decide?' — as the externalized form of the existing skill's per-decision reaction-and-adjust loop, useful at the end of a session to surface what the cumulative micro-decisions were drawing on. Saunders's framing of the exercise's terminal product — 'all that's left is to seek the best arrangement of those elements per your taste' — restates the editing-basis stance from Afterthought #6 (apply your taste, no defense or rationalization needed), with 'taste' as 'voice' here. The chapter also reaffirms the skill's central claim by negative example: at no point does Saunders consult a theory of the good sentence; he reads each draft, drops a one-line reaction, adjusts. The exercise is iterative-preference-revision on a single sentence, performed in front of the reader as a teaching demonstration.

sources.chapter: We End
sources.note: Saunders closes the book by sharpening the disposition that licenses the iterative method, the long-horizon framing of what the cumulative practice is for, and the reader-side signal of a single preference-driven phrase. Three elements fold in. (1) The maniacal-preference disposition (7311): 'The road to that 'iconic space' I mentioned at the outset (that place where you do the work that only you can do) is marked by moments of strong, even maniacal preference. (And defiance, orneriness, fascination, indefensible obsession.) What Randall Jarrell said about stories holds true for the writers of stories: they 'don't want to know, don't want to care, they just want to do as they please.'' The fold installs maniacal-preference / defiance / orneriness / indefensible-obsession as the authorized register the iterative method runs on, and names 'iconic space' as the long-horizon terminal product the cumulative practice produces — the place where the writer does the work only they can do, reached not by deciding-in-advance but by the accumulated effect of acting on per-decision preference for many years. The Jarrell quote restates the existing skill's anti-top-down stance — the writer 'doesn't want to know' general theory, only to act on the felt impulse. (2) The Frost-WORK story and the worry-as-avoidance diagnosis (7335-7345): 'go forth and do what you please. It really is true: doing what you please (i.e., what pleases you), with energy, will lead you to everything — to your particular obsessions and the ways in which you'll indulge them, to your particular challenges and the forms in which they'll convert into beauty, to your particular obstructions and your highly individualized obstruction breakers. We can't know what our writing problems will be until we write our way into them, and then we can only write our way out'; Frost's 'Young man, don't worry: WORK!'; 'We can decide only so much. The big questions have to be answered by hours at the desk. So much of the worrying we do is a way of avoiding work, which only delays the (work-enabled) solution.' The fold adds the worry-as-work-avoidance failure mode — when the writer finds themselves deliberating a craft question (about form, structure, voice, theme) away from the page, the deliberation is delaying the work-enabled solution, because the resolution is produced by the cumulative micro-decisions of drafting, not by abstract reasoning. The macro-version of the existing skill's locality stance, applied to whole-craft questions rather than to individual sentences. The footnote at line 7466 — Frost may have actually said 'Young man, don't work: WORRY!' — is acknowledged as Saunders's self-aware reversal-joke and has no procedural form. (3) The hose-tussle as reader-side signal (7397-7413): 'The hose is lying out there, pale green in the hot sun. It is not, by the way, 'greenish-looking, on this hot, sunny day.' It's 'pale green in the hot sun.' Why? Because it's better that way. Why is it better? Because I prefer it'; 'we can disagree about that. Above, as you read 'pale green in the hot sun,' did you see that hose? Your reading energy did something at that phrase. Were you in or out? With me or against me? Compelled slightly forward or held slightly back? By that little tussle, you know I'm here. And I know you're there. That phrase is a little corridor connecting us, giving us a fragment of the world over which to tussle, i.e., connect.' The fold adds the reader-side success criterion at sentence scope: a preference-driven phrase produces a slight tussle in the reader (in/out, with/against, forward/back), neither neutral pass-through nor smooth agreement, and the tussle itself is the corridor of connection. This is the registration moment of the existing skill's 'evidence of care' framing from Afterthought #3 — first-time reader intuiting 'the many less-cared-for versions of a sentence behind the one the writer let stand.' Hose-as-corridor names the reader's specific moment of registering the writer's preference work; the writer's confidence that the procedure works comes from the reader's tussle being detectable, however small.

sections_html.when: You are revising your own draft. You catch yourself trying to settle big questions before touching the page (what is this story about, what should the structure be, what is the meaning). Or you are stuck because the first draft seems too poor to bother revising. Or your sessions feel like deliberation: each change requires an argument, and arguments are exhausting. Reach for this whenever the work is sitting there waiting and you are sitting there thinking instead of reacting.
sections_html.move:
1. Print the draft, or open it fresh. Read it from the top, trying to read as a first-time reader would, without hope and without despair, in Saunders's phrase. The first-time reader is yourself, freshly. Saunders sharpens this in Afterthought #3: the work is "not so much trying to perfectly imagine another person reading my story, but to imitate myself reading it, if I were reading it for the first time." The whole skill is the ability to lapse into a reasonable impersonation of yourself encountering the prose as new.
  2. Imagine a meter on your forehead: P on one side for positive, N on the other. As you read, watch which way the needle points. The reading is what generates the data, not the analysis.
  3. When the needle drops into N, stop and admit it. Do not argue with the reaction. The reaction is the data. Saunders's diagnosis of an N-drop, in Afterthought #3: "this tells us that, in the instant of the writing, we weren't there." The writer was on autopilot, executing rather than perceiving. Revision is the chance to be there now.
  4. Wait the brief moment for the fix to present itself. A cut, a rearrangement, a swapped word, a moved sentence. The fix usually arrives as an impulse, with at most a quiet internal yes, not as a deliberation. Saunders's image: it is like the model-railroad hobbyist who turns a plastic figure a quarter-turn because something in him just liked it better that way.
  5. Mark the change. Continue reading until the next N drop, fix it, continue. Aim for a single full pass per session, three or four passes per writing day, dozens or hundreds of passes over the life of the draft.
  6. Treat each change as reversible. The next pass can undo it. This is what permits the impulse to be acted on without negotiation: nothing committed today cannot be revisited tomorrow. Saunders's stopping rule for any one place, named in Afterthought #6: a decision has been reached "when that place stops changing." The criterion is operational, not evaluative — you are not waiting for "this is good" but for the cessation of impulse to change across passes. Until then, leave it open; once it stops moving, move on.
  7. Trust block-level signals as they emerge. When a passage finally reads through with no needle drops, mark it as undeniable. Once two or three blocks are undeniable, listen for what they say to each other: what order they want, which ones can be cut so that two others abut, which ones cause the reader to ask which question.
  8. Do not pre-plan the meaning. The big thematic decisions are produced by the accreted micro-decisions, not the other way around. When you sense the story trying to be about something, note it and return to the meter; the theme will be carried by the line-level work whether you name it or not. The same anti-top-down stance applies to general theory: refuse to ask "what makes a good sentence?" in the abstract. Saunders's framing in Afterthought #3: "I find I can't complete that sentence, not in any general way. And I don't need to. To be a writer, I only need to read a specific sentence of mine, in its particular context, on a given day, pencil in hand, changing the sentence as it occurs to me to do so. Then do that again, over and over, until I'm pleased."
  9. Repeat across days, weeks, months. The story turns slowly, like a cruise ship, by way of thousands of small adjustments. Saunders's mixing-board image, in Afterthought #6, makes the across-the-life-of-the-draft picture legible: a story is a mixing board with thousands of fader switches; revision is microtuning each switch, with two distinct moves available at each pass — set an existing switch (decide among the versions of a moment that is already on the page) and create a new switch (notice that a moment in the prose is in fact a decision point, and leave the moment in so it can later be set). The four-axis description of what the cumulative process produces — pronounced causation, precisely selected elements, decisive escalation, everything to purpose — names the terminal product, the highly organized system, that the iterative method is for.
  1. Apply your taste without defense. Saunders's framing in Afterthought #6: name your accumulated preferences your "editing basis" and apply it to whatever prose is in front of you, "no defense or rationalization needed." What makes the work feel highly organized is not the flavor of your taste but the intensity with which you apply it. Two writers with different editing bases will produce two different highly organized systems from the same opening passage; both can be right. The work to refuse is the demand for a justification before each change; the impulse is the data, and the cumulative effect of acting on it is the procedure's product.
sections_html.signal: You can describe a recent revision session in concrete reaction terms: where the needle dropped, what fix you made, where it dropped next. You did not deliberate any single change for more than a few seconds. The session produced specific line-level changes, not a list of intended-but-deferred decisions. At least one block has crossed into undeniable, and you can name what it now does for the story that it was not doing before.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of your current revision session: "Am I changing the page, or am I deciding what to change?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer flips from "deciding" to "reacting and changing," and you can name three line-level changes the session produced, each tied to a place where you felt the needle drop.
sections_html.failure:
Without this method, revision becomes architecture: you sit above the draft trying to settle what the story is, what it means, what its structure should be, and you delay the work of changing the page until those decisions feel earned. They never feel earned, because the decisions that would actually settle them are made line by line by the writer who has been reacting to the prose for a long time. So the draft sits, polished only at the level you can articulate, with the deeper micro-level work undone. Or you suffer the inverse failure, refusing to revise because the first draft is bad, not yet seeing that the first draft is not the work, only the substrate the work happens to. Saunders is direct about the alternative: a piece written this way, like a seed crystal, starts small and devoid of intention and grows into something neither writer nor reader could have planned, and is recognizably the writer's because of what they did to any old text by way of the iterative method, not because of what they decided in advance.

A second failure mode the method prevents, sharpened in Afterthought #3: arriving at the page with a fixed plan and merely executing it. Saunders's image is the anxious dater consulting index cards ("7:00 p.m. Inquire re childhood memories"); the dater's plan substitutes for moment-to-moment responsiveness, and the date feels the substitution as disengagement. A draft executed against a plan reads the same way: a lecture or a data dump, not a story. The reader registers care or its absence as a felt difference, intuiting (in Saunders's framing) "the many less-cared-for versions of a sentence behind the one the writer let stand." The iterative method is what produces evidence of care, because each sentence visibly stands as the survivor of many considered alternatives rather than as the default.

A third failure mode, named in Afterthought #5: the writer who treats the story as a vehicle that must arrive at a known destination becomes, in Saunders's image, the stressed engineer trying to bring a train into a particular station at a particular time. The freeze-up is not a personality failure; it is a structural consequence of the destination-as-known stance. The anti-top-down corrective has three Saunders metaphors that motivate the same procedure. The black box: a story is a room-sized box the reader enters in one state and leaves in another, where the writer does not need to know the exact flavor of the thrill in advance because the writer is writing to find it out. The archery release: stop aiming at the target and concentrate on the feeling of the arrow leaving the bow; the arrow finds its target by adjusting course. The carnival barker and the roller-coaster designer: usher the reader into the apparatus, focus on the particular curves and drops you can technically intensify, and let the dazed-and-changed reaction at the end be whatever it is. The unifying procedure across the three metaphors is the same as the iterative method: attend to the local decision, ask of each decision "does this delight me," and trust that the delight you registered will, by way of accumulated micro-decisions, produce a state-change in the reader that you did not have to specify in advance.
sections_html.example: Saunders opens the Afterthought with Stan, a hobbyist arranging a model-railroad town. Stan places a hobo under a bridge, notices the figure is gazing back toward town, then turns a tiny plastic woman in a Victorian window so she is gazing toward the bridge. He has not exactly decided to turn her: it occurred to him to do so, in a split second, with no language attached, except a quiet yes. Saunders names the moment as the instant of intuitive preference and says all art begins there. The same impulse, scaled up, is the P/N meter at work over hundreds of drafts. He extends the picture with the apartment-redecoration analogy: a room he decorates for you in one day will reflect only one of the people you are, while a room you redecorate one swap per day for two years will reflect hundreds of you, all contributing, and will end up more yours than either of you could have planned. That is what iteration does to a draft.
sections_html.source:
Chapter "Afterthought #2," lines 2183-2189 (Stan and the railroad; "all art begins in that instant of intuitive preference"), 2199-2203 (the P/N meter on the forehead; instantaneous fix per needle-drop; lather/rinse/repeat across hundreds of drafts), 2213-2217 (block-level "undeniability"; blocks start telling you their order), 2229-2237 (felt-not-decided; "intuition plus iteration"), 2247-2253 (apartment redecoration), 2261-2267 (seed crystal; "you don't need an idea to start a story, you just need a sentence").

Chapter "Afterthought #3," lines 3197-3209 (the index-cards-on-a-date image, Barthelme on the writer who does not know what to do, Stern on the two-dogs-fucking poem, the Einstein paraphrase that no worthy problem is solved in the plane of its original conception), 3225-3227 ("What makes my reader keep reading?"), 3233-3235 (impersonating yourself reading the prose for the first time; "evidence of care"; the first-time reader intuiting "the many less-cared-for versions of a sentence behind the one the writer let stand"), 3239 ("when we're reading along and something deflects our needle into the N zone, this tells us that, in the instant of the writing, we weren't there"), 3243-3245 (the locality framing: a specific sentence, in its particular context, on a given day, pencil in hand, then do that again).

Chapter "Afterthought #5," lines 5722-5732 (the black-box framing of story as state-change device whose exact thrill the writer does not have to know in advance; the archery release metaphor: stop aiming at the target and concentrate on the feeling of the arrow leaving the bow), 5790-5806 (the carnival barker / stressed engineer contrast; the roller-coaster designer who attends to particular curves and drops without specifying the exact flavor of the reader's reaction; the closing criterion: in every decision, proceed by "Does this delight me?", and since you delighted, there should be some delight for the reader too).

Chapter "Afterthought #6," lines 6433-6443 (the four-axis description of the highly organized system as the terminal product of the iterative method: pronounced causation, precisely selected elements, decisive escalation, everything to purpose), 6453-6471 (the central claim that the difference between writers is the quality of the instantaneous decisions they make as they work, and the reduction of all writing to "we read a line, have a reaction to it, trust that reaction, and do something in response, instantaneously, by intuition"), 6479-6481 (the stopping rule: a decision has been reached when the place stops changing across passes), 6493-6497 (the editing-basis framing: name your system of preferences, apply it without defense or rationalization, and trust that intensity not flavor is what produces a highly organized system), 6507-6519 (the mixing-board metaphor with thousands of fader switches; the two-move framing — create the switch by leaving the decision-point in, set the switch by deciding among versions; the metaphor adjusted to a board that floods a room with light; the closing motif "the choosing, the choosing, that's all we've got").

Chapter "Appendix C: A Translation Exercise," lines 7686-7704 (Saunders walks his own iterative micro-revisions on a single sentence as a live teaching demonstration of the existing skill at sentence scope: each draft followed by a one-line P/N reaction, then a per-decision adjustment, with no consultation of theory at any step; the introspection questions "what was I honoring? on what was I relying? how did I decide?" are the externalized form of the per-decision reaction-and-adjust loop; the framing "all that's left is to seek the best arrangement of those elements per your taste" restates the editing-basis stance from Afterthought #6 with "taste" as "voice").
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also follow-the-voice. The two skills are sequential: follow-the-voice generates the draft from a seed, with no top-down conception; iterative-preference-revision then revises that draft per-decision by P/N reaction. Both refuse the engineer-with-destination stance Saunders flags as the freeze-up failure mode; the first applies it to drafting, the second to revision.
  • See also forced-cut-pace-discovery and escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint (constraint exercises that surface the writer's editing basis and a felt sense of escalation, which the iterative method then has something to act on) and respect-the-reader-cut (one of the reactions that drops the P/N needle is "the reader could supply this," and the cut is the corresponding fix).

name: matrix-of-facts
description: You are drafting prose and want the reader to accept characterization, judgment, or interior states without being asked to take the writer's word for it, or you are revising prose whose elevated, poetic, or aphoristic register is producing skepticism rather than belief. The reader is being told things instead of shown them, and the telling is being noticed.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Saunders observes that Tolstoy's prose is 'almost entirely facts' and that even subjective judgments and characters' interior thoughts are 'rendered as objectively and precisely as Nikita's crossing of the yard.' The Nikita-bridle and samovar-flick passages are paraphrased to show how a basic action ('woman carries samovar to table') is embroidered with concrete factual details (apron flick, carried with effort, set down with a thud, raised before set down). Saunders names the criterion 'a fact draws us in' and proposes the color-coding exercise: mark facts in one color, authorial opinions in another, and notice the page is nearly all the first. The dented-red-car parking-lot example surfaces the underlying mechanic: factual statements produce involuntary buy-in. Subjective claims that arrive 'in a matrix of facts' get accepted in the same spirit as 'the bridle is leather and tasseled.'

sections_html.when: You are drafting or revising prose and want the reader to believe something about a character (that he is cheerful, that she is honestly convinced, that the marriage has gone cold, that the host means it) without writing a sentence that asks for that belief on the writer's authority. Or you have a passage of prose that reads as elevated, poetic, philosophical, or aphoristic, and you can feel that the reader is reading it skeptically: the prose has slipped from showing into telling, and the telling is being noticed. Reach for this whenever a sentence is asking the reader for trust the prose has not yet earned.
sections_html.move:
1. On the page in front of you, do the color-coding exercise. Mark in one color every sentence (or clause) that reports a concrete fact: an action, a gesture, an object, a sense impression, a quoted speech, a measurable detail. Mark in another color every sentence that asserts a judgment, a feeling, an opinion, a generality, an aphorism, or any interior state described abstractly.
  2. Read the proportions. If the page is mostly the second color, you have a register problem: the reader is being asked to accept assertions on your authority. Aim for a page that is mostly the first color, where any second-color sentence sits in a matrix of factual ones.
  3. For each second-color sentence you want to keep, rewrite it into the syntax of the first color. The form is short, plain, declarative, free of qualifier and metaphor. Saunders's example: Tolstoy does not write "Vasili was a hypocrite who deceived himself"; he writes "honestly convinced that he was Nikita's benefactor" — a fact about Vasili's mind, in the same factual idiom as "the bridle is leather and tasseled."
  4. For each scene's basic action ("woman carries samovar to table"), embroider with concrete factual additions that make the same action more particular: the apron flick at the boiling top, the effort of the carry, the lift before the set-down, the thud. Each addition is a fact, not a judgment. The cumulative effect is a more particular world without a more abstract narrator.
  5. When a character's interior must be reported, render the thought "succinctly and precisely, using simple objective sentences that seem factual in their syntax and modesty of assertion." A thought becomes another fact about the character, observable in the same way the bridle is observable. Avoid the lyrical interiority that announces itself as poetry.
sections_html.signal: The reader's belief in your characterizations no longer depends on the reader's trust in your authority. You can point to any subjective claim on the page (he is cheerful, she is honestly convinced, the host means it) and show the matrix of factual sentences around it that produced the involuntary buy-in. A reader stopped at any sentence cannot easily tell whether the sentence reports an external fact or an internal state, because the syntax is the same.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, pick a paragraph and ask: "How many sentences here would a stranger, reading this paragraph cold, classify as 'the writer said so' versus 'the world said so'?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the count of "world said so" sentences has gone up and the few "writer said so" sentences left have been recast in the same factual syntax as the world-sentences around them.
sections_html.failure: Without this register, prose drifts into one of two failure modes. The first is the editorialized version: the narrator names emotions and judgments directly (he was furious, she was a hypocrite, the situation was unbearable), and the reader, sensing the prose is asserting rather than showing, holds it at arm's length. The second is the lyrical version: prose reaches for elevated metaphor or aphorism to do the work of characterization (his rage was the rage of a thwarted king), and the reader, sensing the writer's hand on the wheel, stops believing in the world. Both modes break the involuntary buy-in that comes from factual statement. Saunders's diagnosis: the page becomes a referendum on the writer rather than an encounter with the world the writer is reporting from.
sections_html.example: Saunders contrasts a generic version, "The maid carried the samovar to the table," with Tolstoy's version, "After flicking with her apron the top of the samovar which was now boiling over, she carried it with an effort to the table, raised it, and set it down with a thud." Every addition is a concrete factual detail: the apron flick, the boiling top, the effort, the lift before the set-down, the thud. None is a judgment about the maid. Yet by the time the sentence ends Saunders, as reader, is supplying details he was never given (her red cheeks, the sweat-stained underarms, the strand of hair) — produced by his reading mind in response to facts, not in response to assertion. The same procedure applies to interior facts: Vasili is "honestly convinced that he was Nikita's benefactor," not described as a self-deceiving hypocrite. The hypocrisy is felt by the reader because the omniscient narrator has, a moment earlier, factually reported that Vasili routinely cheats Nikita; the gap between the two factual reports does the work an aphoristic sentence would have ruined.
sections_html.source: Chapter "And Yet They Drove On," lines 4181-4213. Saunders catalogues Tolstoy's factual idiom across three early-story passages, names the criterion "a fact draws us in," proposes the color-coding diagnostic, and gives the dented-red-car parking-lot example for why factual sentences produce involuntary buy-in.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also increasing-specification (both produce belief through factual specificity: matrix-of-facts is the sentence-register, increasing-specification is the character/situation render). The two often fire together when a draft drifts into authorial assertion.
  • See also mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts (when interior states must be reported, render them in the same factual idiom as exteriors) and transformation-by-redirected-energy (the climactic action is rendered as concrete factual sentences without interior commentary).

name: mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts
description: You are drafting or revising a scene at the center of which is a morally loaded exchange (a hypocrisy, a transaction with mixed motives, a lie passed in conversation, an unstated grievance). The prose currently shows only one of public-speech, private-thought, or objective-narration, leaving the reader unable to feel where each character stands relative to the truth.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Saunders walks two worked instances. (1) The Vasili-Nikita exchange about wages: five vantage shifts in three paragraphs — the omniscient narrator declares the objective truth that Vasili cheats Nikita, Vasili lies in dialogue ('we deal straightforwardly'), Vasili thinks (revealing he is 'honestly convinced he is Nikita's benefactor'), Nikita responds in dialogue with his own spin, Nikita thinks (and 'is quite aware' he is being cheated). (2) The Grishkino departure: five perspectives across four paragraphs — Vasili's thoughts, the aged host's mind, Petrushka's mind, Nikita's mind, then the omniscient narrator's QED that 'no one to hinder the departing travelers.' Saunders names the technique 'going from mind to mind' and identifies the disposition that powers it: 'a confidence... that he has an inner Vasili, an inner aged host, an inner Petrushka, an inner Nikita.' The magician analogy frames how the audience reads the result: a passable version of God, not actual omniscience.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders sharpens the procedure for the case where the two characters' private contents are unrelated to each other and to the surface conversation. He gives Doug Unger's two-cartoon-bubbles model: 'When two people are talking, each has a cartoon bubble overhead, full of his or her private hopes and projections and fears and preexisting worries and so on. Person A talks, Person B listens, waiting to respond, but as what Person A is saying passes into Person B's cartoon bubble, it gets mangled.' The worked example: B's bubble is full of guilt over forgetting her mother's birthday after her brother's chiding text; A's bubble is full of anxiety about an upcoming speech. A says 'I have to give a speech next week.' B, thinking of her brother's text, replies 'People can be so harsh.' A hears 'It's true, you'll probably blow it,' and frowns. B reads the frown as confirmation that she is the kind of jerk who forgets her mother's birthday. The added procedure: before composing the dialogue, prepopulate each character's bubble with private content unrelated to the other's, then write the dialogue so each speaker's words pass through the other's bubble and arrive distorted. The distortion IS the meaning. The Hollywood-agent-and-farmer story (5316-5322) is the compressed worked instance: the agent's bubble is preloaded with class anxiety and divorce; the farmer's bubble is unloaded ('Need a lift?'); the agent shouts 'Fuck you!' through his own filter. Saunders names the engine: 'we each have an energetic and unique skaz loop running in our heads, one we believe in fully... Skaz-Headed Person #1 steps outside, where he encounters Skaz-Headed Person #2. Both, seeing themselves as the center of the universe, thinking highly of themselves, immediately slightly misunderstand everything.' The fold sharpens the trigger: when the morally loaded exchange's load comes from each character bringing unrelated private content rather than from one character lying to another about a shared subject, the bubble-preload step is the preparation that makes the vantage shifts land.

sources.external: James Wood, How Fiction Works (2008), sections on 'Free Indirect Style' and 'Narrating'
sources.note: Names and formally treats the technique that does the interior-rendering work inside Saunders's rotation. When the narrator drops into a character's mind and renders thought in the character's idiom while keeping third-person grammar (Vasili 'honestly convinced that he was Nikita's benefactor'), that move has a name (free indirect style) and a tradition (Flaubert, Austen, Joyce). Wood's treatment supplies the formal vocabulary for the in-the-mind half of the rotation, which Saunders performs but does not theorize. Useful for a reader who wants to recognize and tune the interior-vantage register independently of the rotation pattern.

sections_html.when: You are working on a scene whose meaning depends on the gap between what people say and what they know. A landlord telling a peasant they "deal straightforwardly." A mother praising her daughter's husband while privately appalled. A doctor delivering bad news with practiced kindness. A defendant maintaining innocence to a jury. Reach for this whenever a scene's moral weight lives in the difference between a character's public stance and private knowledge, and your prose currently shows only one of them.
sections_html.move:
1. List the characters present in the morally loaded exchange. Two is enough; more works the same way.
  2. For the exchange, name the objective truth — what the omniscient narrator could state outright if asked. (Vasili routinely cheats Nikita.) The truth must be statable factually, not as a judgment.
  3. For each character, draft three short renderings: their public stance (what they say in dialogue), their private stance (what they think, in the same factual register the rest of the prose uses), and how the omniscient narrator describes their position from outside. You will not necessarily use all three for every character, but you will need to know all three before you choose what to show.
  4. Compose the scene by rotating among these vantages. Saunders's worked Tolstoy passages cycle through five shifts in three to four paragraphs: objective truth, character A's speech, character A's thought, character B's speech, character B's thought, and a closing objective frame. The order is not fixed; the rotation is.
  5. When you go into a character's mind, render the thought as a factual report (matrix-of-facts), not a confession or soliloquy. The reader must feel they are observing a thought from outside, not being lectured by an interior monologue.
  6. Hold the disposition that makes this work: you have an inner version of every person you are writing. Not a special one for each, but the same one — yours, in their situation. The thoughts you ascribe to them are thoughts you would have, were you initiator, host, kid, servant. This is what the magician-and-saw analogy is about: a passable version of God, sustained for the duration of the scene.
sections_html.signal: The reader can name, after the scene, the gap between what each character said and what each character privately knew. The reader does not feel the writer judging anyone, and yet the moral architecture of the exchange is unmistakable. The hypocrisy, the resignation, the small dishonesty are all visible without anyone being labeled.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, pick a morally loaded exchange you have drafted and ask: "Could a reader, reading this scene cold, tell which character is closer to the truth and which is spinning?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer changes from "the reader has to take my word for it (or guess from tone)" to "the reader sees the gap because the rotation has shown them speech-vs-thought for both characters and an objective frame around the exchange."
sections_html.failure: Without this rotation, morally loaded scenes fail in one of two predictable ways. The single-mind version: the scene stays inside one character's head, and the reader, having access to that character's interpretation only, cannot tell whether they are being lied to. The dialogue-only version: the scene shows only what is said, and the reader, lacking interior access, must read tone and gesture for clues to private knowledge — a brittle method that fails as soon as the characters are practiced liars. Either way, the scene reduces to the writer asserting (through narrative position) that someone is the hypocrite or the truth-teller. Saunders's diagnosis: what reads as a writer's saintly compassion is actually a technical rotation among vantages, sustained by the writer's confidence that they are inside every person on the page.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks the Vasili-Nikita wage exchange across three paragraphs. The omniscient narrator opens by stating an objective fact: Vasili routinely cheats Nikita. Vasili then says aloud to Nikita, "We deal straightforwardly. You serve me and I don't neglect you" — a public stance the reader, freshly informed, recognizes as untrue. The next sentences move into Vasili's mind, where he is "honestly convinced that he was Nikita's benefactor." This is critical: a "bwa-ha-ha" version of Vasili (knowingly lying to an idiotic servant) would be a different character; the un-self-aware hypocrite is what the rotation produces. Nikita then answers in dialogue, with his own spin ("I serve you and take as much pains as I would for my own father"), which the reader doubts in the same way they doubted Vasili. Finally Nikita's thoughts confirm he is "quite aware" he is being cheated and resigned to taking what he can get. Five shifts, three paragraphs. The reader leaves the exchange knowing the moral architecture without anyone having labeled it.
sections_html.source:
Chapter "And Yet They Drove On," lines 4253-4293. Saunders walks the Vasili-Nikita wage exchange and the Grishkino departure as two worked instances of mind-to-mind movement, names the disposition that powers the technique (a confidence that the writer has an inner version of every character), and gives the magician-and-saw analogy for how the reader receives the result as a passable version of God.

James Wood, How Fiction Works (2008), sections on "Narrating" and "Free Indirect Style." Wood gives the formal name and lineage for the interior-rendering half of the rotation. When the prose enters Vasili's mind and reports him "honestly convinced that he was Nikita's benefactor," the technique is free indirect style, and Wood's treatment (Flaubert, Austen, Joyce) supplies the vocabulary and historical context Saunders does not theorize. Useful for a reader who wants to recognize and tune the in-the-mind register on its own, separate from the rotation pattern.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also matrix-of-facts (interior thought is rendered in the same factual idiom as exteriors, so the rotation does not feel like a shift in register). The two together produce the felt belief in moral architecture without the writer asserting it.
  • See also convert-moral-critique-to-technical (a moral critique of point-of-view often surfaces as inequitable narration: one character was granted a vantage another was denied; the parallel-moments audit is the corrective).

name: multiple-superimposed-weirdness
description: You are drafting or revising a story whose initial strange premise was strong but whose middle pages now feel like they are restating the strangeness rather than compounding it. The opening was vivid; the next pages are tonally similar but not earning new ground. The strangeness is sitting at one tier when it could be at four.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders names the move 'Multiple Superimposed Weirdness Syndrome' and enumerates the tiers in 'The Nose' explicitly. Initial weirdness: a nose appears in a loaf of bread. Second-level weirdness: the couple reacts irrationally to the nose's presence. Third-level weirdness: because they reacted irrationally, they make an odd plan in response (ditch the nose). Fourth-level weirdness: Ivan executes the plan badly because the world he finds out there is inflected with a slight, ornery hostility toward him (a constant flow of acquaintances, a street thick with policemen). Fifth-level weirdness: how the story is being told (the narrator is off too). Each tier adds weirdness in a different category from the prior tier rather than restating the prior tier in louder form.

sections_html.when: You have a story with a strong strange premise. The opening pages set it up well. The middle pages feel oddly flat. Each new beat is in the same key as the premise rather than building on it. You sense the story is repeating its strangeness rather than compounding it, and the reader is starting to settle into the tone instead of being pulled deeper. Reach for this whenever a strange-premise story plateaus and you suspect the fix is not "more of the same kind of weird" but "weird in a category that has not been weird yet."
sections_html.move:
1. List the tiers of strangeness already in your draft. Saunders's enumeration on "The Nose" gives the categories: (1) the premise itself (an impossible event); (2) the reactions of the characters present (their responses are irrational by our standards); (3) the plans they form in response (the plans are odd in light of the situation); (4) the execution of those plans in the world (the world itself behaves with a slight ornery resistance); (5) the narration (the voice telling the story is itself off). Other categories are possible, but these five are a working palette.
  2. For each tier, write one sentence describing what kind of weirdness lives there in your current draft. If a tier is empty (the world your characters move through behaves perfectly normally; the narrator is a transparent camera), name it as a tier with potential.
  3. Identify which tier the draft is currently leaning on. If the strangeness is concentrated in one or two tiers and the others are empty, that is the diagnosis. The draft is restating, not compounding.
  4. Ratchet up one of the empty tiers. Saunders's procedure on the bridge scene is exemplary: Ivan, having accepted the irrational accusation (tier 2) and formed the odd plan (tier 3), is unable to execute the plan because the world keeps providing acquaintances and policemen — that is the world itself becoming weird (tier 4). The world's resistance is a fresh category; it does not restate the prior tiers, it compounds them.
  5. Make each tier add weirdness in a different category. The rule is not "more weird" but "weird in a way the prior tiers were not weird." Repeating the same category louder is the failure this skill prevents.
  6. Hold the narrator-tier as a special option. Saunders flags it as the highest-leverage tier because it changes how every prior tier reads. If the prose is currently transparent, the question to ask is whether the story is one in which the narrator's voice could itself be revealed as off, in which case the existing weirdnesses are recoded as filtered through that voice. (See the related skill skaz-narrator for the procedure.)
sections_html.signal: You can name, for your draft, four or five distinct categories of strangeness, each operating at a different level. A reader cannot collapse the strangeness into a single phrase; each tier supplies something the others do not. The story does not feel like one weird premise extended; it feels like a system whose oddness compounds.
sections_html.probe:
Before. For your current draft, complete the sentence: "The strangeness in this story is X." If you cannot expand that sentence into four or five categories that are each genuinely distinct (premise, characters' response, plan they form, world's resistance, voice telling it), the strangeness is concentrated.

After. Same prompt. The answer is a list of distinct categories, each filled. If you cut any one of them, the others would still hold but the compounding would weaken.
sections_html.failure: The draft that has one good weird premise and then several pages of restating it. The middle goes flat because the reader has accepted the premise on offer and is now waiting for the story to do something with it. The writer, not having decided what to compound, intensifies the same key (more talking objects, louder surreal description, the same kind of impossible event repeated) and the strangeness loses its purchase. By naming distinct categories and ratcheting fresh ones up, the strangeness keeps producing new ground rather than re-decorating ground already taken.
sections_html.example: The premise: a nose appears in a loaf of bread. That is one tier of weirdness. A weaker version of the story would make the nose more vivid, give it more lines, render the bread more strangely. Gogol does not. He moves to the next tier: the couple's reaction is irrational (Praskovya leaps to the conclusion that her barber husband cut it off a client; Ivan accepts this rather than asking the obvious questions). Then the next tier: the plan they form is to ditch the nose, which is the wrong response for either an innocent or a guilty party. Then the next tier: out in the world, Ivan cannot execute the simple act of dropping a wrapped object, because acquaintances and policemen are everywhere. Then the next tier: the narrator who has been telling us all this is himself unreliable, pedantic, and digresses about Ivan's "respectability" in a way that proves the opposite. Five distinct categories, each weird in a different way. None of them restates the others. The compounding is what makes the story feel infinite.
sections_html.source: George Saunders, A Swim in a Pond in the Rain, "The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'," lines 5252-5263.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends psychological-physics-of-strangeness. The physics skill establishes the rule of the world via immediate character reactions to the impossible event; this skill builds on that base by layering additional tiers (plan, world's resistance, narrator) so the strangeness compounds rather than re-decorating already-taken ground.
  • See also skaz-narrator (the narrator-tier, named here as the highest-leverage tier, is what skaz-narrator fills) and improper-narrative-emphasis (the sentence-level deflection that lets later tiers stay strange without exposing literal mechanics).

name: page-break-diagnostic
description: You are mid-read of a story, mid-revision of a draft, or trying to understand how a story is working on you moment by moment, and you want to make the reader's evolving expectations visible at chosen pauses instead of skimming through to the end and judging the whole.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders walks the entire Chekhov story page by page, pausing at each break to apply the same three-question protocol. He restates the protocol explicitly at lines 329-368 and again at lines 735-748.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Restates the same beat-tracking in different vocabulary: reading fiction as 'a series of establishings, stabilizations, and alterations' (the dog is sleeping; he is really sleeping; uh-oh, he woke up). The vocabulary names what the three-question protocol surfaces between pauses.

sections_html.when: You are reading a story to learn from it, revising your own draft and trying to feel where the reader is at each turn, or workshopping someone else's draft. You suspect the writer's choices later in the piece are responding to expectations the earlier pages set up, but those expectations are invisible to you because you read straight through. Reach for this whenever you want to see the story as a sequence of pulses rather than a finished whole.
sections_html.move:
1. Pick a pause. Treat each natural break (a chapter end, a scene break, a literal page) as a stop, or, in a draft, the points where you sense a new beat is starting.
  2. At the pause, before reading on, answer three questions in writing, briefly. (a) Look away from the page and summarize what you know so far in one or two sentences. (b) What are you curious about? (c) Where do you think the story is headed?
  3. Be specific. If your curiosity is "I want to know what happens," sharpen it: which character, about what, by when.
  4. Note the expectations your answers imply. Anything the next section does will land against this list. Saunders calls these the bowling pins in the air.
  5. Read the next section. Run the same three questions again.
  6. Compare the two answers. What did the new section confirm, complicate, defuse, ignore, or replace?
  7. Treat anything the next section ignored as a question the rest of the story still owes you. Treat anything it complicated as evidence the writer is working with what the earlier pages set up, not against it.
sections_html.signal: You can produce, for a given pause, a short list of specific questions and expectations the reader has at that moment, plus a record of how the next section spoke to each one. The list contains items you would have missed if you had read straight through, including expectations the story raised and then chose not to satisfy.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, pick a pause in the story or draft and ask: "What does the reader know, want, and expect here?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the second answer is more specific, names questions you did not see the first time, and lets you predict (and then check) which of those questions the next section will address.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, you read the story as a finished object and judge the whole. You miss the moment-by-moment pulse Saunders calls the way a story makes meaning at speed, and you cannot reverse-engineer the writer's choices because you are not reading from the seat of the reader whose expectations the writer was actually managing. Late-in-the-story decisions look arbitrary or "well-crafted" without you being able to say what they are crafted in response to. In a draft, you defend a scene because it is good in isolation, never noticing that the reader, at that point, is asking a different question than the scene answers.
sections_html.example: Saunders pauses at the end of the first page of "In the Cart" and runs the protocol. What do we know? An unhappy schoolteacher is in a cart, sick of her monotonous life. What are we curious about? How she got here. Where is the story headed? Some change in her situation. He shows that the story's second page opens with the answer to exactly that "how she got here" question (her parents died, and necessity forced her into teaching), demonstrating that the structure of the story is an organized response to questions the prior page caused the reader to ask.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 279-1349 (especially 329-368, 735-748, 1148-1152). Saunders frames the entire chapter as an exercise: he gives you "In the Cart" one page at a time, and at every break asks variants of the same three questions. The chapter's central claim is that this is how a story makes its meaning, and that paying attention to it page by page is how you learn what the form is doing.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts the-basic-drill. Use the page-break diagnostic mid-read at chosen pauses when the goal is to surface evolving expectations as the story unfolds. Use the basic drill after a complete reading, when the goal is to register the felt reactions of the finished experience. Both anchor reactions to places on the page, but operate at different stages of the reading.
  • See also truncation-test, which uses a related cut-and-observe procedure but on a fixed cut near the end (or on an internal unit) rather than at every break.

name: panel-of-judges
description: You are drafting or revising a scene whose central event is something the reader cannot directly experience on the page, a piece of music, a speech, a meal, a dance, a kiss, an act of charisma, and you need the reader to know whether it landed without hearing or seeing it.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Saunders names the function: the side characters in 'The Singers' serve as 'a sort of ascending ladder of credibility,' a graded panel of judges whose differing personalities and authorities let the reader weight their reactions to the unhearable singing.

sections_html.when: Your story has at its center an event the reader cannot directly access on the page. Singing, a sermon, a kiss, the taste of a dish, a magical performance, a charismatic argument, a speech that supposedly transforms its listeners. You can describe the event but the description alone will not tell the reader whether it is working on the people in the story. Reach for this whenever "the reader has to feel that it landed" cannot be solved by better description of the event itself.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the unwitnessable event. The thing the reader cannot fully experience: the singing, the eulogy, the meal.
  2. Recognize that the reader's verdict on the event will be drawn from the reactions of bystanders, not from the description of the event. Plan accordingly.
  3. Build the panel. Identify or invent multiple side characters who will be present during the event, each with a distinct personality, susceptibility, and credibility relative to the event's domain.
  4. Establish each panelist's credibility on the page, before the event. Saunders's pattern: a long static description, even a tedious one, that fixes for the reader what kind of judge this person is. The drunken Idiot Judge whose enthusiasm is too easy. The cynical innkeeper who does not pass false judgment. The locally feared Hercules whose verdict is final. The hardened wife who never cries.
  5. Make the panelists differ in at least two dimensions. Susceptibility: how easily they are moved. Authority: how much weight their verdict carries. A panel that is uniform in either dimension cannot grade.
  6. During the event, narrate the event minimally and the reactions specifically. A first impression from the lowest-credibility panelist is your "broad indicator." A subtler reaction from a higher-credibility panelist refines the verdict. The highest-authority panelist, held in reserve, finalizes it.
  7. Use the order deliberately. Saunders calls this an ascending ladder of credibility: lower-authority reactions come first, higher-authority reactions later. The reader learns to weight earlier reactions against later ones. A premature verdict from the Idiot Judge can be subtly overturned by the silence of the high-authority panelist, then confirmed or replaced when the highest panelist finally speaks.
  8. The verdict on the event is the cumulative shape of the panel's reactions, not any single one. Once the panel has spoken, the unwitnessable event has been adjudicated for the reader without the writer ever having to render the event itself convincingly.
sections_html.signal: For each side character present during the central event, you can finish three sentences. "What kind of judge they are: ___." "Where on the credibility ladder they sit: ___." "What their reaction during the event tells the reader: ___." The reactions, taken in order, form a weighted verdict that the reader receives even though the event itself was not directly rendered. Cutting any one panelist would weaken the verdict in a specific, nameable way.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of the central event: "How will the reader know whether it worked?" Note your answer. If it is "I will describe the event vividly," you have not solved the problem.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if your answer becomes "the panel will tell them, in this graded order, with these weights," and you can name each panelist's contribution.
sections_html.failure: Without the panel, you try to make the unwitnessable event land through the quality of your description of it, and you cannot. Singing on the page is not singing. A sermon transcribed is not the sermon delivered. The reader's verdict on these events is being constructed from the bystanders whether you plan it or not, so an unplanned panel is a panel that votes randomly. Worse, if all your bystanders react identically (they all weep, or all applaud), they collapse into one undifferentiated voice and the event reads as merely asserted, not adjudicated. Saunders is exact about the alternative: differentiated panelists with established credibilities make the event readable in real time, and the reader leaves the scene knowing both what happened and how to weight it.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks through Turgenev's contest scene a second time with the panel-of-judges lens. Booby is the Idiot Judge, a known local drunk whose pronouncement that the contractor has won serves as a broad early indicator and is then subtly overturned by Nikolai the innkeeper, who is "an excellent judge of what a true Russian considers to be either interesting or important." The Wild Gentleman has been set up via a long description as having "enormous influence in all the district," "passionately fond of singing," with "tremendous forces" hidden inside him. He becomes the highest-authority panelist, and his reactions are the ones the others orbit. During the contractor's song he smiles at one transition and softens his gaze, but does not finalize. During Yashka's song the hardened wife cries, Nikolai looks down (hiding tears), Blinker turns away (hiding tears), Booby is stunned silent, the little peasant whimpers, and finally the Wild Gentleman sheds "a heavy tear" and says the single word "Yashka." The verdict is delivered. The singing itself is unhearable. The graded reactions, in order, have made it not just heard but adjudicated. Saunders names the underlying engineering: this is exactly why the eleven pre-contest pages of static description had to exist, so the reader would know how to weight each reaction once the contest began.
sections_html.source: Chapter "The Heart of the Story," lines 1924-1938. Saunders identifies the function the side characters serve, names the structure as "an ascending ladder of credibility," and uses it to explain why the long static descriptions earlier in the story are not bloat but infrastructure for the contest.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also heart-of-the-story (when the heart is an unwitnessable event — singing, a sermon, a kiss — the panel is the device that adjudicates it for the reader, and the pre-event descriptions of side characters are the infrastructure that lets the panel grade).
