  • See also increasing-specification (each panelist must be specified enough on at least two dimensions, susceptibility and authority, for the reader to weight their reactions) and mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts (the panel's verdict is delivered by rotating among bystanders' reactions during the event).

name: parameter-table-for-recurrence
description: You are drafting or critiquing a story whose structure repeats a scene-type, a relationship, or a unit (three sons, four lovers, a daily ritual, a returning antagonist). The recurrences either feel flat (the same beat over and over, anecdote not story) or feel random (variation without inferable meaning). You cannot tell which parameters across the recurrences are doing the meaning-making work and which are drifting.

sources.chapter: A Pattern Story: Thoughts on 'The Darling'
sources.note: Saunders introduces Table 2 on the Olenka-Kukin relationship, then notices on the Olenka-Vasily section that the same column headings carry over and Table 3 builds itself for all four loves. He gives the football-choreography analogy for why internal dynamics (the variation across parameters) is what generates meaning, and the neutralized '9 on a scale of 10' counter-version to show that a flat recurrence destroys it. He demonstrates reading across rows ('How he refers to Olenka,' 'Period of mourning,' 'Status of the wing') to surface the inferred arc of decline.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Three additional worked instances broaden the procedure. (1) The clothesline outside Grishkino, passed four times: shirts struggling and waving, white shirt attached only by a frozen sleeve, frozen washing still fluttering desperately, finally no longer to be seen. The four-column tabulation is the same procedure as Olenka but at the level of a recurring physical detail rather than a relationship; Saunders cross-references the move ('we defined escalation as that which results when we refuse to repeat beats') and gives the place-setting / silverware-tracking-shot analogy as the most compressed worked example: full arrangement, spoon missing, spoon and fork missing, all silverware missing — read as 'evacuation' or 'diminishment' by the reader's row-reading reflex. (2) The 'shadow structure' table covering all ten sections of 'Master and Man': each section begins with a desire/problem/setback and ends with the dangled hope of some improvement. The parameter-table here encodes the shape of the unit itself ('opens with: ___; closes with: ___') rather than elements within the unit; what changes from row to row can be the structural form, not just its contents. (3) Section VI's before-and-after of Vasili's coping methods: the same parameters (cigarettes, disparaging others, bragging to himself, running the numbers) appear in two rows; the second row shows each parameter failing in the same sequence. With a two-row table whose parameters fail uniformly in the second pass, the read-down is 'this person has changed' — change made visible without being narrated. The move generalizes from N-fold relationships to any recurring unit (including a single character revisited at two moments) and from element-tracking to shape-tracking.

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: A held-constant variation type. Alyosha's defining trait (cheerful obedience, smile, get back to work) holds as a single recurrent parameter across rows of escalating stakes: home (teasing, beating, scolding by father), merchant's house (uncouth-peasant reception, indispensability without gratitude), boots-and-pay subplot (deduction without complaint), proposal-refusal (clucks tongue at weeping Ustinya), deathbed (smiles in the snow, attributes amazement at-something to nothing). Across all rows the trait response is the same: smiles, goes silent, accepts. The reader's row-reading reflex registers the held-constancy as the meaning — the trait does not break under any recurrence. Then, at the marriage-refusal climactic recurrence, the trait deviates for the first time: Alyosha smiles, then bursts into tears. The fold installs the held-constant-then-broken pattern as a recognized variation type the parameter table can detect: where the cross-row variation is ordinarily what generates meaning, the held-constant pattern (with a single deliberate break at the climactic row) generates meaning by the contrast between the held-constancy and the break. The procedural test is the same — read across the rows — but the success criterion includes 'the parameter held against pressure that should have changed it.' The break, when it comes, is felt as escalation against the held-constant baseline, and the structural decision of where to place the break (which row, at what stakes) is the writer's primary craft choice.

sections_html.when: You have a story (your own or someone else's) whose structure includes an obvious repetition: a sequence of similar scenes, a series of relationships, a recurring ritual, a returning visitor. You sense the repetition is doing structural work but cannot say what. Or you are drafting and the second instance of a recurrence is starting to feel like a copy of the first instead of a meaningful variation, and the reader is about to hit the "long-dream-someone-told-me-about" feeling. Reach for this whenever a story repeats and you need to know what each recurrence is supposed to add.
sections_html.move:
1. Identify the repeated unit. Name what is recurring at the same structural level: a relationship (each lover), a scene-type (each daily visit to the Big Slot), a ritual (each pre-battle prayer), an event (each knock at the gate at night). The unit must be the same kind of thing each time, not the same exact thing.
  2. From the first instance, extract a list of parameters the story actually specifies for that unit. In Saunders's worked example on Olenka-Kukin: his job, the flavor of their courtship, the flavor of their relationship, how he refers to her, how she refers to him, duration in pages, duration in time, method of parting, period of mourning, the status of the wing of the house. Use what the story specifies, not what you imagine could be specified.
  3. Make a row for each parameter and a column for each recurrence. Fill in the first column from the first instance.
  4. Walk through each subsequent instance and fill in its column using the same parameters. If the story does not specify a parameter for a later instance, that absence is itself data: the story declined to vary that row, or declined to specify it. Note absences explicitly.
  5. Read across each row. Variation across a row is the meaning that recurrence is generating. Read "how he refers to her" across all four columns and you see whether the address-form is intensifying, decaying, or inverting. Read "duration of mourning" across all four columns and you see whether the losses are getting harder to shake. Read "status of the wing" across all four columns and you see a structural undertow the surface narration does not name.
  6. For drafting: as you write each recurrence, fill in the table for it before writing. If a row would copy the prior column verbatim, change something on purpose. The variation must be telling, thoughtful, non-robotic. Saunders's failure case is the "9 on a scale of 10" neutralized version, where every recurrence has identical parameters and the story collapses into anecdote.
  7. For critique: a row that does not vary across recurrences is either a deliberate constant (a character trait persisting through changing circumstances) or a missed opportunity (the writer forgot to service that parameter). Decide which, and act on it.
sections_html.signal: You can name, for any row in the table, what its variation pattern across the recurrences is telling the reader. You can point to at least one row whose variation generates a felt arc (decline, escalation, accumulation, reversal) that the surface plot summary does not name. For a draft, you can point to at least one row whose variation you adjusted on purpose during the latest pass.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "What is the difference between the first recurrence and the last? What does the story gain by repeating?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer changes from a single thematic abstraction ("she keeps falling in love") to a list of specific row-level variations whose juxtaposition generates the abstraction by inference.
sections_html.failure: Without this procedure, repetition reads either as filler (the same beat, a long dream) or as random variation (changes happen but cannot be summed). The writer adds details to the second and third recurrence to keep them from being identical, but adds them by feel, with no schema, so some parameters drift and others stay flat without intent. The reader, sensing the recurrence is supposed to mean something but unable to locate the meaning, drops out. Saunders's diagnosis: a pattern story without controlled variation is a halftime show in which the people on the field drift around in randomly colored street clothes; the difference between a choreographer and a hack is attention to the details of internal dynamics, and the parameter table is what makes those details auditable.
sections_html.example: Saunders builds Table 2 from the first relationship in "The Darling" (Olenka and Kukin), specifying eleven parameters: his job, flavor of courtship, flavor of relationship, how he refers to her, how she refers to him, duration in pages, duration in time, method of parting, period of mourning, status of the wing of the house. When the second relationship (Olenka and Vasily) begins, he starts a new chart and notices the column headings stay the same; he then tabulates Smirnin and Sasha against the same headings and lays the four columns side by side. Reading across "how he refers to her" yields the arc Darling → Darling → Don't butt in → Leave me alone, the address-form decaying as the relationships move from worship to insult to silence. Reading across "duration of mourning" yields three months → over six months → some number of years → none required (still together), surfacing both an arc of deepening loss and the fact that the final relationship has not yet ended. Reading across "status of the wing" yields a structural undertow of who is moving in, who is empty, and who Olenka herself migrates to. None of this is in any single sentence of the story. All of it is in the across-row reading the table makes possible.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Pattern Story," lines 2587 (Table 2 introduced after the Olenka-Kukin section), 2777-2787 (the parallelism noticed on Olenka-Vasily; the column headings carry over; Table 3 across all four loves; "the structure forces a comparison"; "the story, seen in this way, is a beautiful system for presenting a pattern of controlled variation"), 2906-2918 (the football-choreography analogy and the neutralized "9 on a scale of 10" counter-version), 2966-2972 (the late-night-knock structural rhyme as one parameter that recurs and varies; Pavlovian conditioning of the reader by patterning), 2982-2993 (reading across rows surfaces decline; "we are only asking these questions because the length of each relationship was specified by the story and because Chekhov took the trouble to vary this parameter").
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also refuse-to-repeat-beats. The parameter table surfaces which rows vary and which hold flat across recurrences; the refuse-to-repeat-beats rule is then how each held-flat row gets diagnosed (deliberate constant or missed escalation) and acted on. The two together implement Saunders's escalation discipline at recurrence-scope: tabulate, then decide.
  • See also causality-not-sequence (when parameter variation is not just chosen but earned, the across-row reading is also a causal chain) and escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint (when the writer-self-image cannot generate variation, the constraint exercise produces a rising-action draft that the parameter table can then critique).

name: persistent-self-contradiction
description: You are drafting or revising a thematically loaded story (about belief, judgment, value, what is right) and notice the story is moving toward a conclusion the writer endorses. Each scene quietly votes the same way. You worry the story will read as a position paper or sermon, but the obvious correctives (add a counterargument character, hedge the ending) feel mechanical or false.

sources.chapter: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'
sources.note: Saunders names the method on 'Gooseberries' explicitly: 'proceeds by a method of persistent self-contradiction. If one aspect of it seems to be expressing a certain view, a new aspect of it will appear and challenge that view.' The story 'is not there to tell us what to think... It is there to help us think... a structure to help us think.' The procedural form: 'a series of on the other hand statements' — Ivan against happiness on the other hand he sure does enjoy swimming; Alyohin admirably calm and excess-free on the other hand he darkened the water with self-neglect; Ivan's passion is selfish on the other hand Burkin's continual moderation is annoying; the gooseberry passion is weird on the other hand the brother loves something on the other other hand Alyohin never frugalitied anyone to death. The recursive on-the-other-other-hand chain is the procedure. Saunders's stop condition: 'the story keeps qualifying itself until it qualifies itself right out of the business of judgment.' The author-disposition (lines 6299-6313) frames the procedure: 'every human position has a problem with it. Believed in too much, it slides into error. It's not that no position is correct; it's that no position is correct for long.' Chekhov as 'free of agenda... willing to go wherever the data takes him,' and 'if he has a program, it's being wary of having a program.' Stories as 'splendid, brief reconsideration machines.' The closing schema (6285-6289) gives the question shape: 'Question: Is X good or bad? Story: For whom? On what day, under what conditions? Might there be some unintended consequences associated with X? Some good hidden in the bad that is X? Some bad hidden in the good that is X? Tell me more.'

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: The Rubin's-vase outcome names the procedure's terminal product. When persistent self-contradiction has been performed across a whole story so that two readings vote with equal force, the story 'fails to answer' the central question; or, equivalently, 'succeeds in answering — in favor of both views, simultaneously.' Saunders's image: 'the two readings keep moving forward and then receding, as in the optical illusion called Rubin's vase.' The story is 'really these two coexistent interpretations, eternally struggling for prominence. If we decide that the story supports cheerful obedience, it does. If we decide that it opposes cheerful obedience, it does.' The success criterion: 'the story, in declining to answer (in obscuring the place where it might have answered), feels like it's not avoiding the question but irradiating it with increased intensity.' The fold installs the Rubin's-vase image as the procedure's terminal product (when both readings are built equally well, the un-rankability is the meaning) and the irradiation framing as the success criterion (the question is not avoided, it is intensified by being denied a deciding moment). Note the relationship to withhold-the-resolving-interior: persistent-self-contradiction is the macro-structural method that builds the two equally-voting readings across the prior pages; withhold-the-resolving-interior is the single-moment technical decision that preserves the un-rankability at the resolution point. They commonly fire together, with the macro skill setting up the configuration the moment-skill protects.

sections_html.when: You are working on a story whose thematic charge is real and intentional: a story about happiness, about whether to forgive, about the cost of ambition, about whether a belief is worth holding. You can name the position the story is moving toward. Each scene you have written votes for it. The story is becoming the thing the writer thinks about the question, delivered as fiction. Reach for this whenever you can summarize your story in one sentence of the form "X is good" or "X is bad" and feel the summary is faithful to what the draft is doing.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the position the story is currently expressing. One sentence, in the form of a verdict. Not "the story is about happiness," but "the story expresses the view that happiness is decadent" or "the story expresses the view that the small kindness redeems."
  2. Identify each aspect of the story (character, scene, image, voice) that votes for the position. Make a list. The list is the inventory of one-way votes.
  3. For each item on the list, find a counterweight aspect. The counterweight is not a refutation. It is an aspect of the same world that, when held next to the voting aspect, complicates the verdict without erasing it. If a character delivers a passionate condemnation of happiness, find the moment that character is also susceptible to happiness (a swim in cold water, a noticed beauty, a thoughtless pleasure with a smelly residue). If a scene argues that the small kindness redeems, find the cost the kindness imposed elsewhere.
  4. Write the counterweight in. Render it with the same fullness as the voting aspect. The two readings must coexist on the page. Saunders's criterion: "the two readings coexist, making a truth bigger than either would have alone."
  5. Do not rank. Refuse the temptation to make one reading override the other. The story is not delivering a verdict; it is staging a conversation. If you can summarize the story by quoting only the voting aspects, the counterweight is too quiet. If you can summarize it by quoting only the counterweights, the original position has been erased.
  6. Recurse. Once a counterweight is in, look at it. Is it now expressing its own one-sided position? "The brother's passion for gooseberries is trivial; on the other hand, at least he loves something; on the other other hand, his frugality killed his wife." Saunders runs the chain three deep on Alyohin and on the gooseberry-lover. Each new "on the other hand" is a new aspect that complicates the prior layer.
  7. Stop when further qualification would no longer add new dimension. The stop condition is not "the story has reached a conclusion" but "the next on-the-other-hand would be redundant or pedantic." The story has, in Saunders's phrase, "qualified itself right out of the business of judgment."
  8. Test the result with the question schema. Read the draft, then ask of its central concern: "For whom? On what day, under what conditions? Are there unintended consequences? Is there good hidden in the bad? Is there bad hidden in the good?" The story should answer "tell me more" rather than supplying a verdict.
sections_html.signal: A reader cannot summarize the story as endorsing or condemning the central concern. Asked what the story thinks about X, they have to list pairs of aspects that pull in different directions, and they cannot say which pair the story prefers. The summary they can give is procedural ("the story shows X from these angles, and refuses to settle which angle is right") rather than verdictive ("the story shows that X is bad"). At least one aspect of the draft has, on this pass, been complicated by a counterweight you wrote in deliberately, and you can name the counterweight's specific function.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, write your one-sentence summary of what the story is currently saying. Make it a verdict. Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the verdict-shaped summary no longer fits and you have to give a list of pairs instead. The summary becomes "for whom, when, at what cost, with what unintended consequence" rather than "X is bad."
sections_html.failure:
Without this procedure, a thematically charged story slides into polemic. Each scene votes the same way because the writer believes the verdict and is unconsciously stacking the deck. The reader, sensing the stacking, either agrees and finishes converted, or disagrees and finishes annoyed; in both cases the story has functioned as essay rather than fiction. Saunders names the underlying craft fact: a story is not the place to tell the reader what to think but a structure to help them think. The structure works by staging the thought it would otherwise deliver as conclusion. If every aspect of the story votes the same way, the story has nothing for the reader to think with; it has only a position to accept or reject. The reader reads to be enlarged, not converted, and persistent self-contradiction is what does the enlarging.

A second failure mode the procedure prevents: the false counterweight. A writer who has noticed the polemic problem may add a token character who voices the opposite view, then defeat that character. The reader registers the token as a token; the story's vote is unchanged. The procedural test is whether the counterweight aspect coexists with the voting aspect without being defeated. If the counterweight loses, the writer has performed the polemic with extra steps.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks "Gooseberries" through the procedure. Ivan's speech against happiness votes against happiness. Saunders identifies the counterweight: Ivan's exuberant swim in the rain, in which the same man who just delivered the anti-happiness sermon plunges joyfully into a cold river, kicks up waves, dives to the bottom, swims across to chat with peasants. The two readings coexist: Ivan is against happiness; Ivan is also for happiness, perhaps more than his friends. Saunders names the result: "the two readings coexist, making a truth bigger than either would have alone." He recurses. Alyohin lives without striving, which votes for the moderate-life view; on the other hand his dirtiness and lostness vote against it (something has shut down, he lives beneath his potential). The maid Pelageya is so beautiful she stops the men in their tracks, supplying gratuitous beauty as a vote for the reality of joy; on the other hand she does all the work while the men sit and philosophize, supplying labor-cost as a vote for Ivan's position. The rain votes both ways at different points: it makes the men miserable on the road, refreshes them in the swim, and at the close taps on the windows in a way Saunders explicitly leaves irreducible. The recursion stops not at a verdict but at the point where Saunders himself notes that any further articulation of what the rain is "saying" would be reductive and insufficient. The story has qualified itself out of the business of judgment, and the reader is left thinking rather than concluding.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'," lines 6256-6270 (the named method "persistent self-contradiction"; the on-the-other-hand procedure with the four worked recursions; the "structure to help us think" framing; "qualifies itself right out of the business of judgment"), 6285-6289 (the question schema "For whom? On what day, under what conditions?"), 6299-6313 (the author-disposition: "every human position has a problem with it"; "no position is correct for long"; Chekhov as "free of agenda... willing to go wherever the data takes him"; "if he has a program, it's being wary of having a program"; stories as "splendid, brief reconsideration machines").
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also belief-by-character-attribution. The two are complementary and commonly fire together. Belief-by-character-attribution renders one earnest position with full conviction and destabilizes it by a small concrete fact about the speaker; persistent-self-contradiction is the macro-structural method that builds the counterweighting positions across the rest of the story so the destabilization is felt as part of a thinking machine rather than as a single hedge. The first is the move at one position; the second is the architecture that holds many.
  • See also withhold-the-resolving-interior (the single-moment technical decision at the climax that preserves the un-rankability this macro-method builds; the two together produce the Rubin's-vase outcome).

name: psychological-physics-of-strangeness
description: You are drafting or revising a story whose premise involves something that could not happen in the real world (a nose found in a loaf of bread, a talking object, a body part with a will of its own, a surreal arrival, a wheelbarrow of chatting things) and you suspect the strangeness will read as a stunt rather than as meaning. You can write the impossible event but cannot say what it is doing for the reader.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders names the principle: 'If a writer introduces a strange event, then lets the fictive world respond to that event, what we're really learning about is what we might call the fictive world's psychological physics. What are the rules there? How do things proceed? A story like that will feel truthful and essential to the extent that the psychological physics of the fictive world are felt to be similar to the psychological physics of our own.' The meaning lives not in the impossible event but in the reactions that establish the rules of the world. He demonstrates the move on the nose-in-bread (Praskovya assumes guilt; Ivan accepts it; both are afraid of being blamed not of the impossibility itself), on the polite-mild reactions of the offices Kovalyov visits ('Their reactions are strangely mild, as if Kovalyov has lost not his nose but a suitcase'), and on the limit-stretching procedure ('we're now arguing earnestly for the ad to be run, having accepted not only the reality of a nose's conversion into a person but the idea that a notice in a newspaper might help us find it'). The shards-not-vase image (5560) names the long-form effect: by the time tiers compound, the story reads as coherent on the first pass even though 'the pieces don't fit, because it was never a coherent vase to begin with.' Saunders frames the orientation explicitly as a form of realism, not absurdism: 'Gogol is a supreme realist, looking past the way things seem to how they really are.'

sections_html.when: Your story has, or is about to have, an event that could not happen in the real world. A talking object, a body part that walks off, an arrival from outside the consensus world, a premise the reader will recognize as impossible. You can imagine writing the strange event itself, but you cannot say what it is going to mean. You suspect, correctly, that the meaning is not in the strangeness but in the reactions to it, and you have not yet decided what those reactions will be. Reach for this whenever the strangeness is the easy part and the world's response is the hard part.
sections_html.move:
1. Locate the strange event. The nose in the bread, the talking gloves, the body part with a mind, the impossible arrival. State it as one sentence and place it in the manuscript.
  2. Refuse to defend the strangeness. Do not write paragraphs justifying how the impossible thing got there. The reader will accept any premise, briefly, on offer. The defense is not where the meaning is.
  3. Compose the immediate reactions of the characters present. For each character in the scene, ask: how does this person respond to the impossibility? Their response must be specific, particular to who they are, and disproportionately concerned with something other than the impossibility itself. Saunders's worked beat: Praskovya is not dumbfounded by the nose; she leaps to the (skewed) explanation that her husband cut it off a client, and Ivan, instead of pointing out the impossibility, accepts the accusation and worries about the police finding it.
  4. Read the reactions across all the characters together and ask: what rule do these reactions imply? Not what symbol or theme, what rule. (In Gogol's nose-world, the rule is something like: the social calculation of blame outranks the metaphysical fact of the impossibility.) The rule is the world's psychological physics.
  5. Test the rule against our own world. Saunders's criterion: a strange-premise story feels truthful "to the extent that the psychological physics of the fictive world are felt to be similar to the psychological physics of our own." If the rule you have surfaced ("when something disastrous happens, people worry about being blamed before they ask what happened") is recognizable as a way our world actually works, the strangeness will mean. If the rule is arbitrary or merely whimsical, the strangeness will read as a stunt.
  6. Compose the reactions of the wider world. The bystanders, the officials, the offices Kovalyov visits. Their mildness, their strict professionalism, their inability to see this as their problem are themselves the meaning-making engine. Saunders's note: "Their reactions are strangely mild, as if Kovalyov has lost not his nose but a suitcase." The mildness is the rule made visible.
  7. Accustom the reader by stages. Each later strangeness should land inside the system the earlier reactions established, so that by mid-story the reader will accept (without remarking) something the opening would have rejected. Saunders names the moment: by the newspaper-office scene "we're now arguing earnestly for the ad to be run, having accepted not only the reality of a nose's conversion into a person but the idea that a notice in a newspaper might help us find it."
  8. Do not aim for a closed system. The shards-not-vase principle: the story does not have to add up on inspection. It must read as coherent on the first pass, the way snapshots sell as continuous motion. The procedure is to compose tile-by-tile, holding only the local reaction as your obligation, not the long-form audit of consistency.
sections_html.signal: A reader (or you, freshly) can describe what the world's rules are, in plain language, without using the word "absurd." The reactions to the impossibility produce specific, recognizable claims about how power, blame, mildness, or social calculation actually work. The strangeness no longer reads as a stunt; it reads as the most efficient way the story could have surfaced the rule. When asked "what does this story mean," your answer is a sentence about how a kind of system actually behaves, not a sentence about the symbolism of the impossible event.
sections_html.probe:
Before. Pick the strangest event in your draft. Write down, in one sentence, what it means. If your sentence is a paraphrase of the event ("the nose disappears, which represents alienation"), the strangeness is doing the work the world should be doing.

After. Same prompt. The sentence is now about a rule of the fictive world that resembles a rule of our own world. ("In a system where blame is socially distributed before facts are established, an impossibility produces not investigation but defensive scrambling.") The event is the occasion; the rule is the meaning.
sections_html.failure: Strange-premise stories that read as merely whimsical, surreal-for-its-own-sake, or "absurdist" in the dismissive sense. The writer puts the meaning into the strangeness ("a talking nose represents X") and writes the reactions perfunctorily. The reader feels the strangeness without feeling the truth claim. The story collapses to a one-line premise plus narrative filler. By placing the meaning at the response and calibrating the response so its rules resemble our own, the strange-premise story becomes (in Saunders's phrase) a form of supreme realism rather than a stunt.
sections_html.example: A nose appears in a loaf of bread. The husband, a barber, accepts his wife's accusation that he cut it off a client, even though the nose was not there yesterday and the bread was made this morning. He decides, with her, to ditch the nose. He worries about being blamed by the police. He fails to ditch it cleanly because the world he goes out into is full of people who will see him and policemen who will stop him. None of these reactions investigate the impossibility. All of them accept it as the local fact and react to its social consequences. The rule of the world surfaces: in this system, the metaphysical strangeness is irrelevant; the social calculation of who gets blamed is what counts. That rule is recognizable; it is how parts of our own world actually work. The strangeness, by being treated as ordinary, becomes a vehicle for an exact realism.
sections_html.source: George Saunders, A Swim in a Pond in the Rain, "The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'," lines 5197-5220, 5470-5474, 5494-5495, 5556-5562, 5582-5588.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also multiple-superimposed-weirdness (once the world's psychological physics are established by the immediate reactions, layering further tiers — the plan formed, the world's resistance, the narrator's voice — is what compounds the meaning instead of restating the strangeness).
  • See also improper-narrative-emphasis (the deflection technique that lets the impossible thing be on the page without being inspected) and confess-the-oddness (the ending move that closes a strange-premise story without explaining away the irregularities the physics produced).

name: build-character-tolstoy-arc
description: You are drafting a story whose central work is the rendering of a particular person across an arc that culminates in their transformation, and you are unsure how to take the character from "a type" through "a particular person" through "a moral architecture the reader can feel" to a climax that does not read as propaganda. You need a workflow that builds the character at each scope (situation, sentence, exchange, climax) in the order each scope's work depends on the prior.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders names increasing-specification on Marya: she is unhappy, then unhappy because of monotony rather than grief or scorned love, then a thirteen-year veteran of the trip, then unable to imagine another future. Each fact rules out clichés and increases pressure for meaningful action.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Saunders walks all three subsequent steps on Tolstoy's 'Master and Man' as a single integrated demonstration. Matrix-of-facts (4181-4213): Tolstoy's prose is 'almost entirely facts'; even subjective judgments and characters' interior thoughts are 'rendered as objectively and precisely as Nikita's crossing of the yard.' Mind-to-mind vantage shifts (4253-4293): the Vasili-Nikita wage exchange across three paragraphs cycles through five vantages — omniscient narrator, Vasili's speech, Vasili's thought, Nikita's speech, Nikita's thought. The Grishkino departure runs five perspectives across four paragraphs. Transformation by redirected energy (4663-4709, 4731-4737): after Nikita begs forgiveness and announces he is dying, Vasili 'just acts (or, actually, goes back into action),' striking the same resolution he used 'when making a good purchase'; tears come after the action, not before; post-transformation Vasili is 'still self-celebratory, still living to feel good about himself.' The four steps are walked on a single character, in order, in the same chapter.

sections_html.when: You are drafting a story whose engine is one character moving through an arc that ends in transformation. The character is in your head as a type (a miser, a hypocrite, a self-absorbed master, a worry-prone middle-class striver). You want them to land as a particular person whose actions feel inevitable rather than asserted, whose moral architecture the reader feels rather than is told, and whose climactic change reads as redirection of the energy they had all along rather than as a substitution of a new being for the old. Reach for this when the character must carry the weight of the story across all four scopes — situation, sentence, exchange, climax — and you need a workflow that builds each scope on the prior.
sections_html.move:
1. Specify the character until plot becomes available. Apply increasing-specification. Ask "which particular person is this, anyway?" and answer with facts that rule out clichés. "Vasili is a master" rules out nothing; "Vasili is a master who routinely cheats his servant Nikita and is honestly convinced that he is Nikita's benefactor" rules out the simpler hypocrite (the bwa-ha-ha villain) and the simpler saint (the well-meaning patron). Continue specifying until the facts produce pressure for particular events: a specified character generates plot. The output of this step is a list of defining facts on which the rest of the story will rely. Without this step, the prose has nothing to render — every later scope is rendering the character, and the rendering needs a particular person to be of.
  1. Render the world and the character in the matrix-of-facts register. Apply matrix-of-facts. The character's actions, surroundings, and interior thoughts are reported in the same factual idiom: short, plain, declarative, free of qualifier and metaphor. "He flicked the apron at the boiling samovar." "He was honestly convinced that he was Nikita's benefactor." A fact-syntax sentence about an interior state ("honestly convinced that") gets accepted in the same spirit as a fact-syntax sentence about an exterior fact ("the bridle is leather and tasseled"). The output of this step is a prose register in which any sentence about the character — exterior or interior — produces involuntary buy-in rather than skeptical reading. Without this step, the specified character of step 1 cannot be rendered; the writer reaches for editorialized or lyrical assertion, the reader holds the prose at arm's length, and the specification's work is squandered.
  1. Rotate vantages in morally loaded exchanges. Apply mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts. For each exchange whose meaning depends on the gap between what people say and what they know, draft three short renderings per character: their public stance (what they say), their private stance (what they think, in the same factual register from step 2), and how the omniscient narrator describes their position from outside. Compose the scene by rotating among these vantages. The reader leaves the exchange knowing the moral architecture without anyone having labeled it. The output of this step is a scene whose moral weight is felt through structural rotation rather than through authorial assertion. This step depends on step 2 because the rotation only produces involuntary belief if the interior thoughts arrive in the same factual idiom as the exteriors; without matrix-of-facts, the move into a character's mind reads as a confession or a lyrical interiority, and the rotation breaks.
  1. Render the climactic transformation as redirected energy in the character's familiar idiom. Apply transformation-by-redirected-energy. Refuse the soliloquy ("He realized that..."). Place the character in an action they were already capable of taking, with the action's beneficiary or object reset. Borrow the character's familiar idiom from the prior pages — the same resolution Vasili used when striking hands on a profitable purchase. Render only the action in concrete factual sentences (matrix-of-facts again). Allow the character to react to their own action with surprise, but render the surprise after the action. Preserve the character's old register post-transformation. The output of this step is a climax in which the change is visible through action, not announcement, and the post-transformation character is still recognizably themselves. This step depends on step 3 because the rotation produces the moral architecture the climax must carry forward; on step 2 because the climactic action is rendered in the same factual idiom; and on step 1 because "the character's familiar idiom" requires a character who has been specific enough across the prior pages to have one.
sections_html.signal: You can produce four artifacts that share a single character. (a) A list of defining facts that rule out the obvious clichéd version of this character, with the next event the story is now ripe for predictable from the list. (b) A page of prose in which any subjective claim sits in a matrix of factual sentences whose syntax is indistinguishable; a stranger reading the page cold cannot easily tell whether a sentence reports an external fact or an internal state. (c) At least one morally loaded exchange that the reader can summarize as a gap (what each character said vs. what each privately knew) without having been told. (d) A climactic action rendered in concrete factual sentences, in the character's familiar idiom from prior pages, with the character's surprise at their own action arriving after the action and the post-transformation register still sounding like them. A reader of (d) cannot point to a sentence in which the character announces or realizes the change.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of your character: "Is this person specific enough that the next event has to be a particular kind of event, do my sentences make the reader believe in them without being asked, can the reader feel the moral architecture of any exchange they are in, and does my climax change them by redirecting the energy they already had?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if you can answer yes to all four parts and point at the specific work each step did: the facts that ruled out clichés, the factual register that produced involuntary buy-in, the rotation that surfaced the moral architecture, the redirected action that made the change visible.
sections_html.failure:
The character who is rendered well at one or two scopes but not all four arrives broken. Each missing step produces a specific failure.

Skipping step 1 (no specification): the character is a placeholder; no particular plot fits; events feel arbitrary because the character is not the kind of person these things would happen to. Saunders names the underlying fact: before specification, "there was nothing in the story that wanted to happen."

Skipping step 2 (no matrix-of-facts): the specified character is rendered through editorialized or lyrical assertion; the reader holds the prose at arm's length; the specification's work is squandered because no sentence produces involuntary buy-in.

Skipping step 3 (no vantage rotation): morally loaded exchanges depend on the writer asserting (through narrative position) who is the hypocrite and who is the truth-teller; the moral architecture must be told rather than felt; the reader has nothing to think with.

Skipping step 4 (no redirected-energy climax): the climax becomes a soliloquy of realization that the prior steps' careful work cannot save. The character realizes a doctrine, articulates it in interior monologue, converts to its program; the prose reads as a sermon and the character as the writer's mouthpiece. Or, the inverse failure: the character is so radically remade that they no longer resemble the person we have been reading; the reader cannot transfer their belief.

Doing the steps out of order: step 4 first writes a transformation scene with no specified character to redirect; step 3 before step 2 produces a rotation in the wrong register; step 2 before step 1 polishes the syntax of a placeholder.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks the entire chain on Vasili in "Master and Man." Step 1 (specification): Vasili is a master who cheats Nikita and is honestly convinced he is Nikita's benefactor. Step 2 (matrix-of-facts): the prose is "almost entirely facts"; even Vasili's interior thoughts are "rendered as objectively and precisely as Nikita's crossing of the yard." Step 3 (vantage rotation): the wage exchange cycles through five vantages — omniscient narrator (Vasili routinely cheats Nikita), Vasili's speech ("we deal straightforwardly"), Vasili's thought (honestly convinced he is Nikita's benefactor), Nikita's speech (his own spin), Nikita's thought (quite aware he is being cheated). Step 4 (redirected-energy transformation): after Nikita begs forgiveness and announces he is dying, Vasili stands silent for half a minute, then "just acts (or, actually, goes back into action)" with "the same resolution he used to strike hands when making a good purchase." Six factual actions: rake the snow, undo the girdle, open the coat, push Nikita down, lie on top of him, adjust the skirt to tent the body. Then, only then, the surprise: tears come, the jaw quivers, a strange and solemn tenderness arrives. Post-transformation Vasili is "still self-celebratory, still living to feel good about himself" — only now what he is feeling good about is having saved a man's life. Same character, four scopes, one chain.
sections_html.source: Specification: chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 415-434 (Saunders specifies Marya in the opening pages of "In the Cart"; the Vasili specification is throughout "Master and Man" and is read by Saunders in the lead-up to lines 4181). Matrix-of-facts, vantage rotation, and redirected-energy transformation are walked on a single character in chapter "And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'": lines 4181-4213 (matrix), 4253-4293 (rotation), 4663-4709 and 4731-4737 (transformation). The chain is composed here; "Master and Man" is the worked instance Saunders walks across these four scopes.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Composed of increasing-specification, matrix-of-facts, mind-to-mind-vantage-shifts, transformation-by-redirected-energy. Each atomic skill stands on its own; this recipe is the workflow that fires them together when the central work of the story is the rendering of a particular person across an arc.
  • See also causality-not-sequence (the across-beat causation discipline that operates inside this chain — the specification of step 1 supplies the why-this-not-that for each beat, the rotation of step 3 makes causal links visible, and the climactic action of step 4 is rendered as the next causal beat after the prior pages), withhold-the-resolving-interior (the contrast condition for step 4: when the prior pages have built two readings with equal force and the character's interior at the climax must remain un-rendered to preserve the un-rankability, this chain's step 4 is replaced by the omission move), and panel-of-judges (when the climactic event is unwitnessable, the panel adjudicates it for the reader; this chain's transformation is witnessable, but the panel skill is the structural cousin for unwitnessable climaxes).

name: draft-by-energy-bypass
description: You are a writer producing technically careful work that comes back described as "interesting" or "well-crafted" rather than alive, and the writer-self-image you channel when you sit down is the same one producing the inert drafts. You can name the deficit but cannot reason your way out of it because the writer doing the reasoning is the writer who produced the deficit. You need a workflow that diagnoses the trap, bypasses it in a single session, sustains the surfaced voice into a real draft, and then revises that draft per-decision.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Saunders names the energy probe (wrist-meter / 1000-units image), tells the autobiographical case of abandoning Hemingway-mountain for 'Saunders Mountain,' and frames the trade as a precondition for any subsequent writing practice.

sources.chapter: Appendix B: An Escalation Exercise
sources.note: Saunders supplies the constraint-bypass protocol (45-min timer, 200 words, 50 unique words, read-aloud) and names the writer-self-image diagnosis directly: 'When we sit down to write, that writer is the one we start channeling.' The exercise's stated value is the proof that the writer can produce energy under the right conditions, the catalogue of what those conditions did to the prose, and the named moves that can be carried back into unconstrained work.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #5
sources.note: Saunders names 'follow the voice' on the worked autobiographical case for 'Jon': a line from a student paper becomes the seed; he riffs on the voice and within a page has 'Privacy Tarps' and 'Gender Areas' and a confined-teenagers facility he had not set out to write. Frames the failure mode the method prevents: 'knowing-in-advance has a tendency to deaden a work and turn it into a lecture or a one-sided performance and drive the reader away.'

sources.chapter: Afterthought #2
sources.note: Saunders names the P/N meter on the forehead and frames revision as 'intuition plus iteration.' The chained-after-drafting position installs iterative-preference-revision as the line-level closing step on whatever the prior steps generated.

sections_html.when: You are a writer whose recent work has been coming back as "interesting" or "ambitious" rather than producing laughter, tears, hunger to read more. You have read enough craft advice to name the deficit. When you sit down at the page, you channel the writer you imagine yourself to be, and that writer's habitual mode is the slack one. Audit-shaped revision skills have not helped because the writer running the audit is the writer who produced the slack. Reach for this when you need the full sequence: diagnose the trap, bypass it in a single session, sustain the surfaced register into a real draft, and revise that draft to a highly organized state.
sections_html.move:
1. Run the energy probe. Apply energy-not-aspiration. Set aside the question of whether your prose is good in the abstract. Ask whether your readers (friends, editors, your own honest re-reads) react with energy or with respect. List the off-license modes your writer-self-image has been keeping out of the real work (humor at awkward moments, a working-class register, a corporate voice, a raunchy impulse). Identify, by recent evidence, which mode actually generates energy — laughter on a kitchen table, a friend reading aloud unbidden, a piece of writing you almost threw out because it was not "real" writing. The output of this step is the named off-license mode and the commitment to follow whatever produces the energy. Without this step you have no way to know which voice the next step's bypass should sustain.
  1. Bypass the writer-self-image with a constraint exercise. Apply escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint. Set a 45-minute timer; write a 200-word piece using only 50 unique words; pre-install a read-aloud constraint. The conscious mind is busy counting words, which lets a less conscious, more playful part of your mind run the prose. The constraint makes elaboration uneconomic and rising action cheap. Read the result aloud and write down two answers in plain prose. What was different about this draft compared to my usual drafts? What does the writer who produced this draft do that the writer I usually channel does not do? The output of this step is a 200-word piece with rising action that your habitual writer-self-image would not have produced, plus a named catalogue of what the surfaced writer does differently. This step depends on step 1 because without the energy probe you cannot tell whether the surfaced register is the one to carry forward or one of several you would have to choose among.
  1. Sustain the surfaced register into a real draft. Apply follow-the-voice. Use the constrained piece (or a fragment of language whose register matched what step 2 surfaced) as the seed. Imitate the voice. Write a paragraph or a page sustaining the voice with almost no other goal in mind. Let the world emerge — vocabulary forces idiom, idiom forces setting, setting forces situation. Apply the escalation engine: keep putting new events in front of the character so the voice has to find new registers in response. Beat back conceptions of "outcome" or "message" as they arrive. The output of this step is a draft of meaningful length whose setting, cast, and situation you can describe but did not plan, and whose voice is recognizably the off-license one step 2 surfaced. This step depends on step 2 because without the bypassed-self-image draft, the writer trying to "follow the voice" is the same writer-self-image step 1 diagnosed; you'd be following an aspirational voice, not the one that produces energy.
  1. Revise per-decision by P/N reaction. Apply iterative-preference-revision. Read the draft top-to-bottom as a first-time reader; watch the meter on your forehead drop to N; wait for the fix to present itself; mark the change; continue. Aim for a single full pass per session, dozens or hundreds of passes over the life of the draft. Apply your editing basis without defense or rationalization. The terminal product is a highly organized system: pronounced causation, precisely selected elements, decisive escalation, everything to purpose. This step depends on step 3 because iterative revision needs prose to react to; without a sustained-voice draft, the per-decision loop has nothing to operate on.
sections_html.signal: You have four artifacts at the end of the chain. (a) A named off-license mode plus an energy reading on it — at least one piece of recent work in that mode produced an energetic response (laughter, tears, eagerness, alarm) that your old mode was not producing. (b) A 200-word constrained piece with rising action where your slack drafts had none, plus a written catalogue of what the constrained-mode writer did differently from the writer you usually channel. (c) A draft of meaningful length whose voice you can trace back to the constraint-surfaced register, with at least one moment when a new event ("the horse") forced the voice to find a register it had not used before. (d) A revised version of (c) in which you can name the four-axis terminal product (causation, selection, escalation, to-purpose) of the iterative method, and you can describe a recent revision session in concrete reaction terms (where the needle dropped, what fix you made, where it dropped next).
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "Why is my work coming back as 'interesting' rather than alive, and what would I do differently?" Note your answer. It will likely be theoretical (a definition of energy, a quote about voice) or fatalistic ("this is just how I write").

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes a procedural account of what you did at each of the four steps, with reference to specific artifacts you produced (the constrained piece, the sustained-voice draft, the revised pass).
sections_html.failure:
The writer who has named the deficit but cannot escape it stays loyal to the writer they imagined being and produces inert work indefinitely. Each individual step has its own failure mode that the chain's ordering prevents.

Skipping step 1 (no energy probe): the constraint exercise in step 2 surfaces some voice, but the writer has no criterion for which voice to keep, and may sustain a voice that is merely novel rather than energetic. The writer reverts to the writer-self-image after the exercise because they did not first commit to following whatever produces the energy.

Skipping step 2 (no constraint bypass): the writer who has identified the off-license mode in step 1 still cannot generate from it because the writer who sits down to draft is the one channeling the writer-self-image. Step 1 supplies the diagnosis but not the route past the trap; step 2 is the route.

Skipping step 3 (no sustained voice): the writer has a 200-word constrained piece and a named catalogue of moves but no draft of meaningful length. The constraint exercise's value is the proof of capability, but a 200-word piece is not yet a story. Without follow-the-voice's escalation engine and world-emergence, the writer has a fragment, not a draft to revise.

Skipping step 4 (no iterative revision): the writer has a sustained-voice draft but the prose is at first-draft strength. The four-axis terminal product (causation, selection, escalation, to-purpose) cannot emerge from a first-draft voice-piece; it emerges from cumulative micro-decisions. Without step 4 the chain produces an interesting draft but not a highly organized system.

Doing the steps out of order: step 4 before step 3 line-edits a 200-word fragment to perfection; step 3 before step 2 follows whatever voice the writer-self-image is channeling; step 2 before step 1 surfaces a voice but loses it after the session because no commitment was made to follow energy over aspiration.
sections_html.example: Saunders does not walk all four steps on a single project in one chapter. He walks step 1 in "The Heart of the Story" (the autobiographical case of abandoning Hemingway-mountain after his wife laughs at the Seussian poems on the kitchen table). He walks step 2 in "Appendix B" (the practice protocol with the read-aloud intensifier and the Doug Unger on-the-spot anecdote as parallel evidence). He walks step 3 in "Afterthought #5" (the worked autobiographical case for "Jon" — a line from a student paper becomes the seed, he riffs on the voice, and within a page has "Privacy Tarps" and "Gender Areas" and the confined-teenagers facility). He walks step 4 in "Afterthought #2" (Stan and the railroad; the P/N meter; lather/rinse/repeat across hundreds of drafts; the apartment-redecoration analogy). The composition of the four into one workflow is implicit in his career arc: the post-Hemingway-mountain Saunders runs steps 2-4 on every project, and the CivilWarLand book is the worked instance of all four steps composed.
sections_html.source: Energy probe: chapter "The Heart of the Story," lines 2064-2130. Constraint bypass: chapter "Appendix B: An Escalation Exercise," lines 7575-7649. Sustained voice: chapter "Afterthought #5," lines 5700-5754. Iterative revision: chapter "Afterthought #2," lines 2183-2267. The four are composed here; Saunders walks each step in its own chapter and frames the chain's intrinsic ordering through the related-skills sections of the atomic skills. The energy-not-aspiration → escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint extends-relationship is the ordering of steps 1 and 2; the energy-not-aspiration "see also follow-the-voice" and follow-the-voice "see also iterative-preference-revision" body links are the ordering of steps 1→3→4.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Composed of energy-not-aspiration, escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint, follow-the-voice, iterative-preference-revision. Each atomic skill stands on its own; this recipe is the workflow that fires them together for the writer-self-image trap.
  • See also forced-cut-pace-discovery (an alternative or complementary constraint exercise; cuts surface what the writer's editing basis offends to, where escalation-by-vocabulary-constraint surfaces what the writer's playful mind produces) and is-it-helping (the disposition with which to receive every craft model in this recipe and elsewhere; the chain is one specific path Saunders proposes, and the "is it helping" criterion governs whether the writer keeps it).

name: workshop-critique-of-a-draft
description: You are about to critique a draft (in a workshop, an editorial pass, or your own revision) and the conversation is drifting toward interpretation, or proposing changes before anyone has agreed on what the story is trying to be, or defending scenes by their local appeal. You need a workflow that converts the room's energy into actionable revision decisions in the right order.

sources.chapter: We Begin
sources.note: Saunders names the strategic-vs-interpretive shift as the disposition his workshop opens with.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders walks the Hollywood-version artillery sequence on 'In the Cart' and tests Chekhov's teahouse scene against both Cornfeld criteria. The two procedures live in the same chapter as a teaching pair: name the working description of the story, then audit a unit against it.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #1
sources.note: Saunders demonstrates the element-level audit on De Sica's *Bicycle Thieves* sequence as the finer-scope audit that runs after a unit has been judged worth keeping at the unit level.

sections_html.when: You are sitting down to critique a draft, your own or someone else's, alone or in a workshop. The room (or your own notes) is about to start producing comments. You can already feel the conversation trending toward interpretation (what the story means, what a scene symbolizes), or toward proposed changes that assume a target nobody has named, or toward defending scenes because they read pleasantly in isolation. Reach for this when you need a workflow that turns the diffuse energy of "responding to a draft" into a stack of decisions a writer could apply to the page on Monday, in the order the decisions need to be made.
sections_html.move:
1. Re-pose interpretive talk as strategic. Apply ask-strategic-not-interpretive to every comment on the table. Replace "what does X mean" with "what is X doing for the reader" or "why do we need X." Direct each strategic question at a specific element on the page, named by location. A comment that cannot be re-posed strategically is a comment the writer cannot act on; mark it so the discussion knows to set it aside or convert it. The output of this step is a list of strategic questions, each pointing at a specific element of the draft.
  1. Establish the Hollywood version before any change is proposed. Apply hollywood-version. Demand a one-sentence summary of the story this draft appears to want to be, narrowed by successive ranging shots until further specification would not change what the story would do next. The Hollywood version is the standard against which every proposed change will be judged. This step must come before step 3 because the Cornfeld test in step 3 depends on a destination for "advances the story." Without the Hollywood version, "advances the story" has no referent and the audit collapses into taste.
  1. Run the Cornfeld test on each structural unit the strategic questions named. Apply cornfeld-principle to every unit (scene, chapter, section, digression) that step 1 surfaced as potentially in question. For each unit, finish two sentences. "This unit is entertaining because ___." "This unit advances the story by ___." Where "the story" is the Hollywood version named in step 2. A unit that fails either test is marked for cutting, replacing, or rewriting. A unit that passes both is provisionally kept but is now eligible for step 4 if it still feels almost-good.
  1. Element-audit any unit that passes Cornfeld but still feels almost-good. Apply take-responsibility-for-every-element at scope. For the suspect unit, inventory the elements present (background figures, weather, side gestures, the contents of a glance, which side of a tree someone walks past) and finish "this is here because ___" for each. Elements that stall on the test are not yet the writer's choice. Decide for each: commit to a function, sharpen the detail, or remove it. This step runs at finer scope than Cornfeld, on units that survived Cornfeld but where the dilution lives at element scope rather than at unit scope.

The chain's order is intrinsic. Step 1 is the disposition that makes the rest of the discussion actionable. Step 2 supplies the destination that makes step 3 testable. Step 3 surfaces the units worth auditing in step 4. Doing them out of order produces specific failures (see Failure mode this prevents).
sections_html.signal: The session ends with three artifacts. (a) A list of strategic questions, each tied to a specific element on the page, every comment on the table convertible to one. (b) A one-sentence Hollywood version that everyone in the room (or you, alone) accepts as the working description of the story this draft wants to be. (c) For each structural unit named in step 1, a Cornfeld verdict (entertaining? advances the story?) and an action (keep, rewrite, replace, cut). For any unit that passed Cornfeld but felt almost-good, a list of its elements paired with what each is doing for the reader, and at least one element marked for cutting, sharpening, or moving. The writer leaves with a stack of revision decisions, in priority order, that they could apply to the manuscript without a translation step.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of the draft on the table: "If I made the changes the room is about to suggest, would I know whether the story is still the same story, and which suggestions to act on first?" Note your answer. The answer is likely "I'd be tinkering" or "the changes pull in different directions."

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer flips to "yes — the Hollywood version tells me which changes serve the story this story wants to be; the strategic questions tell me which elements are in question; the Cornfeld verdicts tell me which units to keep, cut, or rewrite; and the element audit tells me what to do inside the units worth keeping."
sections_html.failure:
Out-of-order failures are specific.

Skipping step 1 (no strategic re-posing): the conversation produces ever more sophisticated readings of what the story means while the writer leaves with nothing to do. Symbolism talk is especially seductive because it sounds like analysis, but it does not tell you whether the second return to the village is earning its place. The story gets understood and stays unrevised.

Skipping step 2 (no Hollywood version): every reader holds a different unspoken target story in their head and proposes changes that would serve their version. The writer, trying to honor everyone, ends up with a draft consistent with no story at all — Saunders's Frankenstein-draft failure.

Skipping step 3 (no Cornfeld test): suggestions are made and accepted by feel, and the writer has no audit for which units survived because they earn their place versus which survived because they read pleasantly in isolation. The dog runs in and out, the ray of sunlight passes through the window, plates break, all in the manner of a real scene, none of it doing the work of a story.

Skipping step 4 when needed: a unit passes Cornfeld but the polish-level dilution remains. The unit feels almost-good, "well-written" in a way the writer cannot improve, because the part they would need to fix is invisible to them.

Doing the steps out of order: step 3 without step 2 produces verdicts against an unspoken destination that may differ across readers. Step 4 without step 3 polishes the elements of scenes that should not survive. Step 2 without step 1 produces a Hollywood version that the room argues over interpretively rather than testing strategically.
sections_html.example: Saunders does not walk all four steps on a single draft in one chapter. He demonstrates step 1 in "We Begin" (the strategic-vs-academic reframing), steps 2 and 3 together in "A Page at a Time" (the Hollywood version of "In the Cart" emerging in the classroom mid-read at Hanov's appearance, then the Cornfeld test on the teahouse scene), and step 4 in "Afterthought #1" (the element-by-element audit of De Sica's Bicycle Thieves sequence). The composition of the four into one workflow is implicit in his teaching practice: by the time he has demonstrated all four, his classroom has been running them as a stack on every draft they discuss.
sections_html.source: Strategic re-posing: chapter "We Begin," lines 188-278. Hollywood version: chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 569-595. Cornfeld principle: chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 964-1008. Element-level audit: chapter "Afterthought #1," lines 1387-1401. The four are composed here; Saunders does not write a single passage that walks all four steps on one draft, but each step is fully worked in its own chapter, and the chain's intrinsic ordering is described in the per-step relationships of the atomic skills.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Composed of ask-strategic-not-interpretive, hollywood-version, cornfeld-principle, take-responsibility-for-every-element. Each atomic skill stands on its own; this recipe is the workflow that fires them together when the scenario is critique-of-a-draft.
  • See also heart-of-the-story (an alternative to step 2 when the story has a known central event the rest must serve; use Hollywood version for the working description, heart-of-the-story for the structural center to audit parts against), truncation-test (a sharper test than Cornfeld at unit scope when the question is whether the unit's structural work survives its removal), and respect-the-reader-cut (the sentence-and-clause-level continuation of step 4, when the dilution lives below element scope).

name: refuse-to-repeat-beats
description: You are revising a section of a draft and the prose feels like it is occupying a settled state for too long. The character has had a real change (gotten the news, fallen in love, lost the job, won the contest) and the next pages are elaborating on that new state instead of moving past it. Sentences are fine, scenes are pleasant, but the section will not move.

sources.chapter: A Pattern Story: Thoughts on 'The Darling'
sources.note: Saunders names the law 'always be escalating' and immediately adds a procedural rule under it: 'refuse to repeat beats. Once a story has moved forward, through some fundamental change in the character's condition, we don't get to enact that change again. And we don't get to stay there elaborating on that state.' He demonstrates the diagnosis on the Sasha section, identifying the current beat ('Olenka has found a new love and taken on his opinions and is finally happy again') and reading ahead to find the next nudge that pushes the story out of it (Sasha's first 'Please leave me alone!'). He repeats the diagnosis at each subsequent stretch ('half a page left,' 'five lines left'), each time finding a new escalation.

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: Saunders applies the same diagnosis to a held-constant trait at its climactic recurrence rather than to a state-change being elaborated. Alyosha's defining trait (cheerful obedience, smile, get back to work) has held across teasing, beating, scolding, and the marriage refusal. At the moment the merchant's wife checks in after the broken engagement, the obvious response would be the same trait-recurrence: 'Looks like I have to,' Alyosha said with a smile, then bounded out of the room. Saunders names this counterfactual explicitly as 'a repeated beat, a failure of escalation: Alyosha continuing to be what he's always been.' What Tolstoy writes instead is the deviation: Alyosha smiles, then bursts into tears. Saunders names the structural function: 'this bursting into tears narrows the path of the story.' The fold extends the skill to held-constant traits at high-stakes recurrences: the diagnosis is the same (the obvious next move would be the trait recurring; the failure mode is repeating the beat), only the beat is the trait's habitual response rather than the elaboration of a recently-changed state. The same procedural test applies: would the obvious next move be the beat we have been in? If yes, deviate; the deviation is what makes the moment land as escalation rather than as repetition.

sections_html.when: You are reading a section of a draft (your own or someone else's) and feel a low-grade restlessness. The character has just had a real shift: a new relationship, a piece of news, a discovery, a loss. The pages after the shift are spending themselves on the new state. The sentences are not bad, but the section is not earning the space it occupies. Reach for this whenever you can name a beat that has already happened and the prose is still inside it.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the current beat in one sentence in the present tense. Saunders's worked example: "Olenka has found a new love (in this case, a little boy) and taken on his opinions and interests and is finally happy again." If you cannot get the beat down to one sentence, the section is doing more than one thing and the diagnosis applies twice.
  2. Locate where you are in the prose, relative to that beat. You are inside the beat for as long as the prose is rendering, restating, or atmospherically extending the new state without changing it.
  3. Read forward, line by line, and watch for the first sentence that nudges the character or the meaning out of the current state. The nudge can be small. Saunders's example: Sasha's "Please leave me alone!" is the first nudge out of the "finally happy again" beat, because it introduces a new thing the story has not been about yet (the effect of Olenka's way of loving on its object).
  4. If you find a nudge: cut what is between the establishment of the current beat and the nudge, or compress it ruthlessly. The section earns its place not by elaborating on the new state but by getting to the next state-change.
  5. If you do not find a nudge: you have located the failure. The prose is sitting in the beat because the writer has not yet produced the next escalation. Generate one. Saunders names the kinds of things that count: a new resistance from a quarter the story has not heard from yet, an inversion of an earlier moment ("rebuff instead of praise"), a private/honest version of a public/mediated earlier moment (Sasha awake says "Please leave me alone"; Sasha asleep dreams of violence), a development that changes how an earlier scene reads.
  6. Apply the rule recursively. After the next escalation, restart the procedure. Saunders demonstrates this three times within the closing of "The Darling": "still half a page left, what's left to do?" then "five lines left, what's left?" — each time naming the current beat and finding the next escalation that justifies the remaining prose.
  7. Stop the story when you cannot find the next escalation. The end of the story is not where the writer ran out of ideas, it is where the next escalation would not add new meaning the story has not already created.
sections_html.signal: You can point to a section of the draft where you cut or compressed prose because it was elaborating on a state-change that had already happened. You can name the current beat in one present-tense sentence at every page-break in the closing third of the story, and you can name what nudges the prose out of each beat. At least one new escalation has been added because the diagnosis surfaced a beat the prose was sitting in without progressing past.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of a stalling section: "What change has just happened, and what change is happening on this page?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the second answer names a new state the prose did not previously reach, and you can name the specific sentence in the section that produces the change.
sections_html.failure: Without this procedure, a writer who has earned a real character change is tempted to dwell. The new state is hard-won and feels worth lingering in. So the prose renders the new equilibrium for a page or two, then renders it again under different lighting, then renders it once more in summary. None of these renderings is wrong. All of them are, in Saunders's framing, the same beat. The section reads as a "long dream" the reader is being told. The story stops being a story (a system of escalation) and becomes a still-life of the recently-changed condition. The reader's restlessness is the felt absence of escalation; the writer hears it as "I should make this scene better" and polishes the prose, which deepens the failure. The cure is not better prose inside the beat, it is the next beat.
sections_html.example: Saunders applies the diagnosis three times in succession to the closing pages of "The Darling." First, he names the current beat after Sasha's introduction: "Olenka has found a new love and taken on his opinions and is finally happy again." He notes the prose is sitting inside this beat and reads forward to find what nudges out: Sasha's "Please leave me alone!" Second, after a small further move (Olenka returns home contented, Sasha tolerates her, the kitten purrs), Saunders again pauses: "Why not end it there? But there's still half a page left." He locates the next escalation in the late-night knock at the gate that recalls Kukin's death and then turns out to be Smirnin home from the club. Third, with five lines left, he asks again: "What's left to do? Can Chekhov find any more escalation?" The final escalation is Sasha's dream, in which the resistance becomes private, honest, and angry, and which (because Olenka does not register it) reveals that her trait will not be moderated by what it costs the people around her. Three escalations in the closing two pages, each diagnosed by the same procedure: name the current beat, locate the prose still inside it, read forward to find or generate what pushes it out.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Pattern Story," lines 3046-3050 ("Always be escalating. That's all a story is, really: a continual system of escalation. A swath of prose earns its place in the story to the extent that it contributes to our sense that the story is (still) escalating"; "refuse to repeat beats. Once a story has moved forward, through some fundamental change in the character's condition, we don't get to enact that change again. And we don't get to stay there elaborating on that state — not, as in this case, for two full pages."), 3052-3058 (naming the current beat on the Sasha section and reading forward to Sasha's "Please leave me alone!" as the first escalation), 3062-3066 ("there's still half a page left," located next escalation in the knock that recalls Kukin's), 3072-3104 ("five lines left," the final escalation in Sasha's dream).
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also parameter-table-for-recurrence. The parameter table is the diagnostic that surfaces which rows vary and which hold flat; this skill is the rule that says when holding flat is failure (the prose is dwelling in a state that has already changed) and when it is decision (a deliberate held-constant trait that breaks at the climactic recurrence).
  • See also skip-the-meaningless-interval (when the reason the prose is sitting in the beat is that real elapsed time is being rendered for its own sake, the leap is the cure rather than the next escalation) and ritual-banality-avoidance (the structural cousin: refuse to deliver the obvious resolution).

name: respect-the-reader-cut
description: You are revising prose and a passage feels belabored even though no individual sentence is wrong. The narration is explaining what the action already shows, naming the emotional reaction the scene already produces, or restating context the reader has already worked out.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #2
sources.note: Saunders gives two worked cuts (the Jane sentence reduced to 'Jane'; the Jim/Sara passage with thirty-seven words removed because the action of dropping the keys already contains them) and frames the underlying principle as 'a story is a frank, intimate conversation between equals' in which the writer continually demonstrates respect for the reader's intelligence.
