sections_html.when: You are revising at the line level. A passage reads acceptably and you cannot point at a wrong word, but it feels heavy. The narrator is naming what the action implies (he was shocked; she was furious; it was unbearable). Or the prose explains a fact the reader has already inferred (their ages, their relationship, what the dropped object means). Or details accumulate that the reader could supply for themselves (the color of the couch, the room someone walked into, the door someone passed through). Reach for this whenever you suspect the writer is doing work the reader would happily do.
sections_html.move:
1. Pick the passage. A paragraph or short stretch of prose where the heaviness lives.
  2. For each sentence and each clause inside each sentence, ask: "If I cut this, would the reader still know it?" The reader is your imagined intelligent equal, not a beginner. Saunders's frame: a smart person of good taste, a person you would not want to bore.
  3. Identify three categories of removable material: - Stated reaction: language that names what an action would already produce in any attentive reader. ("It was shocking that..." after a shock has been rendered.) - Restated context: facts the reader can infer from prior pages or from the action itself. (Two characters' relative ages when the relationship was established earlier.) - Default detail: descriptors and stage directions that do nothing structural. (The color of a couch nobody returns to, a "came into the room" before someone sits.)
  4. Cut. Then re-read the cut version, not the original, and run the same audit again. Cuts often expose further cuts: once "it was shocking" is gone, the buildup that justified it may also be gone, leaving a much shorter and cleaner sentence.
  5. Test what is left. Does the action still carry the reaction? Does the context still arrive in time? Does the surviving detail do work for what comes next? If yes, the cut earned its place. If no, you cut too much; restore the smallest thing that recovers the missing function.
  6. Continue down to a sentence so spare it is at risk of being too thin. Saunders takes "Jane came into the room and sat down on the blue couch" all the way down to "Jane." This is partly a joke and partly a real demonstration: the question "do we even need this sentence" is a real question. If the answer is no, cut the whole sentence and let the next one carry the load.
  7. Trust the leap. Saunders cuts "Jim couldn't believe it" because the action of dropping the keys carries that. He cuts the parenthetical that Jim and Sara are the same age because the reader will assume it. The leaps are not heroic; they are routine, made on the assumption that the reader is on the production end with you.
sections_html.signal: For the passage you audited, you can produce a before-and-after pair where the after is materially shorter, the cuts fall into the three categories above, and the reader's experience of the scene has not lost anything they will register. Roughly: the passage is shorter by a noticeable fraction (Saunders cuts thirty-seven words from his Jim/Sara example, about half the original), and you can name, for at least one cut, the work the surviving prose does that previously was being doubled.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, point at a heavy passage and ask: "Why does this feel slow?" Note your answer. It will likely be vague ("the rhythm is off," "it's overwritten").

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if your answer becomes specific: "It felt slow because the narrator named the reaction that the action already produced (here and here), restated the context (here), and added detail that did nothing for what came next (here). The cut version trusts the reader to supply those, and the scene moves at the speed of action rather than the speed of explanation."
sections_html.failure: Without this audit, prose accumulates explanation. The writer, anxious that the reader will miss the shock, names it. Anxious that the reader has forgotten an earlier fact, restates it. Anxious that the room is unfurnished, decorates it. The reader, sensing the anxiety, drifts; their experience becomes one of being told things rather than being trusted to take them in. Saunders's diagnosis of why this is fatal goes through the relationship: a story is a frank conversation between equals, and the reader keeps reading because she continues to feel respected by the writer. Every sentence the writer keeps that the reader could have supplied is a small withdrawal of respect. Many small withdrawals compound into a piece the reader leaves before the ending.
sections_html.example: Saunders writes "Jane came into the room and sat down on the blue couch," reads it, and starts cutting on the test "do we need this?" "Came into the room" goes (why does she have to enter?). "Down" goes (you cannot sit up on a couch). "Blue" goes (we do not care). "Sat on the couch" — and now, why is it meaningful that Jane sits on a couch? Cut. The sentence becomes "Jane." Saunders calls this a joke, but a serious one: by cutting all the way down, he has preserved the hope of being original; the world of excellence still lies before the writer. Then the second example, less playful: a passage about Jim seeing his ex-wife Sara with a much younger man, in which Jim's reaction is rendered three ways (he could not believe it; it was shocking; it was so shocking that he dropped his keys). Saunders cuts every reaction-naming sentence and trusts the dropped keys to do the work. Thirty-seven words go, about half the passage. He notes the principle: the latter version has more respect for the reader built into it, because the ideas "Jim couldn't believe it" and "it was shocking" are contained in the action of the keys hitting the floor.
sections_html.source: Chapter "Afterthought #2," lines 2291-2303 (the Jane reduction; the question "why did we make those cuts?"; "we made them out of respect for our reader"), 2313-2335 (the Jim/Sara passage and its edited version; the named cuts; "the latter version has more respect for you, the reader, built into it"; "I made the leap of faith that you'd assume Jim and Sara to be about the same age"), 2337-2345 (story as frank intimate conversation between equals; the writer imagines the reader as intelligent and worldly).
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends take-responsibility-for-every-element. The element-level audit asks whether each detail has a stated function; this skill applies that test at sentence and clause scope, with three named categories of removable material (stated reaction, restated context, default detail) that surface the cuts the reader would happily supply.
  • See also forced-cut-pace-discovery (the constraint exercise that builds the felt knowledge of what tighter feels like, so this audit has a calibration to cut toward) and iterative-preference-revision (the per-decision reaction loop that this audit feeds into).

name: ritual-banality-avoidance
description: You are drafting or revising a story and you can feel it heading toward the resolution your reader has already imagined, the satisfying, expected, on-the-nose answer to the question the early pages set up. The story is about to deliver what the reader pre-imagined and stop there.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders names the move 'ritual banality avoidance,' demonstrates it via Chekhov's removal of Hanov, and gives the love-beads metaphor 'push the new bead to the knot' for surrendering reveals early.

sources.chapter: A Pattern Story: Thoughts on 'The Darling'
sources.note: Saunders sharpens the trigger and supplies the optometrist's framing. At the moment the story expects the obvious next thing (Olenka loves the cat, Trot, who has just been introduced as a possible candidate), Chekhov pauses on what Saunders calls an inflection point and asks 'Will it be more meaningful (richer) for Olenka to love the cat or reject the cat? (As the optometrists say: "Is this better? Or this?")' The cat is rejected, and Sasha, a richer second-order option, becomes the next love. Saunders explicitly cross-references the move: 'as in In the Cart, the removal of the first-order option forces the story to supply something better.' The new trigger framing names the moment where the writer has to (gets to) make an authorial decision: the inflection point between the obvious continuation and the higher-ground alternative.

sections_html.when: Your draft has built up a clear question or hope in the reader's mind. The early pages have set it up so well that the reader can already see the resolution coming. The lonely person will meet someone, the test will be passed, the secret will be confessed, the lost item will be found. Reach for this when the story's first-order solution is the one you and the reader can both already see, and you suspect that delivering it will leave the story feeling thin.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the first-order solution. Write down, in one sentence, the resolution the early pages have set the reader up to expect. The crappo version, in Saunders's word.
  2. Notice that the reader has already received the benefit of imagining this resolution. It is in their head whether you write it or not.
  3. Remove the means of producing it. Take the relevant character offstage, foreclose the conversation, ruin the object, miss the train. Do this on the page, visibly enough that the reader registers the loss.
  4. Sit with what is left. The story now owes the reader a resolution that is not the one they were waiting for. This is uncomfortable, and the discomfort is the point: the story has to find a more honest answer, because the easy one is no longer available.
  5. Push subsequent reveals to the knot, in Saunders's phrase. When you know where the story is going, do not hoard the reveal. Make it land now, then ask the harder question: now what?
  6. Trust that the second-order answer, the one you find by foreclosing the first, is the one the story actually wants. If you cannot find any answer at all once the first-order solution is gone, that is information about the draft.
sections_html.signal: The story produces a resolution the reader did not pre-imagine, but that, once it lands, feels inevitable. You can name what was foreclosed and what arrived in its place, and the arrival is more specific than the foreclosure was. The draft no longer feels like the story everyone could see coming.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "What does the reader already think this story is going to do?" Note the answer.

After applying, ask: "What does the reader still think this story is going to do?" The skill landed if the second answer is "I don't know, but I want to find out," and you, as the writer, have a different and more honest answer to give them than the first one.
sections_html.failure: Without this move, your draft delivers what the reader already pre-imagined and the story collapses to the exact arc the early pages telegraphed. The reader's experience becomes confirmation rather than revelation. Saunders is blunt about why this is fatal: a story that resolves in the plane of its original conception is the story the reader has effectively already read, in their head, and the act of reading it on the page becomes redundant. Hoarding the obvious reveal as your big finish is the same mistake at slower speed.
sections_html.example: In "In the Cart," Chekhov spends five pages building up the reader's hope that Hanov, the wealthy landowner who keeps appearing in the lonely schoolteacher's vicinity, might assuage her loneliness. Then he removes Hanov from the story. Saunders tracks the move: the door labeled "Hanov Might Assuage Marya's Loneliness" has not just been shut, it has vanished. The story has no choice now but to ask a deeper question, "What if a lonely person can find no way out of her loneliness?" The actual ending Chekhov produces, a flash of restored childhood memory at a railway crossing, is a resolution the reader could not have pre-imagined but that, once it arrives, feels exactly right. Saunders adds a process metaphor for the discipline: when you know where the story is going, do not hoard the destination, push the bead to the knot and let the next bead find itself.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 822-855. Saunders names "ritual banality avoidance," argues that to refuse the crappo version is to strike a de facto blow for quality, and offers the love-beads image as the practical discipline behind it.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also refuse-to-repeat-beats (the recurrence-and-pacing form: do not dwell after a real change has happened; here, do not deliver the change the reader has already pre-imagined). The two together implement Saunders's escalation discipline at different scopes: ritual-banality-avoidance is structural (refuse the easy resolution), refuse-to-repeat-beats is page-level (refuse to elaborate the new state).
  • See also withhold-the-resolving-interior (when the obvious move at the climax is to enter the head and announce the realization; this skill's structural cousin at the level of the single climactic moment).

name: skaz-narrator
description: You are drafting or revising a story whose narrative voice currently functions as a neutral camera, while the meaning of the story includes what the narrator does not see, gets wrong, or is too pedantic to register. The transparent-omniscient voice is letting the events through but is not itself doing any of the meaning-making work.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders names the technique skaz, citing Vinogradov ('sharply characterized by his substandard speech'), Maguire (the narrator 'has little formal education and little idea of how to develop an argument, let alone talk in an eloquent and persuasive way about his feelings, although he wishes to be considered informed and observant; he tends to ramble and digress and cannot distinguish the trivial from the important'), and Vinokur ('improper narrative emphasis,' 'misplaced assumption'). He demonstrates the technique on Gogol's botched digression about Ivan's 'respectability' (a digression executed with a straight face and a semblance of authority but unable to do what it sets out to do; we come out of it having learned nothing much about Ivan as a person, and what we have learned undercuts the narrator's claim) and frames the principle: 'this isn't graceless writing; this is a great writer writing a graceless writer writing.' The skaz narrator is 'decidedly not Gogol but a creation of Gogol's, another character in the story, a functionary who, through his prose style, unconsciously reveals that he's not as important or smart as he thinks he is.' Saunders also gives the falling-away-of-skaz observation (5398-5402): 'in places, the clumsiness of Gogol's skaz narration suddenly falls away and the prose becomes beautifully articulate,' which means the form has range and is not a uniform voice but a deliberate one the writer can drop into and out of.

sections_html.when: Your story has a narrator who currently behaves like an invisible camera. Events happen, the narrator reports them, the prose is competent, and the meaning lives entirely at the level of what happens to the characters. You suspect there is more meaning available — about the world the narrator inhabits, about the kind of person who would tell this kind of story — and that the transparent narrator is leaving it on the table. Reach for this whenever the narrator's competence is itself making your story too clean, or whenever the events you are reporting are strange enough that a transparent-omniscient voice feels dishonest about who could be telling this.
sections_html.move:
1. Audit your current narrator. Is the voice neutral, omniscient, transparent? If asked who the narrator is, can you only say "the writer"? If yes, the narrator is doing no work as a character.
  2. Compose the narrator as a person. Give them a station, a level of education, an idea of themselves they cannot quite live up to, a literary aspiration they cannot quite execute, a relationship to the events being narrated (above them, beside them, condescending toward them). Saunders's Gogol-narrator: a functionary, "stiff but imprecise literary formality," "pedantic and superior," who "overestimates his intelligence and charm." Make the narrator specific the way you would make any character specific.
  3. Build the voice on three signature failures. The skaz-narrator's prose is recognizable by characteristic misfires: (a) digressions that set out to prove one thing and prove the opposite; (b) misplaced emphasis (the narrator stresses what does not matter and skims past what does); (c) failure to register adequate amazement at the strange events being narrated. Identify which of these your narrator is going to perform, and where in the story.
  4. Write the misfired digression. Saunders's worked example from "The Nose": the narrator pauses to assure us Ivan is "in many ways a respectable man," then proves the opposite by listing his drunkenness, bad clothing, and stinking hands, plus a "cynic"-defining moment that is actually evidence of normal barbering. The digression is "executed with a straight face and a semblance of authority but seems unable to do what it sets out to do." The misfire is the technique.
  5. Write the non-registering reaction. When something extraordinary happens in the story, have the narrator fail to comment on it, skim past it with a phrase like "and so on," or get distracted by the wrong detail. The Gogol-narrator does not step in to reassure us when the dialogue veers into illogic; the absence of comment is the register choice.
  6. Hold the writer above the narrator. Saunders's framing: this is "a great writer writing a graceless writer writing." You, the writer, must be in control of every misfire. The narrator's incompetence is your competence. The test: can you point to a sentence and say what the narrator is failing to do, and why that failure is the right shape for this story?
  7. Allow the voice to drop. Saunders flags Gogol's "But there is nothing enduring in this world..." passage as a moment where "the clumsiness of Gogol's skaz narration suddenly falls away and the prose becomes beautifully articulate." Skaz is not a uniform mode; it is a register the writer drops into and out of for effect. The drops are themselves load-bearing.
sections_html.signal: A reader can describe who the narrator is, in plain language, as if describing a character. Sentences in the prose can be flagged as the narrator's misfires, not the writer's. The strange events of the story now also reveal something about the kind of person who is willing to narrate them in this way. Trust in the narrator declines without trust in the story declining; the reader, sensing the gap, becomes complicit with the writer rather than with the narrator.
sections_html.probe:
Before. Read three paragraphs of your draft and ask: who is this narrator? If your only answer is "an omniscient narrator" or "the writer," the voice is neutral. If you can describe the narrator's social station, education, self-image, and the gap between what they are trying to sound like and what they actually sound like, the voice is already doing work.

After. Same prompt. The narrator has a recognizable personality, a misplaced emphasis, a digression that misfires, and a relationship to the characters the reader can name. The reader trusts the writer more, the narrator less, and the story is denser as a result.
sections_html.failure: The transparent-camera narrator that lets the events through cleanly while leaving the meaning of the world flat. In a story whose events are strange or whose moral claim depends on the system being shown rather than asserted, a neutral narrator wastes the surface. By making the narrator a character who unconsciously reveals the system that produces him, the prose carries a layer of meaning that no event-level rendering can supply. The opposite failure (writing actual gracelessness rather than great-writer-writing-a-graceless-writer-writing) is also prevented: the discipline of holding the writer above the narrator means every misfire is deliberate.
sections_html.example: A story is about an impossible event: a man's nose disappears. The events alone could be told by an omniscient narrator and the story would be a fable. Gogol instead invents a narrator who is himself a minor functionary, full of literary aspiration he cannot quite execute. He pauses to deliver a digression on the nose-finder's "respectability" and proves, via the digression itself, the opposite. He fails to remark on the scale change when the nose becomes a full-sized man in a uniform. He misplaces his emphases throughout (focusing on the rank of the Nose's costume instead of on whether the Nose has a face). The reader sees the events and also sees the kind of person who could narrate these events without being broken by them — a society in which the metaphysical strangeness is metabolized into pedantic ranking. The narrator is the system made audible.
sections_html.source: George Saunders, A Swim in a Pond in the Rain, "The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'," lines 5256-5289, 5398-5402.
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also improper-narrative-emphasis (one of the skaz narrator's signature techniques is deflecting literal questions onto rank, costume, or social bearing; that sentence-level mechanic builds on the skaz voice and is documented separately because it can fire even when the broader narrator-character is not skaz).
  • See also multiple-superimposed-weirdness (the narrator-tier — the highest-leverage tier in a strange-premise story — is what skaz fills; building the narrator as a person is the procedure for ratcheting that tier) and confess-the-oddness (the closing acknowledgment is delivered in the skaz voice, so the confession sits inside the same character the rest of the story has been built around) and belief-by-character-attribution (kept separate because attribution renders sincere belief whereas skaz renders graceless misfire; conflating them collapses both moves).

name: skip-the-meaningless-interval
description: You are drafting a stretch of narrative time during which the world keeps moving but nothing the story is about is happening. Days are passing in the world of the story, characters are doing their daily things, and you feel obligated to render some of it because it happened. The prose flatlines. You sense the story has nothing to do with these pages but cannot bring yourself to skip them.

sources.chapter: A Pattern Story: Thoughts on 'The Darling'
sources.note: Saunders pauses at Chekhov's leap from Kukin's funeral to 'Three months later Olenka was returning from Mass,' contrasts it with a counterfactual filler version ('On Wednesday she saw some pretty clouds. On Thursday it was time to do the washing'), and names the criterion: 'Why not? Because those days don't matter. They aren't meaningful. By whose standard? The story's. The story is telling us, by skipping those days, that nothing meaningful happened during them and that it intends to set us down in front of the next thing it judges meaningful, i.e., relevant to its purpose.' He generalizes the lesson: a short story is not a documentary or a fair-minded accounting of time, it is a radically shaped little machine that thrills with the extremity of its decisiveness.

sections_html.when: You are writing or revising a passage that bridges two events. Something just happened in the story (a death, a decision, a discovery) and something else is going to happen later (a new relationship, a return, a confrontation). The stretch between them, in the world of the story, contains real elapsed time. You are working on prose that occupies that elapsed time and feel the writing going inert. Reach for this whenever you catch yourself rendering days, weeks, or months because they happened in the world rather than because the story has work to do during them.
sections_html.move:
1. Name the event the bridging passage starts after, and name the event it ends at. Saunders's worked example: starts after Kukin's funeral, ends at "Three months later Olenka was returning from Mass" with Vasily about to walk in.
  2. List, in one or two short phrases per item, what the bridging passage is currently doing in the prose. Mourning rituals, household tasks, small observations, seasonal description, minor character notes. Do not soften the list; write it flat.
  3. For each item, ask: is this item delivering meaning the story is built to carry, or is it merely covering elapsed time because the time elapsed? The criterion is the story's standard, not the world's. Saunders is direct: "By whose standard? The story's." A scene where Olenka finds Kukin's spare glasses and weeps may be true to bereavement but not meaningful to a story that is about her trait of becoming-whatever-she-loves; the trait is in stasis during mourning, so the days are not the story's days.
  4. For items that fail the test, cut. Replace the passage with a single time-stamped sentence that lands the reader in the next meaningful moment. Saunders's instance: the entire post-funeral interval reduces to "Three months later Olenka was returning from Mass." The reader registers the elapsed time and arrives where the story has work to do.
  5. For items that pass the test, keep, but on the same justification you would apply to any scene: name what work this item does for the story, not what it shows about the world.
  6. Re-read the bridge. The leap should feel decisive, not negligent. The prose around the leap should signal, by its specificity on either side, that the writer has chosen the leap on purpose. Saunders's framing: a short story is "a radically shaped, even somewhat cartoonish (when held up against the tedious real world) little machine that thrills us with the extremity of its decisiveness." The thrill comes from the decisiveness; bridging filler erodes it.
sections_html.signal: A stretch of in-world time in your draft is now covered by a single time-stamped sentence (or by silence) instead of by paragraphs of small daily detail. You can articulate, in one sentence, what the story was doing during the leaped time (in Saunders's example: "nothing meaningful, by the story's standard"), and you can articulate, in one sentence, what the moment after the leap is doing (introducing the next meaningful event). The cut prose was not bad on its sentence-level merits but was failing the story's-standard test.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of a bridging passage: "What is this passage delivering that the next meaningful event would not deliver on its own?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if you were unable to answer the first question with anything specific to the story's purpose, and the cut left the next meaningful event undamaged or sharper.
sections_html.failure: Without this procedure, a writer who has just delivered a real event feels obligated to honor the world's continuity. The character has just been bereaved, lost their job, won the contest, so the writer renders the days that followed because in life those days did follow. The prose accumulates calendar-tending detail: on Wednesday she did the washing, on Thursday she found a glove, by Friday she felt slightly less heavy. None of this is wrong as observation. All of it is wrong as story, on Saunders's diagnosis, because the story is not a documentary and not a fair-minded accounting of time. The reader feels the bridging passage as inert and starts skimming, and skimming becomes the reading-mode the rest of the story has to overcome. The cure is not to write better filler but to admit the days are not the story's and to leap.
sections_html.example: Saunders pauses at the moment Chekhov has killed Kukin and is about to introduce Vasily. He poses the writer's question: "There are so many possible paths. Which is the best?" Then he reads what Chekhov actually does, which is to skip past the entire post-funeral interval and land the reader at "Three months later Olenka was returning from Mass." Saunders proposes a counterfactual filler version (Olenka does the washing on Thursday, finds Kukin's spare glasses, bursts into tears) and asks why we do not get it. His answer is the criterion the skill operationalizes: those days do not matter by the story's standard, the story signals as much by skipping them, and the leap teaches that the short story's decisiveness about what is and is not meaningful is part of how it generates its effect on the reader.
sections_html.source: Chapter "A Pattern Story," lines 2691-2698 (the question "What now?" after Kukin's death; the bold leap to "Three months later Olenka was returning from Mass"; the counterfactual filler ("On Wednesday she saw some pretty clouds"); the criterion "By whose standard? The story's"; the generalization that a short story is "a radically shaped, even somewhat cartoonish little machine that thrills us with the extremity of its decisiveness").
sections_html.related_skills:
• See also refuse-to-repeat-beats. Both are pacing diagnostics for prose that is occupying space without earning it, but they fire on different conditions. Skip-the-meaningless-interval fires when the stretch is bridging filler between two events (real elapsed time the story has no work to do in); refuse-to-repeat-beats fires when the prose is elaborating a state-change that has already happened (no elapsed time, but the same beat). The first is a leap; the second is the next escalation.
  • See also respect-the-reader-cut (the sentence-level form of the same trust: the reader will register the elapsed time from a single time-stamped sentence without needing the days rendered).

name: take-responsibility-for-every-element
description: You are revising a paragraph, a scene, a shot, or any composed unit of a piece of art, and you suspect it contains elements you put there by default rather than by intent. The unit reads fine, but you have not actually decided what each detail in it is doing.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #1
The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Names the terminal condition for the audit: the artist's final approval comes by letting the work stand. The doorway image — pausing on the way out, seeing nothing to change, and by not darting back in to adjust 'one more thing,' pronouncing the room finished — gives the stopping rule the audit needs.

sections_html.when: You are revising a unit of a piece of art whose composition you control: a paragraph, a scene, a stanza, a shot, a frame. The unit holds together at first glance. You can feel that some details are doing work and others are decorative or accidental. You have not gone through them one at a time. Reach for this whenever "I just wrote it that way" or "it was already there in the draft" is the only justification any detail has.
sections_html.move:
1. Pick the unit. A paragraph, a scene, a shot, a panel, the area inside one frame of attention.
  2. Inventory the elements present in it. Not just the protagonists and the named action, but the supporting details: the weather, the background figures, a small gesture, the contents of a glance, the side of a tree the character walks past, the items the character checks.
  3. For each element, finish the sentence: "This is here because ___." The blank must name an effect on the reader: it primes a turn, deepens an established mood, registers a relationship, foreshadows, contrasts, ironizes.
  4. If the blank stalls on a detail, that detail is not yet your choice. Decide. Either commit to a function for it (and possibly sharpen the detail to serve that function), or remove it.
  5. Do not assume that vivid detail justifies itself. A loving couple in the background of a reconciliation scene is doing work; a loving couple anywhere else may be filler. The test is the named function, not the vividness.
  6. Run the audit until every element in the unit has either a stated function or an explicit "I am keeping this for atmosphere even though it does nothing structural" tag. Atmosphere is permitted, but as a decision, not as a default.
sections_html.signal: For the unit you audited, you can produce a list of every element in it paired with what that element is doing for the reader. The list contains items you would not have noticed before the audit (a tree position, a glance in the background, a checked wallet) and at least one item that, once named, you decide to cut, sharpen, or move because it was not actually doing the work you thought it was.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, point at a detail in the unit and ask: "Why is this exactly here?" Note your answer. If it is "because it just sounded right" or "because that's how I wrote it," you have not taken responsibility for it.

After applying, ask the same question of the same detail and of two others. The skill landed if every answer names a specific effect on the reader, and at least one of those effects connects forward or backward to another moment in the piece.
sections_html.failure: Without this audit, the unit accumulates details by default. Some are doing work, some are filler, and you cannot tell which because you never asked. The reader registers the difference even when you do not, because filler details dilute the ones that are doing work. The piece feels almost-good, "well-written" in a way you cannot improve, because the part you would need to fix is invisible to you. Saunders's diagnosis is direct: an artist is the person who has taken responsibility for every aspect of the work, and a piece feels like art exactly to the degree that this has happened.
sections_html.example: Saunders re-watches a sequence from Vittorio De Sica's Bicycle Thieves with his class. On first viewing the sequence simply moves them. On repeat viewings they start to notice the pieces. The boy and his father, walking sadly along the river, pass on opposite sides of one tree. At the next tree the boy swerves over and they pass on the same side. (Possible reconciliation pending.) A truck full of celebrating soccer fans goes past, happy in a way the father and son are not. The father, noticing his son notice the truck, and feeling the shame of having slapped him, checks his wallet and proposes pizza. As the reconciliation plays out at the table, behind them in the frame is a loving couple looking out at the river. Saunders names the lesson: it is a lesser sequence without any one of these things. De Sica had taken responsibility for every aspect of every frame, and the sequence's first-viewing effect on the reader is the cumulative result.
sections_html.source: Chapter "Afterthought #1," lines 1387-1401. Saunders applies the page-at-a-time exercise to film, walks through the Bicycle Thieves sequence detail by detail, and concludes that what makes a work feel like art is the artist's having taken responsibility for every element in it.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends cornfeld-principle. The Cornfeld test runs at unit scope (does this scene advance the story); this skill runs at element scope inside a unit (does this detail in the scene have a stated function). When a unit passes Cornfeld but feels almost-good, the element-by-element audit is what locates the dilution.
  • See also respect-the-reader-cut (the sentence- and clause-level audit, which removes elements the reader would supply for themselves) and holding-up-against-the-heart (when the suspect element is structurally loaded rather than decorative).

name: the-basic-drill
description: After finishing a story (as reader, writer, critic, or workshop participant), you want to learn from it but feel pulled toward summarizing themes or rendering a verdict instead of doing the diagnostic work that would actually teach you something.

sources.chapter: We Begin
Afterthought #7
sources.note: Saunders names the success criterion of the procedure: 'let's go ahead and ask, diagnostically: What is it, exactly, that fiction does? Well, that's the question we've been asking all along, as we've been watching our minds read these Russian stories. We've been comparing the pre-reading state of our minds to the post-reading state. And that's what fiction does: it causes an incremental change in the state of a mind. That's it. But, you know — it really does it. That change is finite but real. And that's not nothing. It's not everything, but it's not nothing.' The fold installs the 'incremental change in the state of a mind' framing as the basic-drill's diagnostic output and the 'it's not everything, but it's not nothing' stance as its closing humility — when applied honestly, the procedure surfaces real data about how a particular reading mind was changed by a particular story, without licensing grandiose claims about what fiction is for in general.

sources.chapter: We End
sources.note: Saunders supplies an enumerated catalogue of common changes the basic drill produces when applied honestly across many stories over time. Seven typical post-reading effects: 'I am reminded that my mind is not the only mind. I feel an increased confidence in my ability to imagine the experiences of other people and accept these as valid. I feel I exist on a continuum with other people: what is in them is in me and vice versa. My capacity for language is reenergized. My internal language (the language in which I think) gets richer, more specific and adroit. I find myself liking the world more, taking more loving notice of it (this is related to that reenergization of my language). I feel luckier to be here and more aware that someday I won't be. I feel more aware of the things of the world and more interested in them.' The longer-horizon framing names the cumulative effect: 'before I read a story, I'm in a state of knowing, of being fairly sure. My life has led me to a certain place and I'm contentedly resting there. Then, here comes the story, and I am slightly undone, in a good way. Not so sure anymore, of my views, and reminded that my view-maker is always a little bit off: it's limited, it's too easily satisfied, with too little data'; the closing 'Everything remains to be seen. Fiction helps us remember that everything remains to be seen.' The fold installs the catalogue as a teaching aid for what the basic drill produces over time, and the 'view-maker is always a little bit off' / 'everything remains to be seen' framing as the procedure's longest-horizon disposition: cumulative honest application of the drill across many stories produces a sustained skepticism about one's own categorizations of the world. The catalogue is descriptive, not prescriptive — these are the effects Saunders typically registers, not the effects every reader is supposed to register; the procedure's output is whichever changes the reader actually surfaces, and the catalogue gives the reader categories to recognize their own changes by.

sections_html.when: You have just finished reading a story (or a draft, your own or someone else's), and you want to understand how it worked on you. You feel the urge to skip past your felt reaction and go straight to interpretation, theme statements, or a thumbs-up/thumbs-down verdict. Reach for this whenever the next move would otherwise be a literary-vocabulary summary.
sections_html.move:
1. Stop before you interpret. Do not yet ask what the story means or whether it was good.
  2. Turn your mind to the experience you just had. Run the prompts: Was there a place that moved you? Something you resisted or that confused you? A moment you teared up, got annoyed, thought something new? Were you bored or pissed off? Any lingering questions?
  3. For each reaction, point to the specific line, paragraph, or scene where it happened. Anchor every reaction to a location on the page.
  4. Write the reactions down in plain language. Refuse the words "theme," "plot," "character development," "metaphor," "symbol," and any other literary-class vocabulary. If a reaction is "I got bored here," that is the right wording.
  5. Treat every honest reaction as valid data, including boredom, annoyance, and confusion. Do not edit them to sound more sophisticated.
  6. Only after the reactions are listed and located, allow yourself to interpret or evaluate.
sections_html.signal: You can produce a list of at least three reactions, each tied to a specific spot in the text, written in everyday language. The list contains at least one reaction you would not have written down if your goal had been to sound smart (boredom, irritation, confusion, a guilty laugh).
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask yourself: "What did this story do to me, and where on the page did it do it?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. Compare. The skill landed if the second answer is longer and more specific than the first, names places on the page, and includes at least one reaction your first answer skipped or smoothed over.
sections_html.failure: Without the drill, you collapse the story into an interpretation ("it's about loneliness," "the theme is grief," "the character arc is well done") and lose access to the diagnostic data your own reactions provide. The interpretation feels like understanding, but you cannot reverse-engineer the technique from it: you no longer know which sentence made you cry, which paragraph lost you, or which line earned the ending. You have summarized the story instead of studying how it worked, and you walk away with nothing you could apply to a draft.
sections_html.example: Saunders frames the entire book around this drill: he will give you a story, you read it, then turn your mind to the experience. He explicitly invites reactions like "I got bored here" or "this confounded me" as the starting data of the analysis, not as failures to be hidden. His own essays then walk through his felt reactions to each Russian story line by line and only afterward offer technical explanations for why those reactions happened.
sections_html.source: Chapter "We Begin," lines 188-278 (especially 243, 247, 251). Saunders introduces "the basic drill," names the diagnostic question "What did we feel and where did we feel it?", and frames the book as a workbook designed to push the reader past a casual first reading by training the reaction-locating habit.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts page-break-diagnostic. Use the basic drill after a complete reading, when the goal is to register felt reactions across the whole. Use the page-break diagnostic mid-read at chosen pauses, when the goal is to surface the reader's evolving expectations beat by beat. Both anchor reactions to specific places on the page, but the basic drill operates on the finished experience while the page-break diagnostic operates on the experience as it unfolds.
  • See also ask-strategic-not-interpretive — once the basic drill has produced located reactions, the strategic re-posing converts those reactions into questions a writer can act on.

name: track-non-normative-excesses
description: You are reading or revising a story whose surface contains conspicuous oddities, an implausible event, an unusual descriptive density, an awkward structural choice, a tonal swerve, and you are about to either dismiss them as flaws or accept them as flavor. Either move loses the diagnostic signal those oddities are carrying.

sources.chapter: The Heart of the Story: Thoughts on 'The Singers'
sources.note: Saunders names this the 'Things I Couldn't Help Noticing' (TICHN) cart, describes the transactional 'waiting to see' state it puts the reader in, and frames a good story as one that creates a pattern of excesses and then converts them into virtues.

sections_html.when: You are reading a story to learn from it, or revising your own draft. The text contains features that call attention to themselves by deviating from what feels normal, an impossible incident, markedly elevated or vernacular language, a long digression at the wrong moment, repeated description of secondary characters when the main event is waiting. You feel either irritation ("this is a flaw") or aesthetic acceptance ("this is the writer's style"). Reach for this before either reaction settles, because both of them stop the diagnostic clock.
sections_html.move:
1. Keep a running list while you read. Saunders calls it the TICHN cart, Things I Couldn't Help Noticing.
  2. Add an item whenever the text presents something non-normative: a surface oddity (alliteration in three pages, a switch into reverse chronology), a structural feature (eleven pages of digression before the contest starts), a physical implausibility, a tonal break, anything that registers as "presentational excess."
  3. Phrase each item neutrally. Not "this is bad" or "this is striking." Just: "Noticed: long static descriptions of every face in the pub." The cart records features, not verdicts.
  4. Once an item is on the cart, enter the "waiting to see" stance. You are now in a transactional relationship with the writer: you have allowed the excess provisionally, on the implicit promise that the story will pay it off.
  5. As you read on, watch each item. Mark whether the story (a) redeems it by making the excess do work that justifies its presence, (b) leaves it unredeemed, or (c) escalates it (which is usually a sign more redemption is coming).
  6. At the end, do a retro-assessment. Items the story redeemed are now virtues, evidence the writer "meant to do that." Items the story left unredeemed are weaknesses, evidence the writer either did not notice or did not solve them. The pattern of redemptions and non-redemptions is your assessment of the story's craft.
  7. For your own draft, do the audit on yourself. List the excesses your draft is asking the reader to tolerate. For each, name what later move is going to redeem it. If you cannot name one, you have a problem to solve.
sections_html.signal: You can produce a list of every non-normative feature in the story or draft, paired with whether and how the rest of the work redeems it. The list contains items you would have either dismissed as flaws or absorbed as style if you had not been keeping the cart. At least one item on your own draft's list lacks a redeeming later move, and you know which item it is and what kind of move would redeem it.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask: "What in this story is asking the reader to extend goodwill, and what is the story doing to repay that goodwill?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if your answer becomes a specific list of features (not "the style"), each paired with a specific later move that earns it (not "the overall effect"), with the unredeemed items separately marked.
sections_html.failure: Without this audit, you collapse non-normative features into either "flaw" or "style" and lose the data they carry. Reading: you cannot say what the writer's apparent excesses are doing structurally, so you cannot reverse-engineer the technique. The story's "voice" feels mysterious in a way you cannot transfer to your own work. Revising: you preserve excesses that are not redeemed (they are "your style") or remove ones that are doing real work (they "felt off"). The draft drifts. Saunders is direct about the underlying craft fact: a story that approaches its ending with nothing in its TICHN cart is going to have a hard time ending spectacularly, because it had no apparent flaws to convert into virtues at the last moment, which is one of the most powerful things a story can do.
sections_html.example: Saunders opens his class on "The Singers" by collecting his students' first reactions. The students name what is on their TICHN carts: the digressions, the endless physical descriptions, the slowness, the way Turgenev seems to need to tell us everything about everyone. Saunders treats these reactions as the start of the diagnostic, not as complaints to be smoothed over. The rest of his essay then does the redemption audit: for each item the cart accumulated in the first eleven pages, he asks what later work in the story uses it. The descriptions of side characters turn out to be needed because those characters function as a graded panel of judges during the singing contest. The description of the ravine turns out to be needed because the story is structured around binaries that the cleft town cues. The cart's items get redeemed one by one, and the story's apparent excesses become its virtues.
sections_html.source: Chapter "The Heart of the Story," lines 1656-1666. Saunders names the TICHN cart, describes the "transactional" reading stance it produces, and proposes the criterion: a good story is one that, having created a pattern of excesses, notices those excesses and converts them into virtues.
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts confess-the-oddness. Use track-non-normative-excesses while drafting or critiquing, when the question is "is this excess being redeemed?"; the procedure is to log items and watch the rest of the work pay them off. Use confess-the-oddness at the ending of a draft whose middle has accumulated unredeemable excesses, when the question is "how do I close without explaining them away?"; the procedure is to acknowledge them without resolving them. The two share the cart-emptying language Saunders uses across the chapters: TICHN tracks; confession empties the cart by being seen.
  • See also truncation-test (the pop-out variant explicitly logs cut units to the TICHN cart for deferred redemption) and holding-up-against-the-heart (the cart's items are commonly the suspect elements held against the heart).

name: transformation-by-redirected-energy
description: You are drafting a scene in which a character is supposed to change (a stinker becomes generous, a coward acts bravely, a skeptic comes to believe), and the obvious move is to give them a moment of realization, an internal monologue announcing the change, and a clean break from their old self. You sense the scene will read as propaganda but cannot see another way to make the change visible.

sources.chapter: And Yet They Drove On: Thoughts on 'Master and Man'
sources.note: Saunders walks the Vasili transformation. The 'lesser writer' version (line 4583): 'have the bad person do some thinking, and come to a revelation, and start acting resolutely on that thought.' Tolstoy's version: after a half-minute of silent stillness, Vasili 'does not launch into a soliloquy or internal monologue describing his changed feelings... he doesn't announce that he has realized something. The order of operations is not: a change overcomes him, and then he realizes this, tells us about it, then acts. He just acts (or, actually, goes back into action). And he goes back into action just like himself, in the same way he always has.' He acts 'with the same resolution with which he used to strike hands when making a good purchase.' Saunders names the principle in lines 4731-4737: 'moral transformation, when it happens, happens not through the total remaking of the sinner or the replacement of his habitual energy with some pure new energy but by a redirection of his (same old) energy.' The worried-vs-activist example, the bull-and-china-shop redirection, and Saunders's own reassurance-of-the-frightened-kid-on-the-plane experience extend the principle. The post-transformation Vasili remains 'still self-celebratory, still living to feel good about himself, still fundamentally selfish and proud (he is so good at saving people!)' — proof the transformation is redirection, not replacement.

sources.chapter: The Door to the Truth Might Be Strangeness: Thoughts on 'The Nose'
sources.note: Saunders names the contrast condition. Some characters, by virtue of who they are, will not change, and the right ending for that kind of character is the unchanged-protagonist ending. Kovalyov's nose leaves him, then comes back. 'What does Kovalyov learn from this terrible trial? Nothing. What is his hero's journey? A momentous, miraculous thing happened to me, and I stayed the same throughout, although, at times, I did become rather vexed.' He is 'pretending to a rank he hasn't earned' both before the ordeal and after; 'from that time on, Major Kovalyov went strolling about as though nothing had happened. And his nose too, as though nothing had happened, stayed on his face.' Saunders frames the explicit comparison with Vasili: 'Like Vasili, Kovalyov gets a foretaste of death... But unlike Vasili, Kovalyov doesn't take the hint. It never occurs to him that his ways need changing. He just wants to get back to them, as soon as possible.' The medical-test analogy generalizes: 'There are some issues with a medical test. Could this be it? Our life suddenly seems precious, our habits stupid... The results come back: all is well. The mind relaxes into its previous torpor.' The fold adds the contrast condition: when the character is not the kind who would take the hint (a player, an unreflective social climber, someone whose habits are constitutive of who they are), the right ending is the unchanged return rather than the redirected energy. Treating the character as if they would transform when their nature precludes it would betray the character. The diagnosis question for the writer: is the climactic event the kind that this person would register, or the kind they would, given who they are, regard as 'rather vexing' and proceed past?

sections_html.when: You are working on the moment a character is supposed to change. A miser becomes generous; a coward acts bravely; a self-absorbed person notices someone else for the first time. You can feel the obvious version coming: a beat of stillness, a thought "I have been wrong all my life," a clean resolve, a new behavior. You sense the obvious version will read as a lecture or a moralized fable, but no other shape is visible. Reach for this whenever you are about to write a soliloquy of realization that announces a change.
sections_html.move:
1. Refuse the soliloquy. Do not write a sentence of the form "He realized that..." or "She understood, finally, that..." between the old behavior and the new. The internal-monologue announcement is the failure mode this skill exists to prevent.
  2. Place the changed character in an action they were already, by virtue of who they are, capable of taking — but with the action's beneficiary or object reset. The character does not become someone new; they do the thing they have always done with their habitual energy, only directed somewhere it has not previously been directed.
  3. Borrow the character's familiar idiom for action. Saunders's Vasili acts "with the same resolution with which he used to strike hands when making a good purchase." The point is not that he hesitates and then resolves to be different; the point is that he uses the same resolution he used in his old life, and uses it now. If your character is a busybody, let them busybody at the new task. If they are a boaster, let them boast about the new act.
  4. Render only what they do, in concrete factual sentences (matrix-of-facts). Vasili rakes the snow off Nikita, undoes his girdle, opens his coat, pushes Nikita down, lies on top of him, adjusts the skirt of his coat to tent Nikita in. Six observable actions. No interior commentary on what he has realized.
  5. Allow the character to react to their own action with surprise — but render the surprise after the action, not before. Vasili's "tears came to his eyes," his "lower jaw began to quiver," his "strange and solemn tenderness," his murmured "that's our way" all follow the action. He observes himself doing the new thing and is moved by what he sees. The order is: act, then notice, then feel.
  6. Preserve the character's old register post-transformation. The Vasili of the final pages is still self-celebratory, still proud, still talks "with the same boastfulness with which he spoke of his buying and selling." The change is redirection, not replacement; if your character becomes a saint who no longer resembles the person who acted, you have replaced rather than redirected, and the reader will smell the substitution.
sections_html.signal: A reader of the scene cannot point to a sentence in which the character announces or realizes the change. The change is visible only in the action and in the character's surprise at having done it. The character's voice, gestures, and habitual tics survive into the post-transformation pages. You can summarize the transformation as "the same energy turned in a new direction," not "a new person installed in place of the old."
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, mark the sentence in your draft where the change is announced. Read the surrounding paragraph and ask: "What in this paragraph makes the change feel inevitable rather than asserted?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the announcement sentence is gone, the change is now carried by an action whose particulars resemble actions the character has already taken in the story (with a different object), and the character's voice in the next pages still sounds like them.
sections_html.failure: Without this discipline, transformation scenes break the story in two predictable ways. The propaganda failure: the character realizes a doctrine, articulates it in interior monologue, and converts to its program; the reader experiences the prose as a sermon and the character as the writer's mouthpiece. The replacement failure: the character is so radically remade that they no longer resemble the person we have been reading; the reader, who built belief in the old version across many pages, cannot transfer that belief to the new version, and the change reads as a substitution. Both failures share a root: the writer treated transformation as the installation of a new being. Saunders's diagnosis: real-life transformation is not a remaking but a redirection — the worrier becomes an activist by directing the same worry at a worthier object; the bull about to wreck a china shop becomes useful when turned toward a building slated for demolition. The energy is the same, the object is new, and the character we have come to know is still recognizably themselves.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks Vasili's transformation. After Nikita begs forgiveness and announces he is dying, Vasili stands silent and motionless for half a minute. The lesser writer would now write the soliloquy: a realization about master and man, a doctrine of compassion, a resolution to act. Tolstoy writes nothing of the kind. Vasili simply goes back into action, in his familiar mode — the same resolution he used when striking hands on a profitable purchase. Six factual actions: rake the snow, undo the girdle, open the coat, push Nikita down, lie on top of him, adjust the skirt to tent the body. Then, only then, he reacts. "There, and you say you are dying! Lie still and get warm, that's our way..." The phrase is in Vasili's voice — boastful, celebratory, that's-how-we-do-it. Tears come; the jaw quivers; a strange and solemn tenderness arrives. The transformation is complete, and Vasili is still recognizably Vasili: post-transformation he is "still self-celebratory, still living to feel good about himself" — only now what he is feeling good about is having saved a man's life. Same energy, new direction. Saunders's own version, on a plane he believed was about to crash, makes the principle portable: he reassured a frightened kid in the parental-teacher voice he had used a thousand times before, and was brought back to himself by acting like himself, with the energy redirected outward.
sections_html.source: Chapter "And Yet They Drove On," lines 4663-4709 (Tolstoy's transformation of Vasili: refusal of soliloquy, "he just acts (or, actually, goes back into action)", the "same resolution" parallel to commercial dealing, the post-action surprise and tenderness, the "still Vasili, mostly" observation post-transformation) and lines 4731-4737 (Saunders's principle stated: transformation as redirection of habitual energy, the worrier-vs-activist example, the bull-and-china-shop redirection, the relief of the model: we don't have to become an entirely new person to do better).
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts withhold-the-resolving-interior. Both refuse the soliloquy of realization at the climax, but they fire under opposite conditions and produce opposite climaxes. Use transformation-by-redirected-energy when the character is the kind who would change and the prior pages have built toward redirection; the procedure renders external action in their familiar idiom, lets the character react to their own action with surprise, and preserves their old register post-transformation. Use withhold-the-resolving-interior when the prior pages have built two readings of the central question with equal force and the climactic interior would collapse them into a verdict; the procedure is to step out of the head and render only externals, leaving the resolution un-answered. The first ends in changed action; the second ends in irradiated question.
  • See also matrix-of-facts (the climactic action is rendered in concrete factual sentences without interior commentary) and compress-to-force-resolution (the structural decision that creates the time-pressure under which redirected-energy or withheld-interior must operate).

name: truncation-test
description: You are revising your own draft and trying to decide whether the ending is doing its work, or you are studying a published story and trying to isolate what the last section actually supplies that the rest of the story does not. You sense the ending matters but cannot say what it provides on top of what came before.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders proposes the move explicitly, applies it to 'In the Cart' by ending the story one section early, and uses the resulting feeling to derive what the actual ending must accomplish.

sources.chapter: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'
sources.note: Saunders extends the same cut-and-observe procedure to internal structural units rather than to the ending. On the rain-caused digression in 'Gooseberries' (an 82-line block representing nearly a fourth of the story's length), Saunders performs the cut: 'Watch — if we cut those pages, the result reads like this: Ivan Ivanych heaved a slow sigh and lit his pipe before beginning his story. [TWO-PAGE CUT HERE.] We are two brothers, he began, I, Ivan Ivanych, and my brother, Nikolay Ivanych, who is two years my junior. (The seam is invisible.)' The pop-out variant of the test: when an internal unit can be cut without leaving a seam, do not infer the unit is unneeded. The seamlessness is the diagnostic question, not the answer. Saunders's stance: 'For now, let's just log that digression as a structural feature of the story (a quirk, a goiter). It's in our TICHN cart, and when we get to the end, we're going to find ourselves looking over at it, asking, Okay, so what were you for?' The fold adds the pop-out variant: same cut-and-observe procedure, applied to any internal structural unit, with the question reshaped from 'is it story yet?' to 'what was this unit for?' The unit is not cut on its own; it is logged on the TICHN cart for deferred redemption, and the rest of the story is read with the cart in mind to surface what the unit was enabling. The framing 'the story form makes a de facto case for efficiency... all of its parts must be there for a purpose' (line 6035) is the warrant: a seamlessly-popable unit in a published, working story is data — its work is structural rather than plot-level, and the procedure for finding the work is to read forward.

sections_html.when: You are working on the ending of your own draft and unsure whether it is finished, or you are reading a published story whose ending feels inevitable but whose work you cannot articulate. Reach for this whenever you suspect the difference between "narrative" and "story" lives in the last move and you want to make that difference visible. The same procedure also fires on internal structural units (a digression, a setting passage, a frame device) when you suspect a unit is doing structural work but its plot contribution is invisible.
sections_html.move:
1. Pick the cut. Choose a place near the end of the story but before its actual conclusion (the ending variant), or pick an internal structural unit you suspect is doing structural rather than plot work and prepare to cut it from the middle (the pop-out variant).
  2. Read the story up to the cut, or read across the cut, ignoring the popped-out unit. Pretend the cut version is the real one.
  3. Ask the diagnostic question. For the ending variant: "Is it a story yet?" Saunders's variant: "Is it soup yet?" For the pop-out variant: "Is the seam visible?" If the prose reads continuously across the cut, the seam is invisible — and the seamlessness is data, not a verdict. Note the felt answer.
  4. Name what is missing or what was not lost. For the ending variant: what questions are still in the air, what energy has been built up that has nowhere to go, what moment of change has not yet occurred. For the pop-out variant: log the unit on the TICHN cart with the question Saunders gives it, "what were you for?", and read forward looking for the work the unit enabled — characters introduced, complications staged, scenes that exist only because the unit happened.
  5. For the ending variant, read the actual ending and ask what it supplies that the truncation lacked. For the pop-out variant, read the rest of the story and watch for moments that retroactively justify the unit: a counterweight to a thematic position, a swimming scene that complicates a speech, a panel of judges set up before a contest, a maid whose presence asks a question the rest of the story will answer.
  6. The answer to step 5 is what the unit is doing. You can now use that knowledge two ways: as a diagnostic for your own draft (does my ending do this work? does my digression earn its place?) or as a learning observation (this is what the form requires of an ending or an internal unit here).
sections_html.signal: For the story or draft in question, you can finish two sentences. "Truncated at point X, the piece feels ___ because ___ is still missing." "The actual ending supplies ___, which discharges ___ and produces the feeling of completion." The blanks are filled with specific elements (an event, a memory, a recognition, a return), not with vague evaluations.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of the ending: "What is this ending doing that the rest of the story has not already done?" Note your answer. It will likely be vague.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if your answer becomes specific and points at concrete material the ending introduces (a memory, a glance, a detail) and concrete questions or expectations from earlier in the story that the new material settles or transforms.
sections_html.failure: Without this test, you judge endings holistically and cannot revise them. Your ending feels off but you cannot say where the off lives, so you tinker at the level of word choice or pacing while the structural problem (an unanswered question, a built-up energy with nowhere to land, a moment of change that does not arrive) remains untouched. Equally, when learning from another writer's ending, you absorb the satisfaction without identifying its mechanism, and cannot transfer it. Saunders is direct: a story is not a lively narrative that briskly continues then stops; the ending is what makes it story rather than narrative, and you can only see what makes it story by isolating the work the ending does.
sections_html.example:
Saunders truncates "In the Cart" at the end of the river-fording section, two pages before the actual ending, and asks the reader to feel the result. The truncated version: lonely teacher, possible suitor who turns out to be no possibility, near-humiliation at a teahouse, ruined purchases, end. He names the feeling: anecdotal, harsh, a series of bad things happening to a nice lady; not yet a story. Then he reads the actual ending, in which a glimpse of a stranger on a passing train looks like Marya's dead mother and unlocks a flood of vivid childhood memory, and then dissolves. By comparison, he can name what the actual ending supplies: a moment of change, a flickering-back-alive of something long-dead, the discharge of all the accumulated pity into the sudden glow of restored selfhood. That is the move that converts narrative into story.

The pop-out variant is worked on the rain-caused digression in "Gooseberries." The digression sits between Ivan announcing his story and Ivan beginning to tell it, and it covers eighty-two of the story's three hundred forty-two lines. Saunders performs the cut, splicing across the digression: "Ivan Ivanych heaved a slow sigh and lit his pipe before beginning his story. [two-page cut.] We are two brothers, he began, I, Ivan Ivanych, and my brother, Nikolay Ivanych." The seam is invisible. The cut version reads continuously. Saunders does not infer that the digression is unneeded; he logs it as "a quirk, a goiter" and reads forward, asking what the rest of the story is about to do that the digression made possible. The deferred redemption arrives several pages later: the digression is what allows the swimming scene, the maid Pelageya, the host Alyohin, the rainstorm-as-mood, and the friend Burkin's grumpy referee role — all of which complicate what would otherwise be Ivan's unchallenged anti-happiness lecture. The pop-out test located the unit; the read-forward located its work; the writer's understanding shifts from "this digression is bloat" to "this digression is the entire counterweight machinery the story needs not to collapse into polemic."
sections_html.source:
Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 1130-1182 (the truncation experiment on "In the Cart" and the derivation of what an ending must do to convert narrative into story).

Chapter "A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'," lines 6023-6037 (the pop-out variant performed on the rain digression: "Watch — if we cut those pages, the result reads like this... (The seam is invisible.)"; the digression logged as "a structural feature of the story (a quirk, a goiter)" on the TICHN cart with the deferred question "Okay, so what were you for?"; the framing that "the story form makes a de facto case for efficiency... all of its parts must be there for a purpose").
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends cornfeld-principle. The cut-and-observe procedure is a specific diagnostic for the second Cornfeld criterion ("advances the story in a non-trivial way") at scope: cut the ending to surface what it supplies; cut an internal unit to surface what it enables. The pop-out variant explicitly hands logged units to the TICHN cart (track-non-normative-excesses) for deferred redemption.
  • Contrasts two-version-comparison. Use the truncation test when the comparator is created by deletion (cut the unit out and read what the surviving prose lacks). Use two-version comparison when the comparator is composed by you (write the inverted version side by side and read what the actual version supplies). Composition isolates one craft choice; truncation tests whether any work was being done.
  • See also heart-of-the-story (the heart is the destination the truncation test reads back to).

name: two-version-comparison
description: You are looking at a description, a sentence, a setting, or a moment in a story whose effect on you is real but unnameable. You can feel that the choice is doing something, but you cannot articulate what, and "the writer just chose the right word" is not a useful answer if you are trying to learn from it or replicate it.

sources.chapter: A Page at a Time: Thoughts on 'In the Cart'
sources.note: Saunders demonstrates the move twice: once with the worsening road versus a counterfactual flowering road, and once with the rough teahouse versus a counterfactual cheerful teahouse.

sources.chapter: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'
sources.note: Saunders extends the same procedure to a structural unit rather than a sentence-level craft choice. He composes the digression-free counterfactual of 'Gooseberries' explicitly: 'In the bright sunlight, Ivan tells the story of his brother to (just) Burkin as they cross some non-rain-dampened fields. Ivan's final supplication... is addressed to just Burkin. Night falls...' He names what is lost: 'the rainstorm, Alyohin, Pelageya, the swimming, Burkin's reaction to the swimming.' He names the consequence: 'In this digression-free version, Ivan lays out his case against happiness and there's nothing in counterweight. The result is an unchallenged lecture from Ivan with a simple takeaway: Happiness — not as great as you think. A lecture worth hearing, but not the complex experience that is Gooseberries.' The fold extends the comparison to whole sections; the test on a digression or a sub-plot is 'what specific complications does the inverted version lose, and is the loss the story collapsing into lecture or anecdote?' The procedure is the same — write the counterfactual, hold the two side by side, name what the actual version supplies — only the unit being inverted is structural rather than sentence-level. Saunders also names the move's terminal effect (lines 6241-6247): the digression has 'fully justified itself'; what 'we first felt as waste or indirectness... turns out to be exactly what elevates the story out of the plane of its original conception and makes it so complex and mysterious. What at first seemed like a digression is understood to be beautifully efficient.'

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: Saunders adds a fifth comparator-mode at the level of an interior-monologue insertion at a single climactic moment. The actual story has 'He didn't talk much. He just kept asking for water and looked like he was amazed at something. Then something seemed to startle him and he stretched his legs and died.' Saunders composes two didactic alternatives, one for each reading of the story (the cheerful-obedience-rewarded reading and the lived-too-passively reading), each filling in the omitted interior with a sentence of cognition, then literally strikes through the added text: 'That cut stuff is all interior monologue I made up. As you can see, unlike me, Tolstoy declined to go into Alyosha's head.' The strike-through visualization is the move's most compressed teaching aid: write the alternative the actual story refused, line it through, and the surviving prose is what the omission preserves. The procedure is the same as the four prior modes — write the inverted version, hold the two side by side, name what the actual version supplies that the alternative ruins — only the unit being inverted is the omitted interior at a single climactic moment, and the alternative fills in rather than removes. The fold note: when the unit being compared is an omission (a place where the writer chose not to render something), the inverted version fills in what the actual version withheld; the strike-through is the visualization that makes the omission's work visible.

sources.chapter: Afterthought #6
sources.note: Saunders adds a third comparator-mode where the comparator is published rather than composed and the unit is whole works by the same artist at different career points. The exercise: read Tolstoy's 'The Snowstorm' (written soon after Tolstoy was lost in a snowstorm in a sled at age twenty-three) alongside 'Master and Man' (which Tolstoy wrote forty years later from the same material); also watch the boxing sequence in Chaplin's early short The Champion alongside the corresponding sequence in City Lights sixteen years later. 'Let the later work resonate against the earlier one.' The diagnostic target is named explicitly (lines 6433-6443): 'in a highly organized system, the causation is more pronounced and intentional. The elements seem to have been more precisely selected. Things escalate decisively; everything is to purpose.' The 'Snowstorm' fails on each axis (none of the characters stand for anything or do anything that indicates character; they get saved by chance; the story does not seem to be saying anything in particular about human beings, except that, this one time, some of them got lost). The Champion fight is 'saggy and static... lots of jumping around that seems improvised' and the early-Chaplin gags 'keep getting repeated' rather than escalating. The procedure is the same as the two prior modes — hold the two versions side by side, name what the more-organized version does that the less-organized version does not, locate the work the named differences are doing — only the source of the comparator is the artist's own earlier published treatment of the same material. The fold's diagnostic value is anti-mythologizing: the masterpiece stops being an unreproducible gift and becomes the result of identifiable improvements in causation, selection, escalation, and to-purpose-ness across a long apprenticeship.

sections_html.when: You are reading closely, revising, or teaching, and you have arrived at a craft choice that is doing real work but resists analysis. A description that gives a scene a specific tone. An adjective that changes how the rest of the paragraph reads. A weather choice that colors a meeting. Reach for this whenever you find yourself saying "I can feel it but I can't say what it's doing." The same procedure scales up to whole structural units (a digression, a sub-plot, a frame device) when you suspect the unit is doing complicating work but cannot name the complications. It scales up further to whole works when you are studying a masterpiece in order to learn from it: the comparator is the artist's own earlier published treatment of similar material, and the diagnostic target is what got more highly organized between the two attempts.
sections_html.move:
1. Identify the choice. The actual sentence, image, adjective, weather, location, or detail on the page; or, at the structural scale, the digression, sub-plot, framing device, or section you suspect is keeping the story from collapsing into a position paper; or, at whole-work scale, the masterpiece you are trying to demystify.
  2. Obtain the comparator. At sentence-level and section-level, write the inverted version yourself: compose, briefly, an alternate that makes the opposite choice while keeping everything else the same. The road getting better instead of worse. A cheerful teahouse instead of a rough one. A character laughing instead of crying. At structural scale: the same story without the digression, without the sub-plot, without the frame. Stay roughly faithful to the surrounding context. At whole-work scale: locate an earlier work by the same artist that handles similar material — typically a less highly organized treatment the artist later returned to — and use it as the comparator.
  3. Hold the two side by side. Read the actual paragraph (or section, or whole story), then the counterfactual. Notice which feels different and where the difference lands.
