  4. Name what the actual version does that the counterfactual does not. Try to put it in concrete terms: a sense of foreboding, a registering of social distance, a setting up of a later turn. At structural scale: a list of specific complications the inverted version loses (a counterweight character; a mood the storm sets up; a maid whose presence asks a question the story will answer; a swim that contradicts a speech). At whole-work scale: name where the later work has more pronounced causation, more precisely selected elements, more decisive escalation, more everything-to-purpose than the earlier work — the four diagnostic axes of a more highly organized system.
  5. Check that the named effect connects to something else in the story. The actual version, once named, should turn out to be doing work for what comes next: priming a turn, deepening an established mood, placing a character in a position the rest of the story will use. At structural scale: the lost complications should turn out to be exactly what keeps the story from collapsing into lecture or anecdote. At whole-work scale: each named improvement should be specific enough to be a craft instruction you could apply to your own draft.
  6. If the counterfactual is genuinely just as good, the original choice is arbitrary. That is also useful information: arbitrary choices are candidates for cutting or replacing.
sections_html.signal: You can produce a sentence of the form "The actual version does X; the counterfactual does Y; the story uses X for the following purpose at this later moment." The purpose is concrete and points at a specific later moment in the story, not at a general mood.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, ask of the choice: "Why is this version better?" Note your answer. It will likely be something like "it just is" or "it sets the mood."

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the answer becomes specific and connected to the rest of the piece. "It is better because the steadily-worsening road primes the reader for the later moment when the river ruins her purchases; an improving road would have read those same purchases as bounty rather than loss."
sections_html.failure: Without this comparison, craft talk stays at the level of taste. You report that a passage is "well-written" or "evocative" without being able to say what it is evoking and why that evocation matters two pages later. You cannot transfer the move to your own work because you never named it. In revision, you cannot tell which of your own descriptions are doing structural work and which are decorative, so you preserve them all and the draft bloats. At whole-work scale, the failure mode is mythologizing: a masterpiece feels unreproducible because you cannot articulate what it does, and you are left with reverence rather than a model. Locating the same artist's earlier, less organized treatment of similar material breaks the spell and makes the masterpiece's work concrete and learnable.
sections_html.example:
Saunders pauses at Chekhov's description of the road as the cart enters the woods: "the road was growing worse and worse." He composes the counterfactual: a road that widens welcomingly, low-hanging flowers brushing the schoolteacher's cheeks softly as the cart passes a joyful peasant wedding. He holds the two versions up. The worsening road, he names, indicates a steadily degrading situation, primes the reader for foreboding, and connects forward to the moment when the river ruins Marya's purchases. The cheerful counterfactual would have made that same later moment land as random misfortune rather than as the next beat in a darkening sequence. He runs the same exercise on the rough teahouse, contrasting Chekhov's actual description (vitriol, banging door, drunkenness) with a cheerful variant of the same scene to make visible what Chekhov needed the teahouse to be doing in the story.

The structural-scale variant is worked on the rain-caused digression in "Gooseberries." Saunders writes the digression-free version explicitly: in bright sunlight, Ivan tells his brother's story to just Burkin, crossing non-rain-dampened fields, and his closing supplication ("There is no happiness and there should be none... Do good!") is addressed only to Burkin. He lists what is lost when the digression is removed: the rainstorm, the host Alyohin, the maid Pelageya, the swimming scene, Burkin's grumpy reaction to the swimming. He names the consequence: "Ivan lays out his case against happiness and there's nothing in counterweight. The result is an unchallenged lecture from Ivan with a simple takeaway: Happiness — not as great as you think. A lecture worth hearing, but not the complex experience that is Gooseberries." The structural comparison locates exactly what work the digression was doing — supplying a counterweight machinery the story needs to avoid collapsing into polemic — and the procedure is identical to the road and teahouse comparisons, only scaled up.
sections_html.source:
Chapter "A Page at a Time," lines 697-723 (the road) and 944-955 (the teahouse). Saunders performs the comparison live in both cases and uses it to teach how a description does double duty as realistic detail and as a small poem that adjusts the reader's understanding.

Chapter "A Swim in a Pond in the Rain: Thoughts on 'Gooseberries'," lines 6219-6247 (the structural-scale variant: the digression-free counterfactual of "Gooseberries"; the named lost complications; the consequence "an unchallenged lecture from Ivan"; the closing observation that what at first seemed like a digression is understood to be "beautifully efficient" once the comparison surfaces what it was doing).

Chapter "Afterthought #6," lines 6409-6443 (the whole-work-scale variant: the master-vs-lesser exercise pairing Tolstoy's "The Snowstorm" with "Master and Man" forty years later, and Chaplin's boxing sequence in The Champion with the corresponding sequence in City Lights sixteen years later; the four diagnostic axes of a more highly organized system — pronounced causation, precisely selected elements, decisive escalation, everything to purpose — as the test that surfaces what the later work does that the earlier does not).
sections_html.related_skills:
• Contrasts truncation-test. Use two-version comparison when the comparator is composed by you (write the inverted version side by side and read what the actual version supplies). Use the truncation test when the comparator is created by deletion (cut the unit out and read what the surviving prose lacks or what was un-disturbed). Both surface what a unit is doing, but composition lets you isolate one craft choice while truncation lets you test whether any work was being done at all.
  • See also cornfeld-principle (a comparator method that names the unit's contribution) and holding-up-against-the-heart (the imagery-level juxtaposition in which the comparator is the heart of the story rather than a counterfactual).

name: withhold-the-resolving-interior
description: You are at the climactic moment of a draft and the obvious move is to enter the protagonist's head and tell the reader what they realize, what they finally understand, what they feel as the story closes. You sense that one sentence of explicit interiority will collapse the story into the meaning you are advocating for, but no other shape is visible. You can feel your own didactic instinct approaching the page.

sources.chapter: The Wisdom of Omission: Thoughts on 'Alyosha the Pot'
sources.note: Saunders names the central technique on the Alyosha death scene. The story raises an unresolved question about whether Alyosha protests the injustice as he dies; the reader is allowed 'one last glance into Alyosha's mind' (the heart-thought, three translations surveyed) and one external view of the dying ('amazed at something,' 'something seemed to startle him'). Saunders observes: 'Whatever happens inside him in those last seconds, we have to deduce by observation.' He composes two didactic alternatives, one for each reading, and strikes through the added interior monologue: 'That cut stuff is all interior monologue I made up. As you can see, unlike me, Tolstoy declined to go into Alyosha's head. Or, actually, he decided to get out of Alyosha's head.' He names the technical decision: the writer leaves the climactic interior un-rendered. The disposition framing (lines 7149-7167): 'in that swerve we feel him resisting his own didacticism. A form of what we might call artistic reserve kicked in. Like Bartleby, Tolstoy preferred not to. So he didn't.' 'He wrote around that moment when he might have specified what Alyosha was feeling... because — well, because he didn't know. Or didn't know yet. Or didn't like the answer he was about to come up with. That swerve represents a sort of interim decision to not, at that moment, decide — to defer deciding.' The principle (line 7165): 'Omission is sometimes a defect and leads to unclearness. But other times it's a virtue and leads to ambiguity and an increase in narrative tension.' The Rubin's-vase outcome (lines 7117-7141): 'the story, in declining to answer (in obscuring the place where it might have answered), feels like it's not avoiding the question but irradiating it with increased intensity.'

sections_html.when: You are at the climactic moment of a draft. The protagonist is about to die, leave, decide, or otherwise resolve the central question the prior pages have raised. The obvious next sentence enters their interior and tells the reader what they realize, what they understand, what they feel. You can feel two things at once: that you have a position on the question (you want the protagonist's realization to land in a particular direction), and that the prior pages have voted on both sides of the question with enough force that one sentence of explicit interiority will overrule the structure you have built. Reach for this whenever your hand is moving toward "He realized that..." or "She suddenly understood, finally, that..." at the climactic moment.
sections_html.move:
1. Locate the climactic moment. It is the place where the question the story has been raising would, if entered, be answered. In Saunders's instance: the death-scene paragraph in "Alyosha the Pot," at the line that would specify what Alyosha's final amazement is amazement at.
  2. Notice your own didactic instinct. The instinct is the thing that wants to specify the meaning at this moment. It often arrives as a sentence that begins with a verb of cognition ("He saw that...", "He realized...", "He knew, finally, that...") and ends with a position you could state outside the story. Saunders's two attempted alternatives are exactly this shape: one ends in "feeling himself drawn to that eternal reward he had earned, and to which we should all aspire"; the other in "he had lived incorrectly, been too passive. He should have stood up for himself and for Ustinya."
  3. Refuse to write that sentence. The refusal is not a substitution; it is an exit. Get out of the character's head at this moment. Render only externals: a request for water, a stretching of the legs, a held expression, a sound, a small action. Saunders names the procedural form: "Whatever happens inside him in those last seconds, we have to deduce by observation."
  4. Hold the externals to the same factual idiom the story has been using. "He didn't talk much. He just kept asking for water and looked like he was amazed at something. Then something seemed to startle him and he stretched his legs and died." No interior, no judgment, no specification of the amazement. The amazement is named as observable; what it is amazement at is left to the reader.
  5. Trust the prior pages. The omission only works if the prior pages have built both readings of the question with enough force that the un-rendered interior is felt as un-rendered, not as forgotten. If the prior pages vote one way and the climax declines to specify, the reader will read the omission as the writer's reticence about a settled question, not as the question's irreducibility. The prior pages must have set up the Rubin's-vase configuration before this skill can preserve it.
  6. Frame the disposition that produces the decision: you are deferring, not avoiding. Saunders's framing: "an interim decision to not, at that moment, decide." The writer is not refusing to take a position on the question forever; they are refusing to take a position at the moment that would resolve the story's structure into a verdict. The non-decision is the structural decision.
  7. Test the result with a strikethrough alternative. Compose the version that fills in the omitted interior — write the cognition-verb sentence you refused — and strike it through. The strikethrough makes the omission visible. What remains is what the story keeps; what is struck through is what the omission preserves the reader from.
sections_html.signal: A reader cannot point to a sentence in the climactic paragraph that tells them what the protagonist realized, understood, or finally saw. The protagonist's interior at the resolution moment is rendered only by externals (asked for water, stretched legs, expression of amazement) in the same factual idiom the story has been using. You can compose the alternative version that fills in the missing interior, strike it through, and confirm that the surviving prose is what the actual story has on the page. Asked what the story thinks about its central question, a reader cannot quote a deciding sentence; they have to reproduce the un-rendered moment and name the question as the prior pages raised it.
sections_html.probe:
Before applying, point at your climactic paragraph and ask: "If I cut every sentence that names what the protagonist realizes, understands, or feels at this moment, what is left, and is what is left enough?" Note your answer.

After applying, ask the same question. The skill landed if the cut surfaces a paragraph that is structurally complete (the externals carry the moment) and the un-rendered interior is felt as un-rendered rather than as missing — that is, the reader knows the moment is interior-shaped and the writer has chosen not to enter.
sections_html.failure:
Without this discipline, the climactic moment becomes the place where the writer states the story's verdict in the protagonist's head. The reader, who has been holding both readings of the central question across the prior pages, suddenly receives one reading as the protagonist's final understanding, and the structure collapses. The story stops being a structure to help the reader think and becomes a structure that delivers the writer's conclusion. Saunders's diagnosis works in two directions. Toward didacticism: "the story, in declining to answer (in obscuring the place where it might have answered), feels like it's not avoiding the question but irradiating it with increased intensity" — which means the converse, that a story that does answer at the place it might have declined collapses the irradiation into a position. Toward dishonesty: "the most artful and truthful thing is sometimes simply that which allows us to avoid being false: the swerving away, the deletion, the declining to decide, the falling silent, the waiting to see, the knowing when to quit" — which means a writer who specifies the un-known interior is writing past their own knowing, falsifying the moment to satisfy their own didactic instinct. The cure is not to write a more nuanced interior; it is to step out of the character's head at the place that would resolve the question, and let the omission carry the question forward.

A second failure mode the procedure prevents: the false omission. A writer who has noticed the didacticism problem may render an interior that is vague, lyrical, or aphoristic, and call that an omission. The vague interior still votes; the reader registers the haze as one side of the question. The procedural test is whether the climactic paragraph contains any sentence that names a cognition, realization, understanding, or felt position. If it does, the omission has not been performed; the writer has rendered the verdict in soft language.
sections_html.example: Saunders walks Alyosha's death scene. The prior pages have built both readings with equal force: Alyosha as holy fool whose cheerful obedience is rewarded, Alyosha as unconsciously crushed victim whose passivity has cost him love and now life. The story arrives at the climactic paragraph. The reader is given one last glance into Alyosha's mind ("In his heart was the thought that if it's good down here when you do what they tell you and don't hurt anybody, then it'll be good up there too") and then one external view of the dying ("He didn't talk much. He just kept asking for water and looked like he was amazed at something. Then something seemed to startle him and he stretched his legs and died"). What the amazement is amazement at, the story does not say. Saunders composes the two didactic alternatives — one in which Alyosha sees that God loved him and approved of his obedient cheerfulness and feels himself drawn to eternal reward; one in which Alyosha sees that he lived incorrectly, was too passive, should have stood up for himself and Ustinya — and strikes them through. The struck-through text is the interior monologue Tolstoy refused to write. What remains is what the actual story keeps. Saunders names the reason for the swerve: Tolstoy did not know, did not yet know, or did not like the answer he was about to come up with, and a lifetime of artistic practice produced the deferral as an "interim decision to not, at that moment, decide." The Rubin's-vase outcome is what the deferral protects: both readings keep moving forward and receding, and the central question is irradiated rather than answered.
sections_html.source: Chapter "The Wisdom of Omission," lines 6837-6897 (the death-scene paragraph; the heart-thought in three translations as the last interior glance; the externalized "amazed at something" / "something seemed to startle him" closing; "We're allowed one last glance into Alyosha's mind before he dies"); lines 7045-7089 (Saunders's two didactic alternatives, one for each reading, and the strikethrough demonstration of the omitted interior monologue: "That cut stuff is all interior monologue I made up. As you can see, unlike me, Tolstoy declined to go into Alyosha's head. Or, actually, he decided to get out of Alyosha's head"; "Whatever happens inside him in those last seconds, we have to deduce by observation"; "why omit those final thoughts, thoughts that would have told us exactly how to read the story? Well, maybe that's it: he didn't want to tell us exactly how to read the story"); lines 7117-7141 (the Rubin's-vase framing of the two coexistent readings; "the wonder of the story is that it fails to answer that question; or, rather, that it answers — it succeeds in answering — in favor of both views, simultaneously"; "the story, in declining to answer (in obscuring the place where it might have answered), feels like it's not avoiding the question but irradiating it with increased intensity"); lines 7149-7167 (the disposition that produces the decision: "in that swerve we feel him resisting his own didacticism. A form of what we might call artistic reserve kicked in. Like Bartleby, Tolstoy preferred not to. So he didn't"; "He wrote around that moment when he might have specified what Alyosha was feeling... because — well, because he didn't know. Or didn't know yet. Or didn't like the answer he was about to come up with. That swerve represents a sort of interim decision to not, at that moment, decide — to defer deciding"; "The most artful and truthful thing is sometimes simply that which allows us to avoid being false"; "Omission is sometimes a defect and leads to unclearness. But other times it's a virtue and leads to ambiguity and an increase in narrative tension").
sections_html.related_skills:
• Extends persistent-self-contradiction. The macro-structural method builds two readings of the central question across the prior pages with equal force; this skill is the single-moment technical decision at the resolution point that preserves the un-rankability the macro-method has set up. They commonly fire together: persistent-self-contradiction across the body, withhold-the-resolving-interior at the climax. The Rubin's-vase outcome the macro-method aims at is what this skill protects.
  • Contrasts transformation-by-redirected-energy. Both refuse the soliloquy of realization, but under opposite conditions. Use withhold-the-resolving-interior when the prior pages have built two readings with equal force and entering the head would collapse them into a verdict; the procedure renders only externals and leaves the resolution un-answered. Use transformation-by-redirected-energy when the prior pages have built toward a real change and the character is the kind who would take the hint; the procedure renders the changed action in the character's familiar idiom and lets the character react to their own action with surprise. The first ends in irradiated question; the second ends in changed action.
  • See also compress-to-force-resolution (the structural device that creates the time-pressure under which the omission or the redirected action discharges).
